Regulatory drivers: HARs are the fastest-evolving conserved non-coding elements, many act as neurodevelopmental enhancers (e.g. HARE5 enhancer of FRIZZLED8 accelerates progenitor cell cycle and enlarges mouse brain); a hominin-specific SOX5-binding-site deletion in a CBLN2 enhancer drives human-specific prefrontal CBLN2 upregulation and spine formation. Caveat: even the strongest human adaptive-trait cases have largely relied on correlative evidence, now beginning to shift toward functional validation.

Connections

brain-development-neurogenomics — this Perspective is a foundational conceptual anchor for the topic: it organizes brain evolution by developmental stage and supplies the bradychrony/tachychrony vocabulary.
Pairs with single-cell atlases of the developing human brain as the conceptual/evolutionary framing for their cell-type findings — e.g. the protein-level companion wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (which independently flags the intermediate-progenitor→neuron transition as disease-vulnerable, consistent with this paper’s emphasis on conserved-cell-type shifts and neurodevelopmental disease susceptibility).
Touches developmental gene-regulation themes shared with the developing-brain expression/isoform siblings walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.

Open questions

What are the global vs stage-specific molecular clocks setting tempo, and how much of human bradychrony follows allometric scaling vs is slower than expected for our size?
Are human-specific maturation features (extended axons, dendritic arborization, spine number) shared with other great apes or truly human-specific, given the ethical/technical limits on great-ape fetal tissue?
How do structural variants and 3D-genome reorganization (enhancer hijacking near HARs) causally rewire neurodevelopmental gene regulation in their endogenous context, beyond reporter-assay evidence?
Do cerebellar and thalamic (non-cortical) cell types diversify in human evolution, and how does neuronal activity feed back to shape these protracted developmental processes?

wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.title: liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral
wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.content:
Single-cell RNA sequencing of peripheral blood links cell-type-specific regulation of splicing to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases
One-line: A population-scale single-cell splicing study of ~1 million PBMCs from 474 healthy Eastern/Southeastern/South Asian donors (the aida cohort) that maps cell-type-specific, sex-biased and ancestry-biased alternative splicing plus 11,577 cis-sQTLs, and links these to autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability — validating an Asian-specific TCHP sQTL for Graves’ disease.
Key takeaways

Uses 10x Genomics 5′ scRNA-seq plus the endogenous “exon painting” phenomenon (stochastic mRNA cleavage and recapping creating multiple 5′ ends per isoform) to gain exon coverage that standard 3′-biased droplet scRNA-seq lacks, enabling splicing-qtl mapping at single-cell resolution across 21 PBMC subtypes from 474 donors.
Two-level alternative splicing (see alternative-splicing) quantification: SpliZ at the single-cell level (median 1,146 AS genes/cell, 4.3× the OneK1K estimate of 267) and leafcutter at the pseudobulk level (median 7,721 AS genes/pseudobulk, saturating at ~11,500 genes; R²=0.92 sigmoid fit to cell count).
Identifies 11,577 independent cis-sQTLs (10,874 protein-coding + 703 lncRNA sGenes, FDR<0.05; ~39.9% of tested genes), 607 trans-sGenes (FDR<0.01) and 107 dynamic sQTLs along B cell differentiation; most differential splicing and many sQTLs are cell-type-specific.
A cell-type-specific trans-regulatory relationship: a cis-eQTL for HNRNPLL (a master regulator of T cell activation-induced splicing) colocalizes with a trans-sQTL for PTPRC/CD45 (rs6751481, H4≈1.00 in naive CD4+ T), with higher HNRNPLL expression driving shorter PTPRC isoforms — recapitulating the CD45RA→CD45RO isoform switch in T cell development.
cis-sQTL effects are enriched in autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability (S-LDSC: SLE, atopic dermatitis, Graves’ disease, RA highest); colocalization with 20 Asian-focused GWAS traits nominates 563 putative effector genes (see splicing-and-disease).
Functional validation: an Asian-specific sQTL (rs74416240) disrupting the 5′ splice site of TCHP exon 4 colocalizes with Graves’ disease GWAS; a K562 minigene assay confirmed the alternative (A) allele induces intron retention while the reference (G) allele yields the normal isoform.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: AIDA Data Freeze v.1 — 474 donors (Japan, Korea, Singapore: Singaporean Chinese/Malay/Indian), avg 1,959 cells/donor, avg 53,846 reads/cell of which 46.9% overlapped a splice junction; median 85.3% of exonic bases covered per gene (IQR 51.8–97.3%).
QC/replication: 59.3% of pseudobulk introns canonical in GENCODE v.32; 85.9% of short-read junctions replicated in PacBio MAS-seq long-read data (4 donors); ~93%/98.8%/99.5% of junctions seen in >1,000/>100/>10 Snaptron samples. cis-sQTLs replicated at π1 ≈ 0.70–1 across BLUEPRINT, DICE, GTEx whole-blood/LCL and ImmuNexUT, with ~14.3% new vs ImmuNexUT (largest existing PBMC sQTL set).
Sex/ancestry splicing: 48 sex-biased differential splicing events (DSEs) across 32 genes (e.g. female-biased FLNA isoform ENST00000498491 in T/NK cells); 1,031 ancestry-biased DSEs across 509 genes. SNP rs11064437 disrupts the 3′ splice site of SPSB2 exon 2 (AG|C→AA|C) with MAF declining EAS 30.7% → SEA 13.9% → SAS 3.4%; the EAS-biased alt intron was absent from GENCODE.
sQTL context-dependence: 27 sex-biased sQTLs across 20 genes; 8 Malay-biased and 19 Indian-biased cis-sQTLs; allelic heterogeneity up to 16.1% of sGenes with ≥2 independent variants. Sample size (r=0.95) and library size (r=0.96) independently drive sQTL discovery.
Dynamic sQTLs across B-cell pseudotime (naive → IGHMhi memory → IGHMlo memory, 52,964 cells): 865 dynamic introns (3 modes: stepwise/linear/quadratic; e.g. PAX5A↓/PAX5B↑, PTPRC exon 4↓, DOCK8 exon 1 transient) and 107 dynamic sQTLs (62 strengthening, 37 weakening, 11 transient); CD83 dynamic sQTL colocalizes with RA (H4=0.980), BCL2A1 with lymphocyte count (H4=0.848).
trans-sQTLs are more cell-type-specific than cis: 393/607 (64.7%) appear in only one cell type; 17 cis-eQTL↔trans-sQTL colocalization events (H4>0.75). A lead-SNP switch between naive CD4+ T (rs6751481, promoter) and CD4+ TEM (rs74258942, enhancer; Asian-biased) confirmed by SuSiE fine-mapping; SMR links HNRNPLL expression to memory CD4+ T proportion (P=9.21×10⁻⁷, HEIDI P=0.103).
TCHP/Graves’: lead GWAS risk variant rs74416240 has an East-Asian-biased minor allele (AIDA + 1000 Genomes), sits in the TCHP exon 4–5 5′ splice site, and shows cell-type-specific GD-GWAS/sQTL colocalization across seven cell types.

Connections

A single-cell, immune-cell, population-genetics application built on existing splicing-quantification tooling — pseudobulk via leafcutter (cf. li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna) rather than a new algorithm; the disease-genetics angle parallels zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain-region sQTLs) but resolves cell types instead of tissue regions and adds ancestry diversity.
Extends splicing-qtl and splicing-and-disease into single-cell, multi-ancestry territory; complements the population-splicing line of park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative and disease-splicing reviews (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer).
Mechanistically centers RNA-binding-protein trans-regulation (HNRNPLL→PTPRC) relevant to rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors; splice-site-disrupting variants (SPSB2, TCHP) connect to splice-sites.
Introduces the aida atlas as a reusable multi-ancestry immune single-cell + sQTL resource.

Open questions

Does the East-Asian-biased TCHP exon 4 sQTL causally raise Graves’ disease risk in vivo, and through what TCHP isoform function?
How much of the disease heritability enrichment attributable to sQTLs is orthogonal to eQTLs in these same cell types, and which effector genes among the 563 are druggable?
Can full-length single-cell technologies eventually replace the “exon painting” trick, and would they recover isoforms missed by 5′ coverage?
How well do sQTLs and ancestry-biased splicing events discovered in Asian donors transfer to (or differ from) European-ancestry cohorts like OneK1K?

wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.title: loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor
wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.content:
Multiomic profiling of transcription factor binding and function in human brain
One-line: A large multiomic ChIP–seq resource (braintf) mapping occupancy of >100 transcription factors directly in human postmortem brain tissue and FANS-sorted neuronal/glial nuclei, integrated with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq and DNA methylation to model TF function and to link specific TFs (notably TBR1, PKNOX1) to neuropsychiatric disease risk.
Key takeaways

First and largest direct measurement of TF binding in human postmortem brain: ChIP–seq for 106 total TFs (96 in bulk, 76 in sorted nuclei) plus histone marks across nine brain regions from four neurotypical donors, paired with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, phased WGS and DNA methylation — packaged as the braintf resource.
FANS sorting (NeuN+ neurons, Olig2+ oligodendrocytes, NeuN−/Olig2− microglia+astrocytes) de-confounds cell-type signal versus bulk homogenate; for nearly all TFs profiled, no prior brain binding data existed in ENCODE.
Identifies high-occupancy target (HOT) sites — loci bound by many TFs — that are promoter/CpG-island biased and largely cell-type-shared; they carry little TF-specific information and bias both motif discovery and computational TF-binding predictors. Removing them sharpens motif recognition for low-GC factors.
Neuronal TFs SATB2 and TBR1 bind unique, HOT-depleted cis-regulatory-elements and promote neuronal gene expression; 31% of TF-bound regions have no ENCODE cCRE annotation and are enriched for neuron-specific regulatory elements/pathways.
Stratified LD score regression links TF binding sites — especially in NeuN+ neurons (e.g. PKNOX1, TBR1, BCL11A) — to heritability for neuropsychiatric disorders (schizophrenia, bipolar), more strongly than histone marks or ATAC-seq; Alzheimer’s risk localizes to NeuN−/Olig2− (e.g. REST).

Notable claims / data

Union of TF peaks across the four large regions: 239,361 distinct regions spanning 6.94% of hg38 (text also cites 236,311 in Fig. 1); ~49% distal enhancer-like (dELS), 8% promoter-like (PLS), 31% unannotated (“none”). Promoters shared across more cell types than enhancers (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶).
“None” regions enriched for neuron-specific elements: odds ratio 2.9 for overlap with NeuN+ vs bulk ATAC-seq (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) vs 1.1 (Olig2+) and 1.5 (NeuN−/Olig2−).
TF co-association: PCA on ChIP signal (first 20 PCs = 83.76% variance) yields three clusters — Cluster 1 low promoter-preference/high signal, Cluster 3 promoter-biased (H3K4me3-overlapping), Cluster 2 intermediate (TBR1–SATB2 node). Recovers known interactions (CTCF–RAD21 cohesin, EHMT2–REST, NFIB–NFIC).
HOT sites = top 10% of merged loci by number of bound TFs; 66% are promoter-like CpG-island sequences (OR = 3.81, P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶); shared HOT sites enriched for housekeeping-gene promoters (OR = 8.245). SATB2 binds few-TF regions; HCFC1 binds predominantly HOT sites.
Motifs: from JASPAR 2022, peak motif-match proportion ranged 0.8–86.5%; of 49 factors, 24 were most enriched for their expected motif. Common motifs (e.g. THAP11, NRF/SP families) are GC-rich; removing HOT sites improved enrichment for low-GC motifs (e.g. NFIB motif rose to top-ranked MEME call).
Predictive models (Virtual ChIP–seq, 16 overlapping TFs): bulk-DLPFC auPRs correlated with GM12878 cell-line auPRs (r = 0.69, P = 0.0093); CTCF/NRF1 well predicted, EGR1/REST poorly. auPRs drop sharply when HOT sites removed — models are biased toward easily-predicted promoter/HOT sites and miss neuron-enriched “none” CREs.
Gene-expression modeling (NeuN+ linear models controlling for cobound TF count): most TFs positively affect expression, with largest effect at non-HOT promoters; at HOT promoters individual TFs have minimal effect. TAF1 most enriched for upregulated genes; EHMT2/SIRT1 repressive; TBR1/ZBTB7B dual activator/repressor.
Cell-type-biased TFs: SATB2/TBR1/BCL11A (neurons), OLIG2/SOX8 (oligodendrocytes), MITF (microglia/astrocytes). Example: a NeuN+ PKNOX1 peak overlaps a schizophrenia risk locus at the 3′ end of TSNARE1, linked to neuronal ADGRB1 and ARC expression.
Caveats: only four donors; ChIP–seq quality/skewness covaries with peak count and signal-to-noise; HOT sites should NOT be universally excluded (unlike ENCODE excluded-regions list) since they capture real GC-rich TF behavior; target-gene/distal links rely on a prior single-cell multiomic study (Anderson et al. 2023).

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics / regulatory resource: complements zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at — all interpret noncoding cis-regulatory-elements, but this one supplies direct TF occupancy maps (ChIP–seq) rather than MPRA-measured activity.
Sibling human-brain regulatory/psychiatric-genetics source to li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (PsychENCODE) and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory; uses cell-type FANS sorting like the sorted-nuclei strategy in kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell.
Conceptually adjacent to TF/RBP binding-map efforts such as encode-rbp-map (van Nostrand RBPs) — both build occupancy maps to assign regulatory function, but BrainTF targets DNA-binding TFs in brain tissue rather than RNA-binding proteins.
Relevant to the developing/diseased-brain hub brain-development-neurogenomics via neuronal TFs (SATB2, TBR1, BCL11A, NEUROD1) with established roles in cortical neuron development.

Open questions

Can the BrainTF occupancy maps improve in silico TF-binding predictors for the neuron-enriched, unannotated (“none”) CRE category that current models miss?
Do TF–disease enrichments replicate with more donors and additional disease GWAS, and can dual-role TFs (TBR1, ZBTB7B) be resolved into activator vs repressor contexts?
How do these bulk/sorted-nuclei TF maps relate to single-cell-resolution regulatory states, and to allele-specific binding from the phased WGS generated here?

wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.title: marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative
wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.content:
The physiology of alternative splicing
One-line: A review arguing that most of the transcriptomic complexity generated by alternative-splicing in multicellular eukaryotes is reflected at the protein level and shapes diverse physiological and pathological processes, from tissue differentiation to thermal regulation, cancer and approved splicing-correcting therapies.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing occurs in ~95% of human genes and is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular organisms; the central question is how much is functional versus noise. The authors argue most is functional, citing four decades of single-gene studies.
The apparent contradiction between sensitive RNA-seq and low-sensitivity mass spectrometry (which suggested ~85% of alternative exons are irrelevant) is attributed to technical limits of splicing proteomics (“absence of evidence is not evidence of absence”); ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged.
cotranscriptional-splicing: splice-site choice depends on cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements) AND trans splicing-factors AND coupling to Pol II elongation. Kinetic coupling model — slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons. splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks (H3K9ac, H3K9me2) modulate elongation and thus splicing outcomes.
Global alternative-splicing changes drive cell proliferation, cancer (splicing-and-disease), ASD and thermal regulation; gene-specific switches drive neuron self-avoidance, infrared sensing, telomere maintenance and the Warburg effect.
splicing-therapeutics: mastering splicing control yielded clinically approved ASO/small-molecule drugs for SMA (nusinersen, risdiplam) and DMD (eteplirsen).
splicing-and-evolution: alternative splicing per se has adaptive value as a readily available pool of mRNA variability that can be co-opted without eliminating gene functions or making new genes; it prevails in K-selected multicellular eukaryotes.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila Dscam can generate up to 38,000 mRNA variants (19,000 distinct extracellular domains) — more than the ~15,000 genes in the fly genome — via mutually exclusive exons in 4 regions; basis of stochastic neurite self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion. Vertebrates achieve self-avoidance differently, via clustered protocadherin promoters (~60 variants), no splicing.
CLK1 is a body-temperature sensor: activity is >5-fold higher at 35 °C than 38 °C; its 25-aa activation domain becomes flexible at high temperature (domain-swap with temperature-insensitive SRPKs confirms the activation domain is the thermosensor). Drives global SR-protein phosphorylation and splicing. Conserved in poikilotherms (alligators, turtles, Drosophila); links to fly circadian tim siesta behavior.
Warburg effect: PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons (each 56 aa) → PKM1 (constitutive tetramer) vs PKM2 (low-activity, favors anabolic intermediates, cancer/embryonic). Regulated by hnRNPA1, hnRNPA2, PTBP1, whose genes are activated by MYC.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) infrared sensing: trigeminal-ganglion-specific TRPV1 splice variant (E14a, truncated C-terminus) lowers heat threshold from 43 °C to 30 °C — “sees” prey while retaining noxious-heat protection.
Telomeres: hTERT E2 skipping (vs SON-promoted inclusion) is a developmental switch controlling telomerase; an individual with a SON mutation had shorter telomeres.
ASD: neural microexons (3–27 nt, usually multiples of 3) are highly conserved, promoted by SRRM4 (reduced in ASD); examples in eIF4G1/eIF4G3 and CPEB4 (microexon 4). Gut-microbiota transplant from ASD donors into germ-free mice altered >500 splicing events (52 autism-related) with few gene-expression changes.
Cancer: SRSF1 (SF2/ASF) is a proto-oncogene; SRSF2/U2AF1/SF3B1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes; PRMT5 methylates SRSF1 (AML target) and removes detained introns.
SMA: SMN1/SMN2 differ in 11 nt (key C>T in SMN2 E7 causes skipping). Nusinersen (FDA 2016/EMA 2017) blocks intron-7 ISS bound by hnRNPA1/A2 → E7 inclusion; first splicing-corrective drug. Marasco et al. found SMN2 E7 is a class II exon, and the ASO paradoxically induces H3K9me2 — HDAC inhibitors (VPA) synergize by relieving the elongation roadblock. Risdiplam = small molecule affecting snRNA–5′SS pairing.
DMD: E49–E50 deletions = 13% of cases; eteplirsen (30-mer ASO) targets an ESE in E51 to force E51 skipping, restoring frame (Becker-like internally truncated dystrophin).
Textbook example CALCA: one gene → calcitonin (thyroid C cells, E4 skipped) vs CGRP1 (neurons, E4 included) via alternative splicing + cleavage/polyadenylation + cell-specific proteolysis. FOXP1 mutually exclusive E18/E18b switches between differentiation and pluripotency (FOXP1-ES), upstream-controlled by MBNL1/MBNL2.

Connections

Vertebrate organ transcriptomes: alternative-splicing profiles track species identity more than organ type (primates highest complexity); PTBP1 isoform reshapes splicing across species — links to splicing-and-evolution.
Coupling of alternative-splicing to NMD: ~15,000 human variants annotated as NMD targets (Ensembl 2019); post-transcriptional-splicing control of protein output.
Tissue/organ-specific: RBM20/titin in dilated cardiomyopathy; endothelial flow; obesity; pancreatic circadian exocytosis — rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors as drivers of homeostasis and splicing-and-disease.
G9a (EHMT2) E10 inclusion (Kornblihtt group) creates an H3K9me2-driven positive feedback loop reinforcing neuronal differentiation — direct tie to splicing-and-chromatin.

Open questions

How much RNA-seq-detected isoform diversity is truly functional vs too low-abundance to matter? Authors suggest long-read (third-generation) sequencing to resolve the transcriptomic/proteomic discrepancy.
Why do mutations in constitutive splicing-factors (U2AF1, SF3B1) preferentially perturb specific alternative events rather than global splicing? (Partly redundancy; partly late-recruited regulatory roles of core spliceosome components.)
Molecular mechanism of the bat TRPV1 dorsal-root vs trigeminal splicing switch remains unknown.
Whether ASD gut-microbiota metabolites reverse brain splicing changes, and whether HDAC inhibitors also boost risdiplam efficacy, remain to be tested.

wiki/sources/masland-2004-neuronal-cell-.title: masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types
wiki/sources/masland-2004-neuronal-cell-.content:
Neuronal cell types
One-line: A Current Biology Primer arguing that a complete inventory of functionally distinct neuronal types — the brain’s “parts list” — is foundational to understanding brain function, and surveying how cell types are distinguished across the cerebellum, neocortex, and retina.
Key takeaways

Distinct neuronal cell types are the brain’s elementary computational components; assembling a “parts list” of cell types is a prerequisite for any bottom-up understanding of brain function. This connects to the broader brain-development-neurogenomics program of cataloguing neural cell diversity.
Cell types are defined by the premise that different structure indicates different function (“structure” including both morphology and expression of functionally important proteins). The most common discriminator is cell shape, because shape is a direct reflection of a neuron’s synaptic connections — the level of dendritic/axonal stratification defines which cells can contact each other.
A core taxonomic distinction is projection neurons (axon leaves the structure; “principal cells”) vs intrinsic neurons (synapse only locally; “interneurons”). The distinction applies only to axonal projection, not to the source of inputs.
Nomenclature is unstandardized and inconsistent: terms like “class,” “type,” “subclass,” “subtype” are used loosely; “type” is reserved here for the terminally differentiated level. Like genes, cells acquire whimsical and multiple names.
The retina is now known with unprecedented precision and serves as the proof-of-concept that a complete CNS cell-type inventory is achievable; the neocortex remains “a much harder nut to crack.”
Genome-screening probes will increasingly help sort cells, but truly cell-type-specific gene expression is rare; the practical future method combines partial molecular specificity + morphology (an arborization marker), which is more robust than morphology or expression alone.

Notable claims / data

Cerebellum: one projection neuron type (Purkinje cell, essentially 2-D / leaf-shaped) and five main intrinsic types. Granule cells have tiny somata (5–8 µm) and small dendritic arbors (~50 µm diameter) but mm-long axons, and are the most numerous single neuron type in the nervous system. Other interneurons: basket, stellate, and (larger) Golgi cells.
Neocortex: projection neurons (pyramidal cells) make up ~80% of all cortical neurons — opposite ratio to cerebellum/retina — possibly because cortical pyramidal cells send local axon collaterals (local-circuit + long-range function). The striate cortex of the macaque monkey is estimated to contain >40 morphologically identifiable interneuron types. Only interneurons expressing specific calcium-binding proteins reach the classification certainty of cerebellar/retinal cells.
Retina: ~five major neuron classes divided into ~60 individual types: retinal ganglion cells (projection; ~12 types), horizontal cells (2 types), bipolar cells (~12 types), amacrine cells (~30 types). Example of molecular/functional coupling: ON bipolar cells depolarize to light, arborize deep in the inner plexiform layer, express metabotropic mGluR6; OFF bipolar cells hyperpolarize, arborize high, express ionotropic AMPA/kainate receptors.
Mosaic regularity (law of coverage): neurons of a given type maintain a minimum exclusion distance from same-type neurons, whereas spacing relative to different types is entirely random — a powerful confirmation that a type is correctly identified. The mechanism for self-type recognition (possibly ~60 cell-type-specific recognition signals) is unknown.
The retinal survey is judged essentially complete (~98% of retinal neurons; no major players missed), with a few orphan wide-field types remaining.
Cortex extrapolation: from spacing, cell number, and dendritic-field assumptions, one observer estimated ~1,000 cortical cell types, highlighting a large gulf between that figure and current understanding of cortical diversity.
Caveat: this is a 2004 Primer/opinion piece grounded in morphology and early genomics; “few instances of truly cell-type-specific gene expression have been discovered so far” — partial (mixed) specificity was the norm at the time.

Connections

Sits under the new brain-development-neurogenomics hub as a foundational, pre-single-cell-era statement of the cell-type cataloguing problem.
Conceptual precursor to modern single-cell atlas efforts that this wiki tracks (e.g. proteomic and transcriptomic landscapes of brain tissue such as wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of) — Masland’s “parts list” goal is what high-throughput single-cell/-omics now attempts to deliver at molecular resolution.
The emphasis on cell-type-specific gene/protein expression as a defining feature links to expression-genomics studies of developing brain such as li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis and walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and.

Open questions

How do neurons of the same type recognize and exclude one another to form regular mosaics — are there dozens of distinct cell-type-specific recognition signals?
How many neuronal types does the neocortex truly contain (~100? ~1,000?), and can the retina’s combined morphology + molecular + mosaic approach be scaled to crack cortical taxonomy?
Will genome-wide screening eventually yield genuinely cell-type-specific markers, or will partial specificity plus morphology remain the practical standard?

wiki/sources/mazin-2012-widespread-splic.title: mazin-2012-widespread-splicing-changes-in-human
wiki/sources/mazin-2012-widespread-splic.content:
Widespread splicing changes in human brain development and aging
One-line: RNA-seq of human prefrontal cortex and cerebellum across the whole lifespan (2 days–98 years) shows that splicing changes affect more than a third of expressed brain genes, with a dominant “down-up” inclusion pattern that links infant and elderly brains via NMD-targeting segment inclusion.
Key takeaways

Two human RNA-seq data sets (DS1: paired-end, 30 individuals pooled into 6 PFC + 6 CBC samples; DS2: strand-specific single-end, 13 PFC individuals) cover the entire postnatal lifespan; age-related splicing changes affect close to 40% of genes expressed in the human brain.
Most splicing variation (~70%) occurs during development (0–14 years), much less during aging (25–98 years) — mirroring the known kinetics of gene expression change but, unlike expression, splicing shows strong directional asymmetry.
A single dominant “down-up” pattern (segment inclusion decreasing in development, increasing in aging) accounts for ~62% of age-related changes; these segments are enriched for retained introns, are poorly conserved, and are predicted to trigger nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — a process related to intron-retention and AS-NMD (poison-exons).
Five of six NMD-pathway genes with age-related expression (UPF2, UPF3b, SMG1, SMG5, SMG6; CASC3 being the exception in direction) mirror the down-up profile (low in development, high in aging), supporting NMD as a driver of the shared infant/elderly splicing state.
Age-related splicing patterns associate with expression profiles of splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins — notably ptbp1 and PTBP2 plus several hnRNPs (hnrnpa1, hnRNPH1/H3/F) — providing a candidate regulatory mechanism; >50% of changes could be linked to SF expression changes.
Changes were cross-validated against an independent human data set, rhesus macaque RNA-seq, Affymetrix exon arrays, RT-PCR, and label-free mass spectrometry at the protein level, indicating robustness and evolutionary conservation.

Notable claims / data

Sequencing: 181,555,729 read pairs (DS1) + 274,927,771 reads (DS2), avg ~18M reads/sample; ~64% mapped (≤3 mismatches), 93% within gene boundaries. 21,644 novel junctions found, implying ≥10% of brain transcript isoforms are unannotated.
Significant age-related splicing: 22% of genes in DS1 (3132 segments / 1456 genes) and 38% in DS2 (6114 segments / 2588 genes); 1484 segments / 721 genes significant in both data sets (the core analysis set).
DS1/DS2 age-related changes overlap significantly (Fisher P<0.0001) and correlate strongly; exon-array validation P<0.0001 in both regions; RT-PCR: 24/30 tested segments gave expected bands, all matching RNA-seq direction (change ρ=0.93; estimate ρ=0.88).
Directional bias: 75% of segments show decreased inclusion in development, 77% increased inclusion in aging. Of the 1484, 970 significant in development vs 310 in aging when tested separately.
“Down-up” segments significantly enriched for retained introns (P<0.0001) and NMD targets (P<0.001), and significantly less conserved (phastCons, Wilcoxon P<0.0001, holds even for retained introns alone).
Protein-level confirmation: 24 segments / 19 genes had sufficient mass-spec peptides; RNA–protein splicing-change correlation significant vs permutation (P=0.0004 integrated). Protein-confirmed genes implicated in neural function/disease: MAPT, NDRG2, NPTN, PALM, PTPRZ1.
Macaque reproducibility: of 1484 segments, 496 mappable/covered; human–macaque trajectories more positively correlated than chance (permutation P<0.005); development 321 and aging 131 segments consistent (Fisher P<0.0001 dev, P<0.005 aging).
Eight clusters (CL1–8, 1422/1484 segments); CL6 and CL8 enriched for neural GO terms (synaptogenesis, learning, behavior, regulation of neurogenesis/synaptic transmission). CL1 = the down-up pattern; 10 SFs with binding-motif enrichment, 8 positively / 2 negatively correlated with the CL1 profile.
Regional difference: 15% of DS1 age-related segments differ significantly between PFC and CBC (q<0.05); affected neural genes include APP (Alzheimer’s), BIN1, and protocadherin gamma (PCDHG, with PFC pattern resembling developing mouse brain).
Caveats stated by authors: only two brain regions and a small number of (pooled) individuals → likely underestimate; SFBM identification is computationally unreliable → SF role likely underestimated; un-annotated intron retention could not be detected; functional consequences of the inferred NMD increase in infancy/old age remain untested. Sex and ethnicity could not be assessed but showed no detectable confounding; tissue composition and RNA degradation ruled out as drivers.
Data: deposited at Sequence Read Archive, study accession SRP005169. Methods: Ensembl v57 annotation, hg19, Bowtie mapping, binomial GLM with logit link on age^0.25; analysis released as a standalone Java application + R package (bioinf.fbb.msu.ru/~mazin).

Connections

A foundational human-brain-lifespan companion to gene-expression work from the same group (Somel et al, 2010, reused here for protein data and expression context).
Closely related to kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated splicing) as a developmental-splicing sibling, and provides organism-level human-brain context for scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (NMD / mis-splicing in disease) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Brain/sQTL siblings in the wiki: zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (region-specific brain sQTL), park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative, wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (developing human brain proteome), humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (brain microglia isoforms).
Mechanistic links to ptbp1, PTBP2, and hnRNPs (hnrnpa1) as candidate regulators of the down-up pattern; methodological relation to intron-retention quantification and AS-NMD (poison-exons).

Open questions

Are the inferred NMD-targeting inclusion events in infants and the elderly functionally beneficial, deleterious, or neutral — and do they actively downregulate their host genes?
Why do infant and elderly brains converge on the same splicing state mechanistically — is it fully explained by the shared SF/NMD expression trajectory?
How do these lifespan splicing changes shape the establishment and deterioration of human cognition, and how much is missed by sampling only PFC and cerebellum at low individual numbers?

wiki/sources/michielsen-2026-decoding-ex.title: michielsen-2026-decoding-exon-inclusion-in
wiki/sources/michielsen-2026-decoding-ex.content:
Decoding exon inclusion in the human brain reveals more divergent splicing mechanisms in neurons than glia
One-line: Trains logistic-regression and deep-learning models to predict cell-type-specific exon inclusion (PSI) in human neurons vs glia from long-read single-nucleus data, finding that neuronal splicing of variable exons is consistently harder to predict and mechanistically diverges further from the standard sequence/RBP grammar than glial splicing.
Key takeaways

Uses long-read single-nucleus isoform RNA-seq (SnISOr-Seq) from human hippocampus (6 individuals) and frontal cortex (2 individuals) to compute cell-type-specific percent-spliced-in values for neurons and glia; “variable exons” are those with |ΔΨ(glia−neur)| > 0.25 (2,244 in HPC, 943 in FC).
Three model families predict exon inclusion: logistic regression on ENCODE eCLIP rna-binding-proteins binding sites (122 RBPs × 6 positional bins = 732 features), a deep-learning model adapted from Saluki using pre-mRNA sequence, and a combined sequence+RBP model — directly probing the splicing-code for alternative-splicing.
Central finding: glial PSI is much easier to predict than neuronal PSI across all models and both regions (median Spearman 0.54 vs 0.23 in HPC; 0.57 vs 0.10 in FC), implying neuronal splicing mechanisms in variable exons have diverged further from the general grammar.
Model interpretation (LR coefficients, in-silico saturation mutagenesis, TF-MoDISco) pinpoints cell-type-specific regulators — most strikingly ptbp1 and QKI in glia — and shows the sequence immediately around the splice sites dominates predictions.
The DL models also predict and prioritize the effects of splicing-qtls from GTEx, correctly predicting the expected direction for 83% of variants with a predicted effect.

Notable claims / data

Glia vs neuron predictability: median Spearman correlation 0.54 (glia) vs 0.23 (neuron) in HPC, and 0.57 vs 0.10 in FC; gap persists across LR, DL-sequence, and DL-sequence+RBP models.
Primary sequence is more informative for neurons: DLall-seq beats the best RBP-based LR model on neuronal variable exons (0.29 vs 0.23 in HPC); adding RBP channels helps glia (best model, 0.57 HPC / 0.65 FC) but hurts neurons.
RBP binding profiles of variable vs non-variable exons differ significantly more in neurons than glia (paired Wilcoxon, both HPC and FC). Splicing factors u2af1, U2AF2, SF3B4 mark high-PSI non-variable exons at the 3’ splice site, but this signal disappears in neuronal variable exons.
QKI has the strongest cell-type-specific LR coefficient in HPC (−0.15 glia vs +0.12 neuron at 3’ splice site), is significantly higher expressed in glia (Wilcoxon adj. p < 2.2e-16), and shows known position-dependent regulation (3’ SS represses, downstream promotes inclusion); variable exons are enriched for QKI sites (Fisher adj. p = 1.6e-13).
Neuron-specific exon features: of 82 variable microexons (≤27 bp), 80.5% have higher PSI in neurons; variable exons are enriched for frame-consistency (49.6% vs 37.3%), and frame-consistent variable exons skew toward neuronal inclusion (58.1%).
sQTL prioritization: of 326 GTEx hippocampus sQTL variants in range, an effect (|ΔISM| > 0.005) was predicted for 71; 83% (59/71) matched the expected direction; 74.6% of predicted-effect variants lie within 15 bp of a splice site. ISM showed only the ~50 bp upstream of the 3’ SS and ~150 bp downstream of the 5’ SS carry effects > 0.1.
Cross-validation across mouse data and ENCODE4 PGP1-derived astrocytes/neurons reproduces the glia > neuron predictability gap, arguing it is not a protocol artifact. Controls rule out PSI-distribution, intra-cell-type heterogeneity, and cross-individual variance as explanations.
Caveats: ENCODE eCLIP RBP data is from non-brain cell lines (K562, HepG2) and lacks key neuronal RBPs (NOVA1/2); adding iPSC-motor-neuron NOVA eCLIP did not improve performance (likely batch effects). Authors propose neuronal splicing may be less sequence-dependent and more shaped by chromatin accessibility, RNA methylation, or polymerase speed.

Connections

Builds directly on long-read single-cell brain isoform data and shares lineage with humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (long-read brain isoforms) and the SnISOr-Seq cortex work cited for ASD-variable exons.
Complements brain splicing-qtl work such as zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls by adding a predictive/mechanistic layer (variant effect prioritization) on top of QTL discovery.
A concrete, brain-cell-type instantiation of the splicing-code / alternative-splicing regulatory-grammar program reviewed in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and the developmental angle of kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated.
Uses ENCODE eCLIP RBP binding data (cf. encode-rbp-map) and individual regulators ptbp1, u2af1, srsf1; the sequence-based deep models are conceptually adjacent to spliceai (the paper contrasts its short effective receptive field with SpliceAI’s 10 kb context).

Open questions

What non-sequence layers (chromatin accessibility, RNA m6A methylation, Pol II speed, transcription-factor occupancy) account for the residual neuronal unpredictability, and can they be added as model channels?
Would brain-cell-type-specific RBP eCLIP (rather than K562/HepG2) close the neuron performance gap, especially for NOVA1/2 and other neuron-enriched factors?
Can longer input windows or explicit exon-exon coordination modeling (mutually associated/exclusive exons, common in brain) improve prediction beyond the splice-site-proximal grammar?
Does a per-cell multitask formulation, possibly with spatial isoform information, capture cell-type-specific coordination that pseudo-bulk PSI misses?

wiki/sources/mironov-2021-an-extended-ca.title: mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem
wiki/sources/mironov-2021-an-extended-ca.content:
An extended catalogue of tandem alternative splice sites in human tissue transcriptomes
One-line: Builds tass-catalogue, the most complete to-date catalogue of
tandem alternative splice sites (TASS) — closely spaced (≤30 nt) alternative
donor/acceptor splice-sites — from 8,548 GTEx samples, and shows that a real
fraction of these “splicing-noise” events are tissue-specific, RBP-regulated,
structurally consequential, and under evolutionary selection.
Key takeaways

What TASS are. The second most frequent alternative-splicing subtype
after exon skipping: alternative choice of donor/acceptor splice sites located
only a few nt apart. The two best-studied classes are acceptor NAGNAGs (±3 nt)
and donor GYNNGYs (±4 nt). ~15–25% of mammalian genes possess TASS; most have
been attributed to spliceosomal error / splicing noise.
maSS vs miSS framing. Within each TASS cluster, splice sites are ranked by
read support: rank 1 = major splice site (maSS), rank ≥2 = minor splice
sites (miSS), which are expressed several orders of magnitude weaker. The paper
introduces the relative-usage metric φ (split reads at a miSS as a fraction of
miSS+maSS reads, single-ended) — a TASS analogue of PSI for
exons.
Three-source catalogue. Combines (1) annotated splice sites from GENCODE v19 +
RefSeq (~570k), (2) expressed sites from GTEx split-read alignments (~800k),
and (3) cryptic sites scored by spliceai (>0.1, ~197k unique). A TASS
cluster = ≥2 same-type sites, each successive pair within 30 nt. ~177k of ~1M sites
fall in clusters; the analyzed set (clusters ≤5, ≥1 expressed site) is ~151k sites.
Extends TASSDB2. TASSDB2 (an EST-based TASS database) is limited to 2–12 nt
separation; this catalogue adds ~51k previously-absent TASS (~18k miSS beyond
TASSDB2). Detection plateaus by 8,548 samples, suggesting near-completeness.
Regulation predicted by integrating three data types — tissue-specific φ
(GTEx), RBP differential expression, and miSS response to shRNA knockdown of
103 RBPs (rmats-turbo novelSS), cross-checked with eCLIP footprints. Yields a
flagship prediction: a miSS in QKI exon 6 repressed by ptbp1 in muscle/heart.

Notable claims / data

Abundance. 115,912 expressed TASS (59.8% annotated, 40.2% de novo, but de novo
carry only 0.17% of split reads). 45,739 expressed miSS — 82% unannotated, vs maSS
87% annotated (Table 2).
Significance & tissue-specificity. A zero-inflated Poisson model of rmiSS vs
rmaSS calls 9,303 (20%) of 45,739 expressed miSS significantly expressed
(Q<5%, φ>0.05); a linear dummy-variable model yields 2,496 tissue-specific miSS
(|Δφt|>0.05); 234 (9%) become the maSS in at least one tissue. Testis, cerebellum,
cerebellar hemisphere harbor the most tissue-specific miSS.
Reading frame & NMD. Coding miSS expression is dominated by 3-nt-multiple
(frame-preserving) shifts; frame-disrupting coding miSS are upregulated on NMD
inactivation (UPF1+XRN1 co-depletion), showing NMD suppresses them. Yet ~half of
tissue-specific coding miSS still disrupt frame and are more conserved than
non-significant miSS — suggesting NMD-coupled expression fine-tuning.
NAGNAGs / GYNNGYs. 4,761 expressed acceptor ±3 nt miSS (690 tissue-specific),
reconfirming 89% of 1,884 alternatively-spliced and 29% of 1,338 tissue-specific
NAGNAGs from Bradley et al.; +190 novel tissue-specific NAGNAGs (e.g. MYRF exon 20).
2,794 GYNNGY donors reconfirm 52% of 796 from Wang et al.; +37 novel (e.g. PAXX).
RBP regulation. 256 miSS-RBP-tissue triples → 163 co-directed vs 93
anti-directed; co-directed triples enriched among those with an eCLIP peak.
Six high-confidence eCLIP-supported pairs (e.g. QKI–PTBP1). PTBP1 stimulates
proximal (|shift|<5 nt) but suppresses distal miSS — opposite polypyrimidine-tract
modes.
Cell types. PROMO primary-cell RNA-seq: 1,072 cell-type-specific + 1,821
tissue-of-origin-specific miSS; miSS profiles more similar across tissues within a
cell type than across cell types within a tissue — miSS usage governed more by cell
type than tissue (e.g. IGFLR1 in smooth muscle).
Protein structure. Significant (and especially tissue-specific) miSS preferentially
hit intrinsically disordered regions; their indels enrich for short linear motifs
(SLiMs, ELM) and methylation/PTM sites (dbPTM). Still, >10% of tissue-specific miSS
affect core/PPI/ligand-binding regions — cases modeled with I-TASSER, FoldX
(PUM1: ΔΔG +13 kcal/mol, less stable minor isoform), and BAlaS CASM (ANAPC5, PICALM LIR).
Evolution & noise. O/E substitution test (marmoset/galago outgroups, GTEx +
1000 Genomes SNP allele frequencies): significant and tissue-specific miSS show
maSS-like negative selection preserving consensus nucleotides but no positive
selection (preserving a “suboptimal” state); non-significant miSS show none.
A mixture model bounds the noisy fraction: <15% of maSS, <60% of significant
non-tissue-specific miSS, <54% of tissue-specific miSS, >63% of
non-significant miSS — i.e. ≥46% of tissue-specific miSS are discernible from noise.

Connections

Catalogue/resource — companion on the resource side to shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection
(rMATS-turbo is the engine for the knockdown analysis here) and to the
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying toolkit survey; it quantifies
a specific AS subtype rather than predicting from sequence like spliceai
(which it uses to define cryptic sites).
Metric lineage. φ is a single-ended sibling of percent-spliced-in; relevant
to anyone reading the splicing-methods line of this wiki.
RBP regulation. Provides concrete human-tissue evidence for
rna-binding-proteins / splicing-factors driving alternative site choice;
the QKI–ptbp1 case ties into ptbp1’s known role in muscle differentiation.
Evolution & disease. Frames TASS indels as equivalent to short genomic indels,
linking to splicing-and-evolution and the disordered-region / PTM themes in
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Biology of AS. Tissue/cell-type specificity and the noise-vs-function debate
connect to lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative and
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.

Open questions

Protein-level reality. What fraction of these miSS actually translate? Shotgun
proteomics is poorly suited (low coverage; trypsin sites overlap splice-site
consensus). Ribo-Seq + structure are used as proxies — direct proteomic validation
remains open.
Mechanism of the noisy-but-conserved frame-disrupting tissue-specific miSS —
is NMD-coupled level tuning the real function, or a confound?
Generalizing RBP rules. Only 103 RBPs (ENCODE K562/HepG2) with eCLIP were
testable; the proximal/distal PTBP1 dichotomy hints at position-dependent rules
worth extending to other RBPs and tissues beyond the two cell lines.

wiki/sources/mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-.title: mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian
wiki/sources/mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-.content:
Mapping and quantifying mammalian transcriptomes by RNA-Seq
One-line: Foundational RNA-Seq paper that maps and quantifies mouse transcriptomes by deep short-read sequencing, introducing the RPKM normalization measure and the erange analysis pipeline, and demonstrating direct, microarray-free detection of splice events at genome scale.
Key takeaways

Establishes RNA-Seq as a method to digitally quantify transcript prevalence by deeply sequencing cDNA (41–52 million mapped 25-bp reads) from poly(A)-selected adult mouse brain, liver and skeletal muscle.
Introduces RPKM (reads per kilobase of exon model per million mapped reads) as a normalized read-density measure reflecting molar transcript concentration, enabling transparent within- and between-sample comparison.
Introduces the erange software pipeline to assign unique reads, allocate ambiguous “multireads”, detect splice-crossing reads, and cluster reads outside known gene models (RNAFAR features) into candidate exons/genes.
Detects splicing directly by mapping splice-crossing reads against a library of all known UCSC mm9 gene-model splices — a capability not readily provided by expression microarrays or SAGE.
Finds alternative splicing prominent: 3,462 genes expressed two or more splice isoforms across the three tissues; 1.45 × 10^5 distinct splices observed out of ~2 × 10^5 possible.
Beyond known annotations, the data suggest revised gene models: ~3,000 extended/new 3′ UTRs, ~3,000 new/extended 5′ exons (alternate promoters), ~17,000 candidate new exon features, 596 new candidate transcripts, and new candidate microRNA precursors (e.g., mir-124-1).

Notable claims / data

Linear dynamic range across five orders of magnitude in RNA concentration using in-vitro Arabidopsis/lambda RNA standards (~0.5–50,000 transcripts per cell; R^2 > 0.99).
Technical replicate reproducibility R^2 = 0.96 (brain); ~93% of uniquely mapped reads fall on exons (which are <2% of the genome), ~4% intronic, ~3% intergenic (rough upper bound on noise).
At ~40 M mapped reads, detection is robust at ≥1.0 RPKM for a typical 2-kb mRNA (~80 reads, P < 10^-16); 3 RPKM ≈ one transcript per liver cell; 1 RPKM ≈ one transcript per cell in C2C12.
A splice is detected with 95% confidence if its transcript is represented at >11 RPKM in a 40-M-read transcriptome; splice events identified effectively for transcripts >15–25 RPKM. Splice detection for low-abundance isoforms is sporadic — these are minimum estimates of alternative splicing extent.
Multiread handling: 13–24% of mappable reads are multireads (match 2–10 loci); discarding them (Solexa default) drastically undercounts paralog families (e.g., ubiquitin B: 97% multireads). erange redistributes multireads proportionally to unique+splice reads, changing quantification >30% for 28% of genes above 5 RPKM in muscle and improving correlation with Affymetrix data (R^2 0.62 → 0.69).
Caveats: data are not strand-specific; long-range splice contiguity / full isoform quantification is not attempted (reported per-locus); low-prevalence RNAs from minority cells in complex tissues (e.g., brain) remain inaccessible at current cost/capacity.

Connections

A foundational methods/resource source for the splicing topic: it pioneered direct sequencing-based splice detection that later, splicing-focused tools build on (cf. shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection and rmats for differential alternative-splicing quantification from RNA-seq).
RPKM is an early quantification metric conceptually upstream of isoform-ratio metrics such as percent-spliced-in used by later splicing tools.
Belongs alongside other splicing-methods sources; provides the experimental/computational substrate (RNA-seq read mapping, splice-junction libraries) that downstream alternative-splicing analysis depends on.

Open questions

How would prevalence-normalized input RNA plus paired-end and longer (e.g., 454) reads enable a comprehensive, isoform-resolved splice map — the explicit extension the authors propose?
Can long-range splice contiguity (full isoform assembly) be recovered, which this per-locus RPKM approach deliberately does not address?
How do sequence-error-driven false positives in gene families affect quantification, and how much do longer/higher-quality reads and probabilistic transcriptome models mitigate them?

wiki/sources/neil-2025-poison-exons-tuni.title: neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing
wiki/sources/neil-2025-poison-exons-tuni.content:
Poison exons: tuning RNA splicing for targeted gene regulation
One-line: A review (from Remix Therapeutics) of poison exons (PEs) — alternatively spliced exons whose inclusion introduces a premature termination codon that routes the host mRNA to nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — covering their role in endogenous gene regulation, their misregulation in disease (notably neurodevelopmental disorders and cancer), and how splice-modulating ASOs and small molecules exploit them to drug “undruggable” targets.
Key takeaways

Poison exons couple alternative-splicing to NMD (“AS-NMD”): inclusion of a PE inserts a premature termination codon (PTC), and because exon-junction complexes (EJCs) downstream of a stalled ribosome recruit UPF1/SMG1, the transcript is preferentially degraded. This makes PE inclusion a tunable knob on host-gene abundance, a form of post-transcriptional-splicing regulation rather than proteome diversification.
PEs are detected via short-read rna-seq (exon causing a PTC >50 nt upstream of an EJC), but many are efficiently degraded and only surface under NMD perturbation (UPF1/SMG5/6/7 knockdown or chemical NMD inhibition), EJC RNA-immunoprecipitation, capture sequencing of NMD intermediates, or long-read-rna-seq; computational/ML methods increasingly predict functional PEs.
PE misregulation drives disease in both cis (SRE/splice-sites mutations shifting PE inclusion) and trans (splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins dysfunction). Neurodevelopmental disorders are especially affected because of long introns and embedded regulatory elements.
PEs are a therapeutic opportunity (splicing-therapeutics): ASOs can skip a pathogenic PE to restore expression (haploinsufficiency), or small molecules can induce PE inclusion to degrade a disease driver — analogous to PROTAC/molecular-glue targeted protein degradation but acting on RNA.
Two mechanistically distinct splice modulators of SMN2 are approved (nusinersen, risdiplam) and ~6 PE-targeting drugs are in clinical trials across oncology and non-oncology indications, signaling a maturing modality.

Notable claims / data

90–95% of human genes produce multiple isoforms via alternative splicing; large-scale reporter assays imply >1 million autonomous exons in the human genome.
~15% of all mRNAs are NMD targets; ~5% of exons (in ~13% of genes) show differential expression attributable to AS-NMD; many genes incorporate PEs into >20% of their expressed transcripts. PE enrichment is highest in introns with weak splice sites and in long introns.
PEs are over-represented in NMD-deficient RNA-seq datasets and show stronger predicted splice-site strength and greater cross-species conservation than “noise” cryptic events. A CRISPR screen estimated up to 50% of PEs may affect cellular fitness; computational models suggest possibly >1 million PEs in the human transcriptome.
Neuronal development: ~3000 alternatively spliced exons are coordinated by antagonistic RBPs and ~33% of such cassette exons are predicted to trigger NMD on inclusion or exclusion. Disease examples: a PE in FLNA (periventricular nodular heterotopia); TDP-43 mislocalization in ALS causing cryptic exon inclusion incl. a UNC13A PE that drives NMD-mediated loss of UNC13A.
Cancer: loss-of-function RBM10 (lung adenocarcinoma) and gain-of-function sf3b1 (myeloid cancers, altered 3’ splice-site selection / frameshifts) reshape the NMD-targeted transcript landscape; SR-protein PEs are autoregulated and dysregulated in tumors.
Therapeutics (Table 1): ASOs — STK-001/zorevunersen skips a SCN1A PE for Dravet syndrome (Phase 1/2a–2/3); STK-002 reduces an OPA1 PE for ADOA; smn2-targeting eteplirsen (DMD, approved) and nusinersen (SMA, approved). Small molecules (U1 snRNP–5’ss molecular glues) — risdiplam (approved, SMA), branaplam (HD; VIBRANT-HD halted for peripheral neuropathy), PTC518 (HD; ~43% mHTT reduction in CSF at 10 mg), SKY-0515 (HD/PMS1; ~72% HTT mRNA reduction in blood at 9 mg), REM-422 (MYB degrader, ACC/AML/MDS, Remix), RGT-61159 (MYB, Rgenta). Beyond PEs: BPN-15477/PTC258/PTC680 for ELP1 (familial dysautonomia), SKY-1214 inducing FANCL/FANCI exon skipping.
NMD escape: some PE-containing transcripts evade NMD and translate potentially harmful truncated peptides — PE inclusion is not always simple loss-of-function.

Connections

Mechanistically a deep dive on the AS-NMD / poison-exon motif introduced more briefly in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator; both frame poison exons as a way splicing tunes abundance rather than protein form.
Disease framing complements scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (RNA mis-splicing as a disease mechanism) — here specialized to PTC/NMD-coupled events and their druggability.
The neurodevelopmental/ALS examples (FLNA, UNC13A, TDP-43 cryptic exons) connect to brain-splicing siblings such as humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.
Names trans-acting regulators already in the wiki: sf3b1 (gain-of-function 3’ splice-site shifts) and the splice-modulator target smn2.

Open questions

Of the vast number of PEs, which trigger efficient degradation, and how can NMD penetrance be predicted up front to pick tractable targets?
Which splicing regulatory elements are most amenable to selective small-molecule/ASO modulation, and how can off-target effects of motif-targeting small molecules be predicted?
Can splice-switching small molecules rescue low gene expression (via PE skipping) as effectively as ASOs while keeping small-molecule delivery advantages?
Can paralog-selective targeting be achieved by exploiting faster-evolving intronic SREs between near-identical proteins?

wiki/sources/nguyen-2025-aberrant-splici.title: nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease
wiki/sources/nguyen-2025-aberrant-splici.content:
Aberrant splicing in Huntington’s disease accompanies disrupted TDP-43 activity and altered m6A RNA modification
One-line: Mechanistic multi-omics study showing that mutant-HTT-driven aberrant splicing in Huntington’s disease arises from TDP-43 loss of function coupled with altered m6A RNA modification, validated across HD mouse models and human patient brain.
Key takeaways

HD aberrant splicing is dominated by increased exon exclusion (~70% of skipped-exon events) in both cortex and striatum, conserved from R6/2 mice to human HD patient cortex.
Two HTT-interacting rna-binding-proteins are implicated as upstream regulators of HD exon skipping: TDP-43 (UG/GU-rich UGUGU motif) and the m6A writer METTL3 (DRACH motif), both identified via de novo motif enrichment on excluded exons.
TDP-43 shows loss of nuclear function in HD: reduced nuclear localization, cytoplasmic phosphorylated-TDP-43 aggregates, and attenuated binding to dysregulated/aberrantly spliced target RNAs — recapitulating cryptic-exon (poison-exon-like, AS-NMD) biology seen in ALS/FTD.
m6A modification is reduced at specific sites on HD-dysregulated RNAs (including sites adjacent to TDP-43 binding), linking the epitranscriptome to TDP-43 activity; the authors argue m6A loss precedes/underlies decreased TDP-43 binding.
A novel neuropathological feature — nuclear pTDP-43 “aggregation-like (AL) bodies” co-localizing with HTT — is reported in HD patient neurons, distinct from canonical HTT inclusions.
Reinforces splicing-and-disease and links HD to the broader TDP-43 proteinopathy family (ALS, FTLD, AD, Parkinson’s). See scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.

Notable claims / data

DGE (R6/2, 3-month, n=10, sex-balanced): striatum 1,309 down / 648 up; cortex 1,253 down / 462 up (adj P<0.05, log2FC>1); 65.8% striatal-cortical DEG overlap, 95.4% same direction. All 266 genes of the HD266ssg striatal signature dysregulated (265 in expected direction).
rMATS AS (FDR<0.05): 9,033 RNAs / 3,620 genes in cortex; 10,074 RNAs / 3,854 genes in striatum. ~70% of skipped-exon events = higher exon exclusion in HD. ~60% of AS-containing genes also up/downregulated.
Human HD (rMATS on published datasets): prefrontal cortex BA9 (grade III/IV, 20 HD, 49 controls) 12,469 AS events; anterior cingulate cortex (6 HD, 6 controls) 7,730 AS events — exon exclusion increase confirmed.
Long-read (PacBio Iso-seq) validation: 1,840/2,311 (79.6%) cortex and 2,083/2,634 (79.1%) striatum significant exon exclusions confirmed; ~57% of novel splice junctions confirmed. Single-nuclei RNA-seq + DESJ-detection localized most splicing changes to inhibitory MSNs.
Unannotated/novel splicing: ~50% of R6/2 AS changes novel; MAJIQ and LeafCutter detected hundreds of novel events. 55% overlap (192/348) with TDP-43-knockdown splicing changes (Šušnjar et al.). Novel events drive expression changes in both directions (e.g. Cntn6 cryptic exon up; Ssbp2 unannotated exon lost).
TDP-43 eCLIP-seq (R6/2 + NT, n=8): ~80% binding overlap HD vs NT (most targets unchanged); binding decreased on downregulated genes and aberrantly spliced cassette-exon transcripts, not on upregulated genes. TDP-43 binds ~30% (79/266) of HD striatal signature genes. Caveat: binding distribution unexpectedly enriched in CDS/3’UTR (not intronic), attributed to cytoplasmic-mRNA enrichment in the protocol.
TDP-43 ASO knockdown (~50% KD) in WT mouse striatum clusters transcriptionally with R6/2 HD; siRNA TDP-43 KD in isogenic HTT-18Q vs mHTT-50Q iPSC-derived MSNs: 68.7% (382/556, P=1.5e-189) of KD-downregulated transcripts overlap HD-down genes; STMN2, UNC13B, CAMK2B downregulated in both.
m6A: Mettl3 nuclear expression significantly decreased in R6/2 cortex and striatum (Rbm15 down in cortex); global m6A by LC-MS unchanged (P=0.0567), but m6A eCLIP-seq showed DEGs carry more m6A sites than non-DEGs (downregulated genes highest), yet those sites are LESS methylated in HD. TDP-43 binding peaks center on m6A sites more sharply than HNRNPC/HNRNPL. Human HD caudate: m6A IF signal significantly increased (P<0.0001) with cytoplasmic METTL3 mislocalization.
Human TDP-43 pathology: nuclear TDP-43 significantly reduced in 5/5 HD SFG patients (P<0.0001); rare cytoplasmic pTDP-43 (S409/S410) aggregates; pTDP-43 AL bodies (~3 µm, in MAP2+ neurons) significantly increased in HD (P=0.0146), containing N-terminal TDP-43 and HTT.

Connections

Direct splicing-and-disease sibling to scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease — provides a concrete neurodegenerative mis-splicing mechanism (cryptic/poison-exon, AS-NMD).
Methodologically uses rmats, majiq-v2 and leafcutter for AS and novel-splicing detection, plus eCLIP and PacBio long-read RNA-seq; complements brain long-read work in humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.
Shares the TDP-43/RBP-regulation theme with baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and the developmental-splicing framing of kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated.
Human-brain disease counterpart to the brain-sQTL/atlas siblings (zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls, park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative).
Couples splicing regulation to the m6A epitranscriptome and to rna-binding-proteins biology — an axis adjacent to splicing-code and rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

Causal ordering: does m6A loss truly precede TDP-43 dysregulation, or are they co-dependent? (Inferred, not directly demonstrated.)
What is the composition/core of the novel nuclear pTDP-43 AL bodies (PML-like, nucleolar, or RNA-free anisosomes)?
Which additional cryptic-exon-regulating RBPs cooperate with HTT beyond TDP-43?
Can pharmacologic control of m6A restore TDP-43 splicing function as an HD therapeutic? (splicing-therapeutics direction.)
Why does the TDP-43 eCLIP binding distribution differ (CDS/3’UTR vs intronic) from prior maps — protocol artifact or HD-specific biology?

wiki/sources/nilsen-2010-expansion-of-th.title: nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome
wiki/sources/nilsen-2010-expansion-of-th.content:
Expansion of the eukaryotic proteome by alternative splicing
One-line: A foundational Nature Insight review arguing that alternative splicing is a principal source of proteomic diversity in multicellular eukaryotes, and that splice-site choice is governed by kinetic competition rather than dedicated tissue-specific regulators alone.
Key takeaways

Mammals have far fewer genes (~20,000) than the ~100,000 once estimated; the “missing” information is supplied largely by alternative-splicing, which lets one gene yield many mRNAs and proteins.
High-throughput sequencing indicates 95–100% of multi-exon human pre-mRNAs are alternatively spliced; isoform counts per gene range from two to several thousand.
Nearly all alternative splicing reduces to four basic modules: alternative 5′ splice-site choice, alternative 3′ splice-site choice, cassette-exon inclusion/skipping, and intron retention (see splice-sites).
Only a small number of splicing-factors regulate large numbers of events; most regulators (rna-binding-proteins) such as SR proteins and hnRNPs are ubiquitously expressed, with few truly tissue-specific exceptions (NOVA, nPTB/PTBP2, FOX1/FOX2, ESRP1/ESRP2, nSR100/SRRM4).
The simple “yin–yang” model (SR-protein enhancers vs hnRNP-bound silencers) has predictive value but greatly understates the biochemical complexity of regulation.
The authors advance a kinetic view: transcription rate, chromatin/histone marks, spliceosome component levels, intron size, and competition between splice sites jointly determine splice-site choice.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila dscam can generate 38,016 distinct mRNA isoforms from 95 alternative exons in four mutually exclusive clusters (12, 48, 33 and 2 variant exons), far exceeding the ~14,500 genes in the organism; 19,008 distinct extracellular domains arise via isoform-specific homophilic binding.
Human KCNMA1 (SLO) can produce >500 mRNA isoforms from one pre-mRNA via combined alternative 5′/3′ splice sites and cassette exons.
D. melanogaster mod(mdg4) makes 28 mRNAs via trans-splicing of common exons (one pre-mRNA) to variable exons on separate pre-mRNAs.
Dscam exon-6 mutually exclusive splicing is directed by competing intronic RNA secondary structures (a docking site pairing with one selector sequence per variant); hnRNP HRP36 (HRB87F) represses all 48 exonic sequences, and co-depleting an SR protein with HRP36 restores correct splicing — showing non-selected exons are not dormant but kinetically out-competed.
Constitutive exons carry more H3K36me3 than alternative exons; slowing RNA polymerase via this mark or heterochromatin shifts inclusion — proposed exon-”tethering” model where slow transcription favors inclusion.
Caveats: extent of “constitutive” vs regulated splicing is unknown; functional relevance of most isoforms is unproven (e.g. Drosophila CadN exon 18A/18B phenotype is rescued by the alternate isoform); cross-species comparisons (worm/fly vs human) rest on far less data.

Connections

Provides the conceptual backbone for many sibling pages: complements review-style sources lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Its kinetic/co-transcriptional framing prefigures cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; its RNA-secondary-structure argument links to rna-structure-and-splicing and peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
The “splicing code” question it poses anticipates splicing-code and modern predictors such as spliceai.
Discusses canonical regulators with existing entities: srsf1 (SR/SF2/ASF family), ptbp1 and nPTB, plus dscam as the flagship diversity example; relates to splicing-regulatory-elements and cis-regulatory-elements.
Evolutionary plasticity discussion connects to splicing-and-evolution.

Open questions

Can the absolute extent of alternative splicing actually account for organismal complexity across species?
How many mRNA isoforms are functionally relevant versus incidental?
Will a reliable, computable “splicing code” emerge, and how much do deep-intronic elements contribute to splice-site choice?
Do tissue-specific transcription-rate / chromatin differences (rather than tissue-specific regulators) drive a large fraction of regulated splicing, e.g. between brain/muscle and low-AS tissues?

wiki/sources/pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-.title: pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-.content:
Deep surveying of alternative splicing complexity in the human transcriptome by high-throughput sequencing
One-line: The first mRNA-Seq survey of alternative splicing across normal human tissues, which raised the estimate of alternatively spliced multiexon human genes to ~95% and put the count of intermediate- to high-abundance alternative splicing events at roughly 100,000.
Key takeaways

First analysis of alternative splicing complexity in human tissues using mRNA-Seq (Illumina Genome Analyzer) data, profiling six normal tissues: whole brain, cerebral cortex, heart, skeletal muscle, lung and liver, with datasets of 17–32 million 32-nt reads.
Combining mRNA-Seq with EST-cDNA data, the authors estimate that transcripts from ~95% of multiexon genes undergo alternative splicing — a substantial jump over the prior microarray/EST-based estimate of ~74% (two-thirds of genes).
New (previously uncatalogued) splice junctions were detected in roughly 20% of surveyed multiexon genes, many of them tissue specific, demonstrating that EST-cDNA catalogues substantially undercount alternative splicing.
mRNA-Seq exon inclusion estimates (a precursor notion of percent inclusion / percent-spliced-in) correlate well with an established quantitative alternative splicing microarray system, validating sequencing as a quantitative readout for splicing levels.
A foundational data point for the field’s view of alternative-splicing pervasiveness; relates to broader splicing-methods and the transition from microarray to sequencing-based splicing measurement.

Notable claims / data

Junction libraries mined from 15,702 multiexon UniGene clusters: 257,257 “known” junctions (EST/cDNA-supported) and 2,459,306 candidate “new” junctions (all hypothetical 5’-to-3’ splice-site pairings).
A logistic-regression classifier (trained on known vs. control “reverse” junctions, five features) achieved 94% sensitivity at 95% specificity by tenfold cross-validation.
Across the six tissues combined, 128,395–130,854 (49.9–50.9%) of the 257,257 known junctions were detected, versus only 121–135 (0.04–0.052%) of control junctions; each tissue contributed 18–31% of detected known junctions.
4,294–11,099 new splice junctions detected, corresponding to one or more new junction in 2,948 (18.8%) to 3,788 (24.1%) of surveyed genes.
Combining EST, cDNA and mRNA-Seq, >85% of multiexon genes contain at least one alternative splicing event; at high read coverage (16 to >500 reads/100 nt) AS events are detectable in 92–97% of multiexon genes — hence the ~95% headline.
Alternative splicing events per gene increase near-linearly (not quadratically) with exon number; median 0.5–0.75 AS events per exon at 32–256 reads/100 bases. From 175,944 exons across 15,702 genes, they predict ~88,000–132,000 AS events of comparable abundance in major human tissues, i.e. at least ~7 AS events per multiexon gene.
mRNA-Seq vs. microarray percent-inclusion correlation: r = 0.80 (n = 1,548) at a ≥20-read threshold, rising to r = 0.85 (n = 546) at ≥50 reads.
Caveats: estimates given as ranges depending on inclusion/exclusion of “repeat” junction reads; the survey covers only six tissues, and authors predict the new-junction count would at least double with fuller coverage. Quantitative validation relied on unpublished microarray data and supplementary methods. mRNA-Seq datasets deposited at GEO accession GSE13652.

Connections

A foundational counterpart to method-centric splicing sources in the wiki such as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection; this paper is the earlier “how pervasive is human AS” landmark that later quantification tools build on.
The exon-inclusion measurement validated here is conceptually the basis for percent-spliced-in, later formalized in tools like rmats.
Broad context for alternative-splicing and splicing-methods; complements review/overview sources like marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative by supplying the empirical pervasiveness estimate they cite.

Open questions

How much higher does the AS-gene fraction and event count go with deeper coverage and many more tissues (the authors expect new-junction counts to at least double)?
How many “new” junctions are functional/translated versus noise or low-abundance transcriptional leakage?
Why does selection appear to limit splicing complexity (near-linear, not quadratic, growth) in large genes — what constrains AS frequency per exon?
How well do these early short-read (32-nt) junction-based estimates hold up against full-length / long-read isoform sequencing?

wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.title: parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna
wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.content:
Genome-wide changes in lncRNA, splicing, and regional gene expression patterns in autism
One-line: Post-mortem transcriptome study of the largest autism cortex cohort to date, showing that ASD molecular pathology converges across lncRNA dysregulation, downregulated alternative splicing of neuron-specific/activity-dependent exons, and attenuated frontal–temporal regional gene-expression differences.
Key takeaways

rRNA-depleted RNA-seq of 251 post-mortem samples (frontal cortex, temporal cortex, cerebellum) from 48 individuals with ASD and 49 controls; differential gene expression (DGE) in cortex replicated prior microarray data (R² = 0.60).
ASD cortex shows 584 upregulated and 558 downregulated genes (FDR < 0.05, linear mixed-effects model); downregulated set enriched in neuronal genes (e.g. PVALB, SYT2 interneuron markers), upregulated set enriched in microglia/astrocyte genes. No significant DGE in cerebellum — cortex is selectively vulnerable.
A comprehensive alternative-splicing analysis found 1,127 differential splicing events in 833 genes in cortex (none in cerebellum). Most events are exclusion of neuron-specific exons; the differential splicing signature is not driven by DGE, consistent with splicing being an independent link to disease risk (splicing-and-disease).
Differential splicing correlates with cortical expression of neuronal splicing factors and overlaps experimentally defined targets of Rbfox1, SRRM4, and ptbp1 (PTBP1); events are enriched for neuronal activity-dependent exons, supporting an excitation/inhibition-balance model of ASD.
60 lncRNAs are in the DGE set; many are brain-enriched and primate-specific (e.g. LINC00693, LINC00689, both upregulated in ASD but normally downregulated during development; several interact with miRNA-processing complexes or FMRP).
Attenuated cortical patterning (ACP): 523 genes differ between frontal and temporal cortex in controls but not in ASD; the transcription factor SOX5 (sole ACP-set member among enriched TFs) loses its correlation with predicted targets in ASD, and SOX5 overexpression in human neural progenitors induced synaptic genes and repressed proliferation.
A genetically defined ASD subtype, chromosome 15q11.2–13.1 duplication (dup15q, n = 9), shows cis upregulation of the 15q region plus genome-wide (trans) DGE and differential splicing highly concordant with idiopathic ASD (R² = 0.64–0.65) but of greater magnitude — biological validation of the shared signature.
WGCNA identified 24 modules; 6 ASD-associated (3 up / 3 down). Downregulated modules are synaptic/neuronal, upregulated modules immune/glial (microglial), and the two anticorrelate. CTX.M19/CTX.M20 trajectories diverge between ASD and controls over the first two decades of life.

Notable claims / data

Cortex DGE replication R² = 0.60 (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) against independent microarray samples; signal present in >2/3 of ASD samples.
1,127 differential splicing events / 833 genes in cortex; reproduced with an alternative pipeline (OLego-based) and validated by RT–PCR.
Splicing-factor perturbation overlaps: Rbfox1 knockout, SRRM4 overexpression, and PTBP1 knockdown all significantly overlap ASD differential splicing (e.g. fold enrichments ~2.0–2.4, P down to 1.7 × 10⁻⁵ for inclusion/exclusion subsets).
PC1 of protein-coding DGE, lncRNA DGE, and differential splicing are highly correlated (R² > 0.8), indicating molecular convergence is a “unitary phenomenon.”
ACP set: 551 vs 51 genes show frontal–temporal DGE in controls vs ASD respectively; SOX5 DGE set enriched in ACP set (P = 1.1 × 10⁻⁷, Fisher’s exact). Δmedian SOX5–target correlation R drops by 0.24 in ASD.
dup15q vs ASD effect-size concordance: DGE R² = 0.64 (slope 3.7), differential splicing R² = 0.65 (slope 3.2), both P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶.
Module CTX.M24 enriched for ASD rare mutations and lncRNAs and fetal-cortex genes; CTX.M20 (overlapping the ACP set) enriched for ASD common-variant polygenic risk (FDR < 0.1), linking polygenic risk to regional attenuation.
Caveat: many observed changes may be compensatory/consequential rather than causal; ASD GWAS at the time was underpowered, limiting common-variant conclusions.

Connections

Direct disease sibling for splicing-and-disease — splicing alterations link to common disease risk independently of expression changes.
Brain/neurodevelopment context shared with kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the developing-brain proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Splicing-factor regulatory programs (Rbfox1, PTBP1, neuronal microexons via SRRM4) connect to baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and mis-splicing-in-disease reviews scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Brain-region splicing variation relates to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain sQTL) and microglial isoform work humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia being the upregulated cell type here).
Uses percent-spliced-in (ΔPSI) metrics and rna-seq throughout.

Open questions

Which splicing/lncRNA changes are causal vs compensatory responses to a primary genetic/prenatal insult?
How would larger, better-powered ASD GWAS sharpen the link between common polygenic risk and the CTX.M20/ACP regional-attenuation module?
What are the functions of the primate-specific brain lncRNAs (LINC00693, LINC00689) and their role as candidate ASD risk loci?
Do the activity-dependent splicing changes reflect altered excitation/inhibition balance mechanistically, and can the early-developmental trajectory window (first decade) be targeted for intervention?

wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.title: park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative
wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.content:
The Expanding Landscape of Alternative Splicing Variation in Human Populations
One-line: A review (from the Xing lab) of how population-scale RNA-seq, sQTL/ASAS analysis, massively parallel reporter assays, and machine/deep learning have revealed widespread naturally occurring genetic variation in alternative splicing across human populations, and its link to GWAS traits and disease.
Key takeaways

Genetic variation in splicing-regulatory-elements is pervasive in human populations; many sQTL SNPs are enriched at the 5’ and 3’ splice-sites but a large fraction lie outside them, modifying exonic/intronic enhancers/silencers or rna-binding-proteins binding sites.
Splicing quantitative trait locus (sQTL) analysis treats percent-spliced-in (PSI) values as quantitative traits tested against genotypes; allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) is a complementary cis-only approach using heterozygous SNPs to compare the two haplotypes within one individual.
sQTLs frequently colocalize with GWAS signals, arguing that alternative-splicing is a primary causal mechanism (not a secondary effect) linking genetic variation to complex traits and disease — comparable to or greater than eQTL effects (Li et al. 2016).
Massively parallel reporter assays (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) plus minigene reporters allow rapid screening of which variants causally alter splicing; deep learning models the “splicing code” to predict PSI and variant effects.
RNA-seq-based splicing analysis is emerging as a diagnostic and prognostic tool (rare-disease diagnosis, cancer survival prediction).

Notable claims / data

cis splicing mutations are estimated to constitute 15%–60% of human disease mutations.
Population sQTL studies (Table 1): Montgomery et al. (LCLs, n=60, 110 sQTL events); Pickrell et al. (LCLs, n=69, 187 sQTL genes at 10% FDR); Lappalainen/Geuvadis (LCLs, n=462, 639 trQTL genes); Battle et al. (whole blood, n=922, 1,370 sQTL genes at 5% FDR, 159 in high LD with GWAS SNPs); Fadista et al. (pancreatic islets, n=89, 371 sQTLs in T2D loci); Li et al. family study (LCLs, n=17, 261 sQTLs at 50% FDR, enriched for rare variants); GTEx pilot (43 tissues, 1,641 samples/175 individuals, ~1,900 sQTL genes/tissue with Altrans and ~250 with sQTLseekeR; tissue-specific sQTLs only 7%–21%); Chen et al.
