(immune cells, n=197, >2,000 sQTL genes/cell type); Pala et al. (Sardinia leukocytes, n=624, 6,768 sQTLs); Takata et al. (brain prefrontal cortex, n=206, 1,595 sQTLs enriched for schizophrenia loci).
Worked sQTL/GWAS examples: SP140 exon 7 (silent exonic SNP rs28445040; T allele lowers inclusion; in LD with GWAS SNPs for CLL, MS, Crohn, IBD) and ERAP2 exon 10 (intronic 5’SS SNP rs2248374 activating a cryptic 5’SS → PTC → NMD; linked to Crohn/IBD). Both causal variants are silent-exonic or intronic and missed by common variant-annotation tools.
MaPSy (Soemedi et al.) tested 4,964 exonic disease mutations: ~10% perturbed splicing in both in vivo and in vitro assays vs ~3% of common SNPs (likely a lower-bound, single cell type HEK293). Rosenberg et al. tested >2 million synthetic minigenes for alt 5’/3’ splice site selection.
Deep learning splicing-code models (Frey/Xiong et al.) achieved R²=0.65 between predicted and empirical PSI on independent data and predicted misregulated splicing in 19 ASD candidate genes.
Rare-disease diagnosis proof-of-concept (Cummings et al.): muscle RNA-seq of 63 patients vs 184 GTEx controls yielded genetic diagnoses for 35% of previously undiagnosed individuals (e.g., recurrent COL6A1 deep-intronic GC→GT creating a novel 5’SS).
Notes the FDA approval of nusinersen (antisense oligonucleotide) correcting splicing in spinal muscular atrophy.

Connections

Methodologically anchors event-based tools cited elsewhere in the wiki: miso, rmats, MAJIQ, suppa, and PSI (percent-spliced-in); transcript-level quantitation via kallisto/bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification.
Complements deep-learning splice-prediction work centered on spliceai and the splicing-code; this review predates SpliceAI but reviews its lineage (Barash splicing code, AVISPA, Leung/Xiong deep models, BRIE).
Extends splicing-and-disease and splicing-and-evolution into the population-genetics regime; relates to cis-regulatory-elements and ai-in-genomics.
Same Xing lab as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (rMATS); ships only the helper rmats2sashimiplot as a Web Resource, not a new analysis tool.

Open questions

How many GWAS signals across all complex traits are ultimately splicing-causal, once full ASAS + long-read coverage and additional tissues are layered in?
Can predictive models incorporate chromatin state, epigenetic marks, and 3D genome organization to capture co-transcriptional splicing regulation?
How well do MPRA/HEK293 reporter results generalize to the cell-type-specific splicing of disease-relevant tissues?
What is the contribution of non-SNP variants (indels, short tandem repeats) to population-scale splicing variation?

wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.title: peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes
wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.content:
Emerging role of G-quadruplexes in alternative splicing regulation
One-line: A mechanistic review arguing that RNA G-quadruplexes (rG4s) are an emerging class of secondary-structure regulators of alternative-splicing, acting position-dependently near splice-sites to recruit or block splicing factors, with strong links to cancer and neurodegeneration and a therapeutic angle via G4 ligands.
Key takeaways

g-quadruplex structures are noncanonical four-stranded folds in guanine-rich nucleic acid: guanines pair via Hoogsteen (not Watson–Crick) bonds into stacked G-quartets, stabilised by K+ cations. They form in both DNA (dG4s) and RNA (rG4s) and act as rna-structure-and-splicing elements distinct from sequence motifs.
rG4s regulate alternative-splicing in a position-dependent way: located near donor (5’ ss) or acceptor (3’ ss) sites, they either recruit rG4-binding splicing-factors that redirect the spliceosome to proximal sites, or remodel pre-mRNA to mask/expose splice sites — functioning as enhancers or silencers (splicing-regulatory-elements).
This is a structure-based complement to the splicing-code: rG4s overlay sequence-defined splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements, and their effect depends on dynamic folding equilibria rather than a fixed consensus.
Many examples span all five major ASE types (exon skipping, intron retention, alternative 5’/3’ ss, mutually exclusive exons); rna-binding-proteins including the hnRNP family, FMRP, AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25 read these rG4s.
dG4s also influence splicing indirectly via cotranscriptional-splicing: by slowing RNA Pol II elongation (and via R-loops on the non-template strand), they alter exon inclusion (Box 3), separate from the direct rG4 effects.
rG4-controlled splicing is heavily implicated in splicing-and-disease, especially cancer (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x, BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) and neurodegeneration (BACE1/Alzheimer, FMR1/Fragile X).
G4 ligands (e.g. PhenDC3, pyridostatin, GQC-05) are model splicing-therapeutics that stabilise/unfold rG4s or block G4-binding proteins; effects across ASEs are often contradictory, so no general rule yet exists.

Notable claims / data

Scale: ~100,000 proteins from ~20,000 human genes via AS. ~60% of human transcripts contain ≥1 putative quadruplex-forming sequence (PQS); ~1,200,000 PQSs (300,000 unique motifs) across transcripts and splice junctions; canonical folding rule d(GGG+N1–7)x4 predicts ~400,000 dG4s in the genome.
A threefold enrichment of G4s near splice junctions, preferentially on the non-template (coding) strand (implying the corresponding rG4 matters); G4-at-ss enrichment is conserved in vertebrates but not invertebrates (possibly because AS matters less there).
FMR1 (Fragile X): two rG4s in exon 15 inhibit inclusion; FMRP binds them via its RGG motif and modulates its own splicing, favouring minor ISO2/ISO3 isoforms (autoregulatory feedback). ~15 loop mutations (ΔG4) altered exon-15 splicing without changing translation.
AFF family (AFF1–4; AFF2/FMR2 → FRAXE intellectual disability) bind rG4s via a conserved C-terminal domain (not RGG), localise to nuclear speckles, and broadly modulate AS.
CD44: intron-8 rG4 (element I-8) promotes the epithelial CD44v isoform; hnRNPF regulates it (depletion accelerates EMT). Alkaloids emetine and cephaeline modulate inclusion by unfolding rG4s (~60% of emetine-affected ASEs are skipped exons).
EWS-FLI (Ewing sarcoma): exon-8 rG4 promotes exon exclusion (keeping the oncoprotein); hnRNPH1 destabilises the rG4. Pyridostatin (PDS) blocks hnRNPH1 binding and reduces viability of EWSR1-fusion cells.
BCL-2: exon-3 rG4 favours skipping → proapoptotic BCL-2β; interplay with three nearby m6A sites; SRSF1 (an rG4-binding factor) resolves the rG4. Bcl-2β sensitises breast cancer cells to chemotherapy.
hTERT: intron-6 rG4s act as intronic splicing silencers; ligands Bisquinolinium 12459, CX-5461, and PhenDC3 shift splicing toward the inactive β transcript (telomestatin/BRACO19 had no effect — ligand selectivity).
TP53: intron-3 rG4 promotes intron-2 retention → dominant-negative ΔNp53/Δ40p53; ligand 360A shifts toward full-length p53. A common intronic polymorphism disrupting this rG4 is linked to increased cancer risk.
BCL-x (“rG4 saga”, Figure 1): exon-2 GQ-2/Q2 rG4 (overlaps the Gt16 G-tract) downstream of the alternative 5’ ss controls Bcl-xL (antiapoptotic) vs Bcl-xS (proapoptotic) balance. Splicing factor RBM25 binds Q2 directly via its arginine–glutamate (RE) domain — a novel G4-binding domain — and this binding is required for Bcl-xS production. Ligands GQC-05, PhenDC3, and the novel PhenDH8/PhenDH9 promote Bcl-xS; a genome screen for the Gt16-like G-tract found 3374 exons in 2707 genes.
Other cases: PAX9 intron-1 rG4 (positively regulates splicing; conserved in placental mammals; affects dentition — splicing-and-evolution); IGFN1 intron-15 (renal/breast cancer, PDS); CD133 introns 3/7 (cancer stem cells, RHPS4); BACE1 exon-3 alternative 5’ ss (hnRNPH/hnRNPL; Alzheimer); TRα/THRA alternative 3’ ss (G30 element, only suggestive evidence); vertebrate mirtron 5’-arm rG4s aiding mirtron biogenesis.
Mechanism (Box 6, splicing-and-chromatin/condensate angle): rG4–protein interactions may nucleate liquid–liquid phase separation (LLPS) at nuclear speckles, concentrating splicing factors — proposed but not yet demonstrated for the validated rG4 cases.

Connections

Structure vs sequence: extends splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements from a sequence-motif view to a secondary-structure view — rG4s are folded ESE/ISE/ESS/ISS-like elements whose activity is position-dependent and dynamic, complementing the splicing-code.
Sits in the same RNA-structure thread as Lee 2015 (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative), which discusses RNA secondary structure masking splice sites; this review supplies the G4-specific molecular detail and the new [[rna-structure-and-splicing]] entity.
splicing-and-disease: a curated cancer-centric catalogue (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x/BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) plus neurodegeneration (BACE1, FMR1).
splicing-therapeutics: positions G4 ligands as splicing modulators; relates to BH3-mimetic (venetoclax) and SF3B1-modulator strategies, and to G4-inducing ASOs (G-ASOs) for exon skipping in Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
cotranscriptional-splicing: dG4/R-loop effects on Pol II elongation (Box 3) tie G4 biology to the transcription–splicing coupling thread.

Open questions

The molecular mechanisms of rG4-mediated AS, and the role of phase separation, remain largely unproven (mostly hypothesised models).
Which methods can confirm that an in-silico PQS actually forms a functional rG4 in cellulo and controls a specific gene’s AS (single-RNA-molecule monitoring is lacking).
G4 ligand selectivity is a major limitation: hundreds of thousands of competing dG4s/rG4s exist, effects across ASEs are often antinomic, and no general predictive “G4–ligand code” yet exists.
Is rG4-mediated splicing a general feature of apoptosis-regulating genes with opposing isoforms (e.g. BCL-x, MCL-1)?
Systematic identification of the full set of rG4-binding RBPs that reshape the AS landscape, and the influence of surrounding chemical RNA modifications (e.g. m6A), are still needed.

wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.title: pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in
wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.content:
Poison exon splicing in the human brain: a new paradigm for understanding and targeting neurological disorders
One-line: A genome-wide catalog of human poison exons (PEs) — alternative exons whose inclusion creates premature stop codons and triggers NMD — mapping their brain-specific and developmental splicing and 1,625 pathogenic variants that disrupt them, with CRISPR prime-editing validation in iPSC-derived neurons.
Key takeaways

Poison exons are conserved exons that, when included, introduce a premature termination codon (PTC) and trigger nonsense-mediated decay (NMD), down-tuning protein output. They are a specialized case of alternative-splicing coupled to NMD (the “AS-NMD” / poison-exon mechanism) and a recurring theme in splicing-and-disease.
A comparative analysis of 957 eukaryotic transcriptomes (Ensembl) shows Homo sapiens has the highest enrichment of NMD-targeted isoforms; 39.6% of human protein-coding genes give rise to >=1 predicted NMD isoform. The authors release the prediction pipeline as the tool evonmd.
Systematic in-silico annotation against Gencode v26 identified 9,814 putative human PEs (17.8% of 55,150 annotated AS events); 59.2% arise from cassette exons, the rest from alternative 3’/5’ splice-site usage.
Using rna-seq from GTEx and BrainSpan, they found 83 PEs uniquely detected in brain, 973 PEs differentially spliced in brain vs other tissues, and 1,284 PEs differentially spliced across brain development (before vs after birth). Brain-expressed genes are depleted (not enriched) for PEs, suggesting tight regulatory pressure.
Integrating ClinVar pathogenic variants with spliceai predictions flagged 1,625 pathogenic variants predicted to affect splicing of 743 PEs, enriched for seizures/epilepsy, intellectual disability, neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative disorders.
CRISPR prime editing in HEK293T and iPSC-derived i3N neurons functionally validated a subset; the deep-intronic NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (Leigh syndrome) variant increased PE inclusion, reduced NDUFAF6 protein, and impaired mitochondrial membrane potential.

Notable claims / data

Evolutionary scan: average 2.67% of coding genes per genome produce >=1 NMD isoform; vertebrates (3.7%) and plants (3.7%) highest, fungi (0.6%) and protists (0.1%) lowest. In humans, NMD isoforms = 20.1% of all protein-coding isoforms (14.3% under stringent TSL 1-3 filtering); 39.6% of genes have >=1 NMD isoform (30.3% under TSL 1-3). Robust to BUSCO completeness >=90% filtering — not an annotation-depth artifact.
PE definition (stringent, to minimize NMD escape): exon <=300 bp; not overlapping 5’/3’ UTR; on inclusion generates a PTC >150 bp downstream of TSS, >50 nt upstream of the last exon-exon junction, and outside the terminal exon.
CHX (cycloheximide) NMD-stabilization assay confirmed 87.5% (14/16) of putative PEs trigger NMD in HEK293T cells; same 87.5% rate confirmed in CHX-treated neurons. Non-responders (SMPD4, WDR45) reflect transcript-context dependence — downstream exon inclusion restores frame.
Disease ontology enrichment (UniProt KW): PE-containing genes enriched in epilepsy (137 genes), intellectual disability (313), neurodegeneration (181). KEGG: metabolic pathways (572 genes, FDR=0.00069), spliceosome (61), mRNA surveillance (43) — consistent with splicing factors autoregulating via PE inclusion (SRS/SF3 factors).
GTEx: 17,382 libraries, 54 tissues, 13 brain regions, 838 donors. BrainSpan: 607 libraries, 41 individuals, 16 brain regions, 10 pcw to 40 yr. PE inclusion correlated inversely with host-gene expression. Splicing factors RBFOX1, NOVA1, UPF3B enriched for brain-specific PEs (p=4e-6, hypergeometric). NOVA1 PE inclusion high in iPSCs, declines during neuronal maturation (validated by RT-PCR + CHX).
Variant pipeline: SpliceAI score cutoff |0.2| (positive = increased PE inclusion); 45,691 intronic vs 22,991 exonic candidate variants. Pangolin cross-validation showed strong concordance; SpliceAI higher sensitivity. ODiseA ontology mapping: 596 variants linked to seizures, 262 to epileptic syndromes.
Prime editing: 13 pegRNAs across 12 genes; 10/13 >5% efficiency, 9 >10% (avg 22.9%). Five representative validated variants — TAFAZZIN/TAZ c.284+110G>A (SpliceAI 0.57, +0.12 PSI), NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (0.23, +0.13 PSI), SLC12A6 c.2633-1G>A (0.99, +0.04 PSI), TSC2 c.4850-1G>A (0.99, +0.06 PSI), COQ9 c.521+1del (0.67, -0.28 PSI).
NDUFAF6 deep dive: c.420+784C>T retains 124 intronic nt, creating a PTC in exon 4. Clonal i3N neurons: WT PSI 0.13, heterozygous (C/T) 0.37, homozygous (T/T) 0.94. Homozygotes showed reduced NDUFAF6 protein (Western), impaired mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM), and smaller/fragmented cell clusters; heterozygotes intermediate. Authors state this is the first functional characterization of NDUFAF6 deficiency from PE mis-splicing in a human neuronal model.

Connections

Direct sibling to disease/mechanism sources: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (NMD-inducing cryptic exons repressed by TDP-43; this paper extends to a genome-wide PE catalog) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (AS as a regulator of gene expression).
Developmental-splicing angle complements kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the brain-proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Brain transcriptomic siblings: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia isoforms), zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (splicing-qtl in brain).
Methodologically anchored by spliceai (variant-effect prediction; Pangolin used as cross-check) and introduces the new tool evonmd.
Therapeutic framing (NMD-sensitive PEs as targetable defects via RNA-based correction, e.g. ASOs) ties to splicing-therapeutics; the SCN1A “20N” PE / Dravet syndrome and SMN2 precedent sit in this lineage (smn2).

Open questions

How many of the 9,814 putative PEs are bona fide regulatory switches vs annotation artifacts of low-abundance isoforms (the authors caution annotation depth inflates human counts)?
Short-read PSI measures only overall exon inclusion, not transcript-context PE usage — long-read RNA-seq could resolve which isoforms actually carry the PTC.
Why does the heterozygous NDUFAF6 line show intermediate PE inclusion and MMP deficit but no significant protein reduction? What sets the dosage threshold for phenotype?
Can PE-targeting ASOs that block aberrant inclusion correct deep-intronic variants like NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T in patient-derived neurons?

wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.title: quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis
wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.content:
Long-read RNA-seq demarcates cis- and trans-directed alternative RNA splicing
One-line: Introduces isolaser, a single-sample long-read RNA-seq method that calls variants, phases haplotypes, and tests allelic linkage of splicing to cleanly separate cis-directed (genetically driven) from trans-directed alternative splicing events — applied to ENCODE human/mouse data, HLA genes, and Alzheimer’s disease brains.
Key takeaways

Splicing is jointly controlled by cis-regulatory elements and trans-acting factors (mainly rna-binding-proteins); separating events that are primarily cis- vs. trans-directed in a given sample clarifies the genetic basis of disease. Long reads phase a heterozygous SNP into two haplotypes (H1/H2), and cis-directed exons show exon inclusion almost exclusively in one haplotype while trans-directed exons show similar inclusion across both.
isolaser (isoform-Level analysis of Allele-Specific processing of Exonic Regions) is a one-stop tool: (1) de novo variant calling via de Bruijn graph local reassembly + multi-layer-perceptron false-positive filter, (2) gene-level phasing via weighted k-means read clustering (haplotagging), (3) linkage test between haplotypes and exonic parts using adjusted mutual information (AMI). Crucially it needs NO pre-phased whole genome.
Works on a single sample (a major advantage for allele-specific splicing); an isoLASER-joint mode merges multiple samples to prioritize candidate functional variants (Splicing-Associated Variants, SAVs) and recover cis-events untestable in one sample (e.g. driven by homozygous variants), effectively achieving an sQTL-type analysis with very small cohorts.
Operates on exonic parts (non-overlapping exonic segments from collapsing isoforms, ~100–200 nt) rather than whole isoforms — granular, event-level resolution that is more sensitive than isoform-level allelic methods like LORALS when a gene has many combinatorial AS events.
Striking biological finding: clustering samples by AMI of splicing linkage segregates them by donor identity, not tissue of origin — even though PSI splicing profiles cluster by tissue. Genetic background shapes an individual’s overall splicing linkage; cis-directed events are individual-specific and maintained across that donor’s tissues.
Resolves allele-specific splicing in the highly polymorphic HLA system (hard with short reads), enables HLA typing from RNA, and surfaces cis-directed events in Alzheimer’s-relevant genes (MAPT, BIN1, MS4A6A, HLA-DQA1).

Notable claims / data

Variant calling: MLP classifier training AUC 0.92–0.99 (ROC) / 0.86–0.99 (PR). On HG002 long-read RNA-seq, isoLASER matched or beat GATK HaplotypeCaller, DeepVariant, and Clair3 on F1 but with superior precision.
Phasing: >99% of heterozygous variants phased by HapCUT2 or the HG002 diploid (T2T) assembly were consistently phased by isoLASER; switch-error rates 0.15% (vs HapCUT2) and 0.1% (vs HPRC). No variants phased by isoLASER only.
Cis-event definition: AMI above 99th percentile of a simulated unlinked background (coverage-dependent cutoff fit by 3rd-degree polynomial) AND |delta PSI| > 5% between haplotypes; trans = below 95th percentile; in between = ambiguous.
Scale: 2–946 cis-directed events per sample. Human tissues 2047 unique cis exonic parts / 1203 genes; mouse tissues 4679 / 2341; human cell lines 3312 / 1703; mouse cell lines 1341 / 778. ~13% of cis exonic parts are novel (not in GENCODE). F1 hybrid mice show more cis-events than F0 inbred (more heterozygosity).
isoLASER identified substantially more allele-specific-splicing genes than LORALS in both ENCODE and HG002 data; example WASHC2C has one isoLASER cis exonic part but none of its 4 transcripts showed LORALS-detectable allelic bias.
Considering all human tissues jointly, 37% of testable events were cis-directed in ≥1 tissue, 46% trans, 17% ambiguous (single-sample: 15% cis / 68% trans). 4–29% of single-sample trans events became cis at the joint level.
SAVs of cis-events had significantly lower GTEx splicing-qtl p-values than matched controls; their delta PSI correlated with the GTEx sQTL genotype beta (R = -0.37, p = 4.5e-08; inverse because GTEx/LeafCutter uses intron usage). 44 SAVs were known to alter RBP binding (allele-specific-binding study); motif enrichment near SAVs implicated HNRNP D/L/K, KHSRP, SFPQ; DeepRiPe predicted SAVs significantly alter RBP-RNA binding vs controls (KS p = 4.79e-12).
HLA: isoLASER HLA typing concordant with HG002 diploid MHC assembly and arcasHLA on 70 short-read samples. HLA-C alleles show allele-specific patterns (C04/C06 exon 5 skipping; C03 alt 3’ splice site in exon 6 ~25% usage; C07 none). AlphaFold shows cis-events reshape the protein (MHC-I C-terminus elongated by 6 aa; exon-5 exclusion deletes a transmembrane helix).
Alzheimer’s: in ENCODE PacBio DLPFC (5 AD / 4 control) plus an Oxford Nanopore frontal-cortex set (6 AD / 6 control), 7 HLA genes showed AD allele-specific splicing. SAVs in MS4A6A, BIN1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DQA1 passed the AD GWAS significance threshold (5e-8). UBC had the highest delta-delta PSI (ddPSI): an intron showed 100% retention with the T allele in AD but the linkage was reversed in controls (C allele 100% retention); the event removes one ubiquitin domain. ~15% (1201) of SAVs were rare/ultra-rare (gnomAD MAF < 1e-2 / 1e-3), a regime sQTL studies cannot address.

Connections

A long-read, single-sample counterpart to short-read allele-specific-splicing tools like pairadise (see demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing) — both detect rare-variant effects that population splicing-qtl mapping misses, but isoLASER needs no near-exon SNP on the same short read.
Benchmarked against LORALS (isoform-level allelic linkage) and against GTEx sQTLs computed with LeafCutter (see li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna); positioned as a viable small-cohort alternative to sQTL mapping (cf. zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls).
Quantifies events with percent-spliced-in (delta PSI between haplotypes) and centrally analyzes intron-retention (UBC event).
RBP-binding interpretation connects to the allele-specific-binding / rna-binding-proteins literature (DeepRiPe, RBNS); related to large RBP binding maps (see van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional).
Tool entity: isolaser. Builds on the same lab’s scAllele variant-calling framework.

Open questions

Do non-consecutive cis-directed events within one gene share a common causal variant (genetic co-regulation of splicing)?
Older Nanopore chemistries force SNP-only analysis with external validation — how much sensitivity is lost, and does newer chemistry close the gap with PacBio HiFi?
Can isoLASER-joint with much larger cohorts yield shorter, more robust SAV lists (fine-mapping toward causal variants)? Not evaluated here.
Functional validation: do the predicted HLA/UBC protein-structure changes have measurable cellular or disease consequences?

wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.title: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing
wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.content:
Regulation of pre-mRNA splicing: roles in physiology and disease, and therapeutic prospects
One-line: A review of recent advances in the molecular mechanisms that distinguish introns from exons and regulate alternative-splicing, connecting these to human disease and to the first generation of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

Intron removal is carried out by the spliceosome, comprising 5 snRNP complexes (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) plus more than 150 additional proteins, through a two-step transesterification reaction; cryo-EM has now captured snapshots of splicing at many assembly and catalytic steps. A principle of accuracy is that intron boundaries are recognized multiple times.
splice-sites in higher eukaryotes are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so recognition depends on base pairing of splicing-factors/snRNPs plus context; U1 snRNA base-pairing defines 5’ss “strength”, and BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF1/U2AF2 and U2 snRNP (SF3B1/PHF5A) defines the 3’ end.
splicing-regulatory-elements (ESE/ESS, ISE/ISS) recruited by rna-binding-proteins (SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM, CELF) tune splice-site recognition and exon definition; the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on position (the splicing-code).
Mutations in pre-mRNAs and core splicing-factors drive splicing-and-disease: genetic disorders, neurodegeneration and cancer (SF3B1, U2AF1/2, U1 snRNA mutations).
splicing-therapeutics are now clinical reality: antisense oligonucleotides (nusinersen, eteplirsen), the small molecule risdiplam, engineered U1 snRNAs, and SF3B1/RBM39-targeting drugs in oncology.
cotranscriptional-splicing is widespread but not universal; Pol II elongation rate, splicing-and-chromatin (histone marks, nucleosome positioning, CTD), nuclear compartmentalization, RNA modifications and RNA structure all shape outcomes — alongside post-transcriptional-splicing (e.g. detained introns).

Notable claims / data

Massively parallel assay of all 32,768 possible NNN/GYNNNN 5’ss sequences confirmed U1-base-pairing relevance but revealed strong context effects; ~90% of BRCA2 5’ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing. G at +1 is required for catalysis; pyrimidine at +2 present in 99.6% of 5’ss.
Spinal muscular atrophy: caused by loss of SMN1; nusinersen (approved 2016) blocks an ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion via quarterly intrathecal injection. Risdiplam (FDA approval 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon 7 bulge by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact.
Familial dysautonomia: IKBKAP intron 20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping; therapeutic strategies include ASOs, modified U1 snRNAs, kinetin, and RECTAS (enhances SRSF6 phosphorylation).
SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; K700E correlates with worse prognosis in CLL but better in MDS. SF3B1 mutations activate cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converge on repressing tumour suppressor BRD9 via a “poison exon”.
U2AF1 S34F/Y vs Q157P/R mutants change which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected. SF3B1 inhibitors (H3B-8800, in trials; pladienolide B; spliceostatin A; sudemycins) block the open→closed BP-recognition conformational change; indisulam degrades RBM39 (synthetic lethality).
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade. Eteplirsen targets ESEs to skip a mutant dystrophin exon in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, restoring an in-frame shorter functional protein. Pladienolide B reprograms mouse pluripotent cells to totipotent blastomere-like cells.
Two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion (vs constitutive exons). Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition; microexons (~3–27 nt) use SRRM4-coordinated recognition relevant to autism.
m6A regulates splicing: at 3’ss AG it inhibits U2AF1 binding (SAM-synthetase feedback in C. elegans and via METTL16 in humans); readers YTHDC1 (recruits SRSF3, antagonizes SRSF10) and hnRNPG/RBMX modulate exon inclusion.

Connections

alternative-splicing diversifies the proteome and regulates expression via PTC introduction and NMD (~30% of AS events introduce PTCs); links to splicing-and-evolution (joint evolution of splicing and protein codes; circular RNAs from back splicing).
splicing-code prediction: decades of tools from SpliceSiteFinder (1987) through SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin; saturation mutagenesis and AI methods are the path forward.
splicing-and-chromatin: MRG15 links H3K36me3 to PTB recruitment; genome-edited histone marks induce splicing changes during EMT.

Open questions

How widespread is the functional relevance of alternative-splicing versus transcriptome noise, and how much variability exists cell-to-cell?
Do introns have autonomous functions beyond being removed by-products?
How general is cotranscriptional-splicing, and do cell-type- or substrate-specific spliceosomes exist?
How can splice sites be reliably predicted from genomic sequence, and how do lncRNAs contribute to splicing regulation?

wiki/sources/scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicin.title: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease
wiki/sources/scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicin.content:
RNA mis-splicing in disease
One-line: A Nature Reviews Genetics review surveying how errors in pre-mRNA splicing — from cis-acting sequence mutations to trans-acting splicing-factor and core-spliceosome defects — cause a growing list of human diseases, and the antisense/small-molecule therapies emerging to correct them.
Key takeaways

The human genome encodes a surprisingly small set of protein-coding genes (~20,000) relative to proteomic complexity; isoform diversity is generated mainly by alternative-splicing, with >90% of human protein-coding genes producing multiple mRNA isoforms. This makes splicing highly susceptible to hereditary and somatic mutation.
Splice-site selection is intrinsically error-prone: small exons (~80% <200 bp) embedded in large introns, extensive alternative splicing, and the co-transcriptional nature of splicing (modulated by RNA Pol II elongation rate) all complicate authentic 5’/3’ splice-sites identification.
Most (>90%) disease-associated SNPs lie outside protein-coding regions, and many disease SNPs in coding genes are proposed to act by altering splicing — underscoring splicing-and-disease as a major, under-recognized mechanism.
Three mutation classes are reviewed: (1) cis-acting mutations in core consensus sequences (5’ss, 3’ss, branch point) and regulatory elements (splicing-regulatory-elements: ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS); (2) mutations in core spliceosome components (e.g. tri-snRNP proteins in retinitis pigmentosa; sf3b1/u2af1/SRSF2 in myeloid cancers); (3) mutations in trans-acting splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins.
An emerging theme: mutation-induced aggregation of splicing factors. Low-complexity / prion-like domains of hnrnpa1, TDP-43 and FUS are mutation hotspots in ALS, linking proteostasis failure to mis-splicing.
Splicing-modulating therapy is advancing: antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) and small molecules are in clinical trials for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, spinal muscular atrophy and myotonic dystrophy (splicing-therapeutics).

Notable claims / data

One LMNA gene, via different splicing mutations, causes multiple distinct diseases (Fig. 1): 5’ss mutations cause intron-9 retention → LGMD1B (c.1608+5G>C) or intron-8 retention → FPLD2 (c.1488+5G>C), both yielding PTCs and NMD; a cryptic 5’ss in exon 11 (c.1824C>T) deletes 150 nt → progerin → HGPS; an alternative 3’ss (c.640-10A>G) adds 3 amino acids → dilated cardiomyopathy. Laminopathies span >14 diseases.
Retinitis pigmentosa: six adRP genes encode U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNP components (PRPF3, PRPF4, PRPF6, PRPF8, PRPF31, SNRNP200/BRR2). PRPF31 incomplete penetrance is explained by a haploinsufficiency model modulated by the CNOT3 modifier gene; the retina expresses the highest levels of some spliceosomal snRNAs, possibly explaining tissue specificity.
Cancer: PRPF6 is overexpressed in colorectal carcinoma and drives proliferation via an oncogenic ZAK isoform; recurrent somatic mutations hit SF3B1, U2AF1, U2AF2 and SRSF2 in myeloid neoplasms (MDS, CMML). U2AF1 mutations change 3’ss recognition and mis-splice hundreds of transcripts. Antitumour drugs targeting the spliceosome exist, notably for MYC-driven cancers.
Minor spliceosome: homozygous RNU4ATAC (U4atac snRNA) mutations disrupting the 5’ stem-loop cause MOPD I (Taybi-Linder syndrome). Minor introns act as stress-responsive switches — p38 MAPK stabilizes the normally unstable U6atac snRNA (t1/2 <2 h), enhancing splicing of minor-intron genes including PTEN.
ALS: TDP-43 and FUS mutations account for ~4% and ~5% of familial ALS, mostly in prion-like domains. Mouse TDP-43(Q331K)/(M337V) mutants cause motor neuron degeneration with target-specific mis-splicing without nuclear depletion or aggregation. A normal TDP-43 function is repressing non-conserved cryptic exons (often NMD-inducing) in distal introns. ~90% of ALS is sporadic; C9orf72 GGGGCC expansions cause C9ALS/FTD.
Alzheimer disease: U1 snRNP proteins (SNRNP70/U1-70K, SNRPA/U1-A) form tangle-like cytoplasmic inclusions associating with tau, correlating with accumulation of unspliced pre-mRNAs; SNRNP70 knockdown or U1 ASO blockade raises amyloid precursor protein.
Microexons (≤51 nt), highly conserved and regulated by SRRM4 (nSR100), RBFOX and PTBP1, are misregulated in autism; RBFOX1 mutations occur in several neurological disorders.
Therapies with specifics: DMD exon-51 skipping via drisapersen (2’OMePS) and eteplirsen (PMO) to restore reading frame (DMD affects ~1 in 3,500 male newborns; gene is 2.4 Mb, 79 exons). SMA (1 in ~10,000 births) treated with MOE ASO-10-27 blocking an SMN2 intron-7 ISS to boost exon-7 inclusion (Phase III); small molecules SMNC1-3 and NVS-SM1-4 raise SMN (NVS-SM1 extended SMNΔ7 mouse lifespan from ~15 to >35 days). Myotonic dystrophy (DM1 CTGexp in DMPK 3’UTR; DM2 CCTGexp in CNBP) sequesters MBNL proteins; ASO gapmers (RNase H) and small molecules (substituted naphthyridine, pentamidine, lomofungin) displace MBNL.

Connections

Sits squarely in splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics; complements the mechanism/regulation reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative by foregrounding pathology.
Provides disease context for splicing-factor entities already in the wiki: sf3b1, u2af1, srsf1 (SMN2 exon-7 ESE), hnrnpa1 (prion-like domain ALS mutations), ptbp1 (microexon regulation) and smn2 (SMA ASO target).
The G-quadruplex angle (splicing factors binding pre-formed RNA structures; small molecules targeting them) connects to g-quadruplex and peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Its closing emphasis on machine-learning “splicing code” models (citing the Xiong et al. 2015 splicing-code study) links to splicing-code and presages tool-driven work like spliceai.

Open questions

Why is the U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNP so selectively essential for retinal (RPE/photoreceptor) function despite its ubiquitous role?
For tri-snRNP and core-spliceosome diseases, when does pathology arise from haploinsufficiency versus dominant-negative/gain-of-function aggregation?
How many ALS/neurodegeneration splicing changes are direct (TDP-43/FUS targets) versus secondary to nuclear depletion or cytoplasmic aggregation? Do other hnRNPs also repress non-conserved cryptic exons?
Do lncRNAs (e.g. MIAT/Gomafu) and circRNAs have direct, causal roles in splicing regulation and disease, or are they correlative?

wiki/sources/sharp-1994-split-genes-and-.title: sharp-1994-split-genes-and-rna-splicing
wiki/sources/sharp-1994-split-genes-and-.content:
Split Genes and RNA Splicing
One-line: Phillip Sharp’s Nobel Lecture reviewing the discovery of split genes and RNA splicing, the biochemistry and assembly cycle of the spliceosome, and the evolutionary kinship between spliceosomal and self-splicing introns.
Key takeaways

The discovery of splicing arose from comparing late adenovirus cytoplasmic mRNAs to the viral DNA, revealing that the mRNA body was assembled from non-contiguous DNA segments (Berget et al., 1977; Chow et al., 1977) — establishing the “split gene” structure and explaining the earlier hnRNA phenomenon.
Introns are pervasive in cellular genes (the average gene has ~8 introns; the primary transcript is typically 4-10x larger than the final mRNA) and bounded by a limited set of conserved consensus sequences common to vertebrate, plant, and yeast cells.
alternative-splicing lets one gene encode many proteins: ~1 in 20 genes is alternatively spliced, and the fibronectin gene alone yields ~20 distinct proteins through cell-type-specific exon inclusion/skipping.
The spliceosome is an ~60S complex assembling a substrate pre-mRNA with snRNPs (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6), splicing pre-mRNA in two transesterification steps through a lariat intermediate (2’-5’ branch at an intron adenosine).
Spliceosomal splicing chemically and evolutionarily resembles self-splicing group I and especially group II introns; the snRNAs may function as “a group II intron in pieces” forming the catalytic core.

Notable claims / data

~25% of human globin-gene mutations underlying thalassemia disrupt sequences specifying correct RNA splicing — splicing fidelity is a genetic constraint (splicing-and-disease).
~100 yeast genes (~2% of the genome) encode products important for splicing; temperature-sensitive PRP (pre-RNA-processing) mutants accumulate unspliced pre-mRNA and define specific transitions in the spliceosome cycle.
The spliceosome assembly cycle proceeds through biochemically distinguished complexes CC (commitment) → A → B1 → B2 → C1 → C2 → I, with U2 binding stabilized by the polypyrimidine-tract-binding protein U2AF (Arg-Ser-rich) and the U1-70 kDa protein.
Stereochemistry with chiral phosphorothioates (Moore & Sharp, 1993): both splicing steps are single transesterifications with inversion, and the Sp diastereomer is active in BOTH steps — implying two distinct catalytic centers (unlike group I, where the two steps are forward/reverse at a single center).
Pyruvate kinase has 9 coding-region introns in chicken but none in bacteria/yeast; intron positions are non-random relative to protein secondary structure and one is shared with alcohol dehydrogenase, supporting an exon-shuffling / ancient split-gene “progenote” hypothesis.
snRNPs concentrate in 20-50 nuclear “speckles” and in coiled bodies, are tethered to a nuclear matrix, and pre-RNA appears to be processed as it moves along curvilinear tracks toward nuclear pores (~4000 pores per mammalian nucleus).

Connections

Foundational ancestor to the entire splicing topic: it predates and underpins later mechanistic and regulatory reviews like lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
The Arg-Ser (SR) splicing factors it describes (SF2/ASF, SC35) connect to the splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins entities such as srsf1; U2AF anticipates u2af1.
The splice-sites consensus and U1/U2 base-pairing it lays out is the conceptual basis for modern splicing-code and spliceai work.
Its evolutionary argument relates to splicing-and-evolution and exon-shuffling themes echoed in wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.

Open questions

Why have introns persisted during evolution, given they drift rapidly and can be lost via cDNA-mediated gene conversion?
What is the mechanism transporting spliced mRNA from sites of transcription/splicing to nuclear pores?
How do the hypothetical PRP protein functions (RNA helicases, RNA-binding, protein-protein interaction) map onto specific spliceosome-cycle transitions?
Do U2AF and U1-70 kDa communicate directly across the intron during commitment-complex formation?

wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.title: shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection
wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.content:
rMATS: Robust and flexible detection of differential alternative splicing from replicate RNA-Seq data
One-line: The methods paper for rMATS — a hierarchical statistical model
that detects differential alternative-splicing from replicate RNA-seq
data by jointly modeling within-replicate estimation uncertainty and
between-replicate variability, and the canonical event-based ΔPSI
tool referenced throughout splicing-methods. See entity rmats.
Key takeaways

The gap it fills. First-generation tools (MISO, SpliceTrap, ALEXA-seq,
rSeqDiff) only compare two samples with no replicates; replicate-aware tools
(Cufflinks, FDM, DiffSplice, DEXSeq) fail to model the estimation
uncertainty of isoform proportion in individual replicates, and none
handle paired designs. rMATS targets both.
Lineage. rMATS = replicate MATS, the successor to MATS (Shen et al. 2012,
a Bayesian two-sample method); it replaces sampling-based p-values with a
faster likelihood-ratio test.
Hierarchical model. Two stacked layers per event:
a binomial for read-count–driven estimation uncertainty within each
replicate, and a logit-normal for variability among replicates —
estimated simultaneously. The metric is the exon inclusion level ψ
(percent-spliced-in).
Length normalization → all event types. A function f(ψ) corrects for the
effective lengths of the inclusion vs skipping isoforms, letting rMATS use both
splice-junction reads and exon-body reads and handle all five canonical AS
event types: skipped exon (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites (A5SS/A3SS),
mutually exclusive exons (MXE), and retained intron (RI).
Paired model. A bivariate-normal layer with an exon-specific correlation
ρᵢ models matched pairs (e.g. case–control / tumor–normal); it reduces to the
unpaired model when ρ=0 and boosts power for correlated pairs.
Flexible hypothesis test. A likelihood-ratio test for |Δψ| > c where the
threshold c is user-defined (e.g. 5%) — not merely the equality test
|Δψ| > 0 used by every other method. This tests for splicing changes of a
chosen magnitude.

Notable claims / data

Real-data validation (unpaired). Prostate cancer cell lines PC3E
(epithelial) vs GS689 (mesenchymal), 6 unpaired replicates, 746M paired reads →
721 differential AS events at |Δψ| > 5%, FDR ≤ 1%. RT-PCR on 34 events
validated 32/34 = 94%; RNA-seq vs RT-PCR Δψ correlation r = 0.96.
Paired model (TCGA ccRCC). 65 tumor–normal matched pairs → paired rMATS
found 315 differential exon-skipping events vs 304 for unpaired (a
superset); the 11 paired-only events are subtle-but-consistent (smaller |Δψ|
but also smaller cross-pair SD). Tumor splicing resembled the mesenchymal GS689
profile (consistent with ccRCC’s mesenchymal phenotype).
Benchmark vs JSD methods. rMATS AUC 86% > Cufflinks 83% >
DiffSplice 81%; the gap widens at low false-positive rate and with
outlier/low-coverage replicates (TPR improvement up to +19 pp). Mechanism:
rMATS down-weights low-count replicates, whereas Jensen–Shannon-divergence
methods weight all replicates equally regardless of read depth.
Experimental-design findings (a second contribution beyond the tool):

Replicates are essential; pooling/merging RNA before sequencing is
not recommended — at fixed total reads, replicate analysis always beats
pooled, and pooled data is highly sensitive to outliers.
The optimal sample-size vs sequencing-depth trade-off depends on
within-group variability: at low SD (0.01–0.02) ~3 replicates suffice; at
high SD more replicates are needed (up to +31% power). Over-splitting a
fixed budget into too many replicates reduces power by raising per-replicate
uncertainty. A low-coverage pilot to estimate variability is advised.


I/O. Inputs: raw reads + genome + transcript annotation (TopHat used for
mapping in the paper); outputs per-event P value + FDR. Source:
rnaseq-mats.sourceforge.net.

Connections

Primary source for splicing-methods — the “rMATS-style event callers”
already referenced there; the empirical, event-based counterpart to the
long-read / single-cell toolkit in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Defines the working metric percent-spliced-in (ψ/PSI, ΔPSI) used across
the splicing literature in this wiki.
Cancer splicing — its EMT (epithelial↔mesenchymal) and tumor–normal use
cases connect to splicing-and-disease.
Complements, does not overlap, the sequence-prediction line. rMATS
quantifies splicing from observed reads; splicing-code models
(spliceai, from capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the)
predict splicing from sequence — measurement vs prediction.

Open questions

Two-isoform limit. rMATS models two isoforms per region; the authors note
the binomial could be generalized to a multinomial for complex events
yielding >2 isoforms.
Bias correction. rMATS uses raw counts; prior bias-correction methods gave
no improvement in their hands, but the framework can ingest adjusted counts if
shown to help.
Beyond splicing. The isoform-ratio framework is claimed to extend to
alternative polyadenylation and RNA editing — untested here.

wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.title: shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in
wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.content:
Co-transcriptional gene regulation in eukaryotes and prokaryotes
One-line: A comparative review of how RNA processing — splicing, 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation, capping, RNA folding — is coordinated with transcription in both eukaryotes and prokaryotes, framed around recent sequencing and imaging methods that capture transient nascent-RNA intermediates.
Key takeaways

Many RNA processing steps occur during transcription. In eukaryotes cotranscriptional-splicing is the central theme; the review draws striking parallels with prokaryotic co-transcriptional translation (ribosomes trailing RNA polymerase) and shows neither coupling is universal.
Two modes of co-transcriptional splicing recur across species: rapid splicing (exon ligation soon after the 3′ splice site emerges from Pol II) and delayed splicing (exon ligation after Pol II transcribes ~2 kb of downstream intron); these mirror prokaryotic “tightly coupled” versus “runaway transcription” translation modes.
New nascent-RNA long-read sequencing methods (LRS of nascent RNA, nano-COP, POINT-nano, CoLa-seq, dNET-seq) and metabolic-labelling/imaging approaches map Pol II position relative to splicing/cleavage status, allowing inference of event timing and order.
Transcription elongation rate shapes outcomes: the “kinetic competition” model links fast Pol II to alternative-exon skipping and slow Pol II to weak-splice-site recognition/exon inclusion; elongation speed also biases alternative polyadenylation (APA) site choice.
U1 snRNP emerges as a general Pol II elongation factor (beyond its splicing role): it suppresses premature cleavage/polyadenylation (telescripting) and propels Pol II through AT-rich introns, reframing intron–exon Pol II density differences as a GC-content effect.
RNA structure regulates processing co-transcriptionally; the only known eukaryotic riboswitches act on splicing, and heat-melting intron structures (e.g. tomato HSFA2) drive stress-responsive intron retention. Relevant to rna-structure-and-splicing.
Under cellular stress, 3′-end cleavage is suppressed, causing transcriptional readthrough and downstream-of-gene (DoG) transcripts — now considered a hallmark of the mammalian stress response.

Notable claims / data

Budding yeast: spliced fraction plateaus by the time Pol II is ~130 nt downstream of an intron (single-molecule intron tracking); a fast Pol II mutant substantially delays splicing completion.
A typical mammalian gene has 8–10 introns with internal exons of ~150 nt; introns constitute roughly 90% of the transcript that must be removed.
Mouse erythroleukaemia (MEL) cells: 75% of splice-junction-to-nearest-Pol-II distances are ≤300 nt (rapid splicing). CoLa-seq in human K562: most introns spliced before Pol II transcribes 1.5 kb downstream of the 3′ splice site.
POINT-nano (human) and dNET-seq (Drosophila): both rapid splicing and delayed splicing (~2 kb downstream) detected; nano-COP detected abundant splicing at 2 kb (human) and 4 kb (fly) downstream.
Operationally, the review sets thresholds: rapid co-transcriptional splicing while Pol II is <1.5 kb into the gene, delayed while >1.5 kb, and post-transcriptional after Pol II completes the gene (Fig. 2a). Caveat: distance-based and time-based (intron half-life) estimates cannot yet be directly compared owing to variable elongation rates.
U1 snRNP binding propels Pol II elongation ~six times faster over AT-rich introns than GC-rich exons; the ~sixfold elongation-rate range in mammals is better explained by GC vs AU content than by intron–exon architecture per se (TT-seq + PRO-seq “elongation index”).
Cryo-EM captured a human Pol II–DNA–nascent-RNA–U1 snRNP complex; the U1-70K subunit contacts RPB2/RPB12, and binding does not require a 5′ splice site in the nascent RNA.
“All-or-none” pre-mRNA processing: nascent transcripts are either fully spliced+cleaved or fully unspliced+uncleaved despite high overall efficiency (e.g. β-globin ~50/50); a β-thalassaemia IVSI-110 minigene mutation that strengthens a 3′ splice site increased both splicing and cleavage efficiency, evidencing splicing–cleavage coupling.
Methodological caveats flagged: metabolic labelling with uracil analogues enriches intron-containing transcripts and can slow splicing; PCR/size bias favours detection of rapidly spliced (shorter) products; reverse transcription limits study to ~6 kb transcripts; DoGs can reach up to 200 kb.
Prokaryotic contrasts: in B. subtilis RNA polymerase moves ~2× faster than ribosomes (“runaway transcription”), decoupling transcription and translation, contradicting the E. coli textbook tight-coupling model; <20% of E. coli operons have single defined start/end sites.

Connections

Anchors and complements cotranscriptional-splicing and post-transcriptional-splicing with concrete kinetic measurements and the rapid-vs-delayed dichotomy.
Strongly relevant to splicing-and-chromatin: histone post-translational modifications and nucleosome-induced Pol II pausing near the first 5′ splice site, FISH-detected stiff chromatin loops at transcribed long genes, and nuclear-periphery localization of long introns.
Connects to rna-structure-and-splicing (riboswitches, heat-melting intron structures, DMS/SHAPE probing) and to splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (U1/U2 snRNP, SR proteins, PTB, U2AF, SF3B1, ELAV).
The spliceosome and splice-sites framing connects to mechanistic exon-definition and “first come, first served” / out-of-order splicing debates.
Among sibling sources, pairs naturally with rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of (mechanistic/regulatory reviews) and with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying (methods, including the nascent-RNA LRS this review builds on).
The sf3b1 entity is directly invoked: CDK11 phosphorylates SF3B1 (U2 snRNP branchpoint selection), tying transcript elongation to splice-site definition.

Open questions

What molecular mechanism enforces all-or-none pre-mRNA processing and coordinates splicing across multiple introns within one transcript?
Can distance-based (Pol II position) and time-based (metabolic labelling) splicing measurements be unified, and can local Pol II elongation rates be measured in absolute time units in vivo?
Does pre-mRNA processing feed back on Pol II in eukaryotes the way co-transcriptional translation feeds back on RNA polymerase in prokaryotes?
How are long introns spliced — do current LRS methods (length-biased toward short transcripts) underestimate delayed/long-intron splicing?
Does intramolecular base pairing in nascent RNA block cleavage-site recognition, and do DoG intergenic regions have intron–exon structure / undergo splicing?
How general is post-transcriptional 3′-end cleavage (observed upon DNA damage) across biological contexts?

wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.title: sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing
wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.content:
Lessons from non-canonical splicing
One-line: A review cataloguing the many “non-canonical” splicing events revealed by improved transcriptome sequencing — cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splicing, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs, chimeric/trans-spliced transcripts and atypical splice sites — their mechanisms, evolutionary role as a source of new transcripts (especially from transposable elements), their quality control, and their relevance to disease and therapy.
Key takeaways

Non-canonical splicing covers events that use cryptic splice-sites located far from annotated exons, or that deviate from the standard rules: cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splice sites, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs (circRNAs), chimeric RNAs and atypical splice sites. New RNA-seq methods + tailored aligners revealed these on a transcriptome-wide scale.
Exon definition — combinatorial recognition by U1/U2 snRNPs of the spliceosome, the U2AF complex and SR proteins bound to exonic enhancers — maintains fidelity and explains why most cryptic splice sites do not fire; many non-canonical events require unconventional exon definition.
Cryptic exons frequently arise from transposable elements (TEs), especially antisense Alu elements whose poly(U) tracts recruit splicing factors (U2AF2, TIA). They are repressed by RBPs (HNRNPC, NOVA, RBFOX2, TDP43); loss of repression can convert them into regulated alternative-splicing exons. Many introduce premature termination codons (PTCs) and are degraded by nonsense-mediated decay (NMD).
Microexons (<30 nt) are preferentially included in neurons (~60%), conserved, length usually a multiple of three (preserving the ORF), and enriched in synapse/axonogenesis genes; promoted by SRRM4/nSR100, which binds the long polypyrimidine tract upstream. SRRM4 is reduced in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), lowering microexon inclusion.
Recursive splicing removes a long intron in steps via recursive splice (RS) sites — a 3′ then reconstituted 5′ splice site — and in vertebrates requires a cryptic “RS exon” for exon definition. Found in extremely long introns of brain-expressed axon-guidance/cell-adhesion genes.
Retained introns result from inefficient splicing (intron-retention); characterized by weak splice sites, high GC content, short length; can retain mRNA in the nucleus, trigger NMD, or yield truncated proteins, and serve gene-regulatory roles (e.g., granulocyte differentiation).
Exitrons are introns within annotated protein-coding exons (cryptic introns); their removal changes protein structure or causes frameshifts rather than typically introducing PTCs.
circRNAs form by back-splicing (head-to-tail), promoted by flanking inverted Alu repeats or RBPs (QKI, MBNL); types include exonic, exon–intron and intronic circRNAs. Mostly rare, some highly tissue-specific (e.g., neuronal dendrites). CDR1-AS/ciRS-7 sponges miR-7 via ≥63 sites.
Chimeric RNAs arise from cis-splicing (read-through of adjacent genes) or trans-splicing (exons from distant loci); the same chimeras can come from physiological trans-splicing or, in cancer, from chromosomal fusions (JAZF1–SUZ12, PAX3–FOXO1).
Atypical splice sites: >99% of introns use the major U2 spliceosome; the minor U12 spliceosome has longer 5′SS/branch-point consensus; GC-5′SS are the commonest atypical U2 sites; A-to-I editing and shifted/bulged snRNA base-pairing also enable recognition.

Notable claims / data

Introns make up ~23% of the human genome; >500,000 non-annotated splicing events found in mouse/human RNA-seq.


330,000 antisense Alu elements sit in introns of protein-coding genes; TEs comprise up to two-thirds of the genome; >1.5 million degenerated LINE sequences but fewer than 100 retrotransposition-competent.


Recursive splicing: 197 RS sites in Drosophila, 11 confirmed in humans (419 putative before requiring co-transcriptional “saw-tooth” patterns) — likely underestimates.
923 exitrons identified within annotated human protein-coding exons.
~1/3 of disease-causing mutations disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (likely an underestimate; excludes synonymous exonic variants). About half of disease-linked cryptic exons derive from TEs (mainly Alu).
Disease examples: U1 snRNP repressive-site deletions activate cryptic exons in ataxia telangiectasia (ATM) and Laron syndrome; Alu U-tract deletion in PTS → hyperphenylalaninaemia; cryptic Alu exon in DMD → Duchenne muscular dystrophy; HTT triplet repeats activate a cryptic polyadenylation site → toxic exon-1 peptide in Huntington disease; TDP43 loss derepresses cryptic exons (ATG4B) in ALS-FTD; minor-spliceosome mutations cause U12-intron retention diseases.
Cancer: widespread intron retention (linked to spliceosome competition under high transcription, tumour-suppressor inactivation, SF3B1/U2AF1 mutations causing cryptic 3′SS via alternative branch points); MYC-driven tumours are vulnerable to spliceosome inhibition (apoptosis), a therapeutic angle.
Therapies: splicing-factor modulation; antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) and modified U snRNAs to redirect splicing (β-thalassaemia, SMA); bifunctional ASOs; CRISPR-Cas9 exon skipping; spliceosome-mediated RNA trans-splicing (SMaRT); engineered artificial circRNAs as sponges/aptamers/ribozymes.

Connections

Anchors the “non-canonical splicing” axis around the core spliceosome, splice-sites, alternative-splicing and intron-retention concept pages.
Quality-control/NMD and disease themes overlap with scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Co-transcriptional “saw-tooth” detection of recursive splicing connects to ameur-2011-total-rna-sequencing-reveals.
Senior author Jernej Ule; first author Sibley also led the recursive-splicing discovery (Sibley et al., Nature 2015) cited within.

Open questions

How many RS sites genuinely regulate alternative splicing, and what is recursive splicing’s physiological function (steric blocking did not reduce overall long-intron splicing)?
What are the disease roles of circRNAs and RS-site variants, still largely unexamined?
How did conserved RBPs (e.g., HNRNPC) govern the transition of TE-derived cryptic exons into regulated alternative exons across vertebrate evolution?
Genome-wide (not just exome) sequencing is needed to capture deep-intronic variants activating cryptic splicing; and how chromatin/transcription kinetics couple to non-canonical splicing control.

wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.title: sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying
wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.content:
Tools and tactics for studying alternative splicing
One-line: A methods/tools review of the modern toolkit for studying alternative-splicing — short- and long-read sequencing (bulk, single-cell, spatial), CRISPR-based functional assays, minigene/reporter assays, population/sQTL genetics, and deep-learning splicing-code models — organized around the goals of building a complete mRNA-isoform atlas, testing isoform function, and decoding the genetic control of splicing. Complements the wiki’s biology-focused sources by cataloguing how splicing is actually measured and perturbed.
Key takeaways

splicing-methods for alternative-splicing have evolved through a timeline (Fig. 2): splicing/AS discovery (1977) → RT-PCR (1989) → ESTs (1991) → exon-junction microarrays (2003) → high-throughput RNA-seq (2008) → long-read full-length sequencing (2013) and single-cell isoform RNA-seq → spatially resolved full-length mRNA sequencing (2023). Two landmark RNA-seq studies estimated 92-95% of human multi-exon genes undergo AS.
AS forms catalogued: cassette-exon inclusion/skipping, alternative 5’/3’ splice-sites (exon elongation/truncation), intron retention, plus alternative TSS and polyadenylation; these are frequently coordinated, and long-read data show coordinated/independent splicing of distant exons and that intron-removal order need not follow transcription order (cotranscriptional-splicing).
Short-read RNA-seq analysis splits into isoform-based vs count-based (exon-based and event-based) methods; event-based tools compute per cent spliced-in (PSI) and compare ΔPSI. Splice-aware aligners (STAR, HISAT2), pseudo-aligners (Salmon, Kallisto) and de novo assemblers (Trinity, Oases, SPLASH) are standard. Benchmarks show substantial tool-to-tool discrepancy → orthogonal validation needed.
Long-read platforms (PacBio SMRT/HiFi, ONT) directly capture full-length isoforms and exon connectivity, resolving complex patterns short reads cannot; ONT direct RNA-seq additionally reads native RNA + modifications (m6A) without RT/PCR.
CRISPR-based functional assays directly test isoform function: Cas9 splice-site/exon editing and pooled fitness screens, base editors for precise splice-site/enhancer disruption, and Cas13/dCasRx for RNA-level, isoform-specific, reversible perturbation and SRE mapping (splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors).
Genetic control is mapped via sQTL analysis, allele-specific expression (ASE), and massively parallel reporter assays (MPRAs); a recent GTEx v8 analysis found a substantial fraction of trait/disease variants act through splicing.
Deep-learning splicing-code models progressed from feature-based predictors to CNNs (spliceai, Pangolin) and newer multimodal/transformer/foundation models (DeltaSplice, Borzoi, DNABERT, SpliceBERT, AlphaGenome); none yet capture full cell-type/condition-specific isoform repertoires.
Central open debate: distinguishing functional isoforms from pervasive “splicing noise” — many AS isoforms are low-abundance and may be nonfunctional mis-splicing, but a subset is biologically meaningful.

Notable claims / data

Platforms (Table 2): Illumina >99.9% base accuracy, reads 50-300 bp; PacBio HiFi ~99.8% accuracy, 10-25 kb reads; ONT cDNA reads 10-20 kb avg (>1 Mb possible), ONT direct RNA ~1-1.3 kb avg (some >20 kb). PacBio/ONT continuous reads average 10-20 kb.
Library prep matters: rRNA is ~80% of total RNA; polyA selection generally preferred over rRNA depletion for splicing; UMIs remove PCR duplicates; strand-specific and paired-end improve junction calling. Typical AS depth 30-60M reads/sample; 100-200M for low-abundance isoforms or poorly annotated genomes.
Short-read limit: <300 bp reads vs ~3 kb average human mRNA → poor full-length reconstruction.
Large isoform-atlas efforts: ONT RNA-seq of GTEx samples found >70,000 novel transcripts; PacBio added to ENCODE4 to catalogue isoforms across 81 human/mouse samples (preprint). Ouro-Seq built a mouse single-cell long-read splicing atlas of >100,000 cells across 12 organs.
Single-cell: droplet 3’/5’ methods (10x Chromium) capture only transcript ends — insufficient for junctions; full-length plate methods (Smart-seq2/3, RamDA-seq, VASA-seq, FLASH-seq) enable event-level AS in single cells. Single-cell long-read methods: ScISOr-Seq2, scTaILoR-seq (>1,000 genes), HIT-scISOseq, MAS-ISO-seq, Ouro-Seq, snISOr-Seq. m6Anet quantifies m6A from ONT direct RNA.
Targeted long-read: nanopore amplicon sequencing resolved ~8,000 full-length cDNAs from four Drosophila genes incl. Dscam1 (most alternatively spliced gene known); RNA CaptureSeq enriches selected transcripts.
Spatial: MERFISH/seqFISH (imaging), barcoding methods (original 2016 spatial transcriptomics, Slide-seq, Seq-Scope, Stereo-seq, Stereo-cell); Spl-ISO-Seq did spatial long-read isoforms at 10-µm resolution (human cortex > white matter splicing regulation); Longcell pipeline handles single-cell/spatial Nanopore barcode/UMI errors.
Proteomics: most shotgun workflows use trypsin and miss splice-unique peptides; a study using six proteases + deep LC-MS found most frame-preserving AS isoforms are translated. Ribosome profiling and polyribosome long-read RNA-seq probe which isoforms are translated; nanopore protein sequencing emerging for proteoforms.
Functional-prediction tools: NEASE (pathway/PPI interpretation of AS), FAScore (ML predictor of functional exon-skipping), ORF cloning for high-throughput isoform interaction profiling (alternative isoforms share <half their binding partners). AlphaFold3 enables AS structural impact studies.
CRISPR examples: sgRNAs at MPRIP exon 23 splice sites → skipping → metastasis; targeting an RBFOX2 motif → inclusion → metastasis suppression (pancreatic cancer). Base-editor screen showed TAF5 exon 8 AS affects transcription. SpliceRUSH = high-throughput dCas13 SRE mapping; dCasRx fused to splicing factors (e.g. dCasRx-RBM25) activates exon inclusion at scale.
Reporter assays: splicing reporters tested >2 million random sequences for splice-site selection (predicts variants in SMN1, SMN2, CFTR, BRCA2); an MPRA of ~5,000 exonic disease mutations found ~10% disrupt splicing vs only 3% of common SNPs. EXSISERS quantifies endogenous protein isoforms (e.g. MAPT exon 10) via intein splicing in patient iPS cells.
sQTL/ASE tools: scNanoGPS (genotype + splicing from single-cell nanopore), LORALS (allele-specific long-read analysis). siRNA knockdown of 305 spliceosome/regulatory proteins reconstructed splicing-factor functional networks.
Splicing-code benchmark: spliceai (CNN, splice-site positions) and Pangolin (quantitative, multi-tissue) achieve highest accuracy among variant predictors, but performance is limited especially for exonic variants.

Connections

Methods companion to the wiki’s mechanism/code sources. Directly complements capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the on the splicing-code and spliceai/Pangolin (both flag exonic-variant and tissue-specific prediction as the weak spots; both name DeltaSplice, Borzoi, SpliceBERT, foundation models). This paper situates those models as the “decode” layer atop the sequencing/perturbation toolkit.
Provides the experimental backbone for the AS functionality debate: long-read isoform atlases reveal vast diversity, while CRISPR screens, ORF-interaction assays, proteomics (six-protease MS, ribosome profiling) and FAScore are the tactics for separating functional isoforms from mis-splicing noise (its closing “Do rare isoforms matter?” question).
post-transcriptional-splicing / cotranscriptional-splicing: long-read data show intron-removal order ≠ transcription order and coordinated distant-exon splicing; subcellular-fractionation long-read RNA-seq links isoforms to translational fate; ONT direct RNA couples isoform structure to m6A epitranscriptome.
splicing-and-disease: minigene/MPRA validation, sQTL/ASE genetics, CRISPR isogenic iPS-cell models (photoreceptors, cardiomyocytes); examples in NRXN1, NDUFB10, MAPT, and neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric risk via long-read neocortex isoform mapping.
splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins / splicing-regulatory-elements: SpliceRUSH and dCasRx-activator screens, the 305-factor siRNA network study, and RBFOX2 editing are the tools for dissecting regulator logic.
Modern instances of this toolkit in the wiki (long-read-rna-seq / single-cell / splicing-qtl era): fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative (longcell, single-cell/spatial AS), quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis (isolaser, cis/trans phasing → allele-specific-splicing), clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse (annotation/ancestry bias), kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of (direct RNA-seq), humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas & liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral (isoform/sQTL atlases), tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants (population e/sQTL), and the clinical venkatasubramanian-2025-splicing-regulatory-dynamics-for-precision (oncosplice).

Open questions

Long-read platforms still need higher accuracy/throughput for complete cell-type-specific isoform annotation from low-input RNA; low-abundance transcripts remain low-precision and require validation.
Need integrated computational frameworks jointly modelling transcription initiation, splicing and 3’-end formation, and spatial methods at true isoform-level single-cell/subcellular resolution.
Splicing is highly dynamic, but time-resolved sequencing and methods to identify transient/partial splicing states are lacking.
Prediction goal unmet: a model that outputs the complete isoform repertoire and relative abundances per gene in any cell type/signalling context (likely needs foundation models integrating chromatin, RBP occupancy, RNA structure, epitranscriptome).
Moving from descriptive cataloguing to interpretable, mechanistic models of splicing control via large-scale perturbation + RNA-protein/structure/nuclear-organization mapping.
The core unresolved question: how to distinguish functional isoforms from pervasive splicing noise.

wiki/sources/stark-2019-rna-sequencing-t.title: stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years
wiki/sources/stark-2019-rna-sequencing-t.content:
RNA sequencing: the teenage years
One-line: A broad Nature Reviews Genetics survey of how RNA-seq has matured over its first decade — from the baseline short-read bulk differential-gene-expression assay to long-read and direct RNA-seq, single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, and the computational pipelines behind them — providing the technological backdrop against which short-read differential-splicing/isoform analysis operates.
Key takeaways

RNA-seq’s primary, routine application remains bulk differential gene expression (DGE); the core workflow (RNA extraction → mRNA enrichment or rRNA depletion → cDNA synthesis → adaptor-ligated library → ~10-30M reads, usually Illumina → alignment/quantification/normalization/statistics) has not changed substantially since the earliest publications. Almost 100 distinct methods have been derived from the standard protocol.
Short-read cDNA sequencing (Illumina) generated >95% of published RNA-seq data on the SRA and is treated as the baseline technology, but it struggles with isoform-level resolution: reads that do not span splice junctions cannot be unambiguously assigned to a specific isoform, complicating alternative-splicing quantification (see percent-spliced-in and splicing-methods).
Long-read cDNA methods (PacBio Iso-Seq, Oxford Nanopore) and direct RNA-seq (dRNA-seq, ONT) sequence full-length molecules, removing the assembly/inference step and reducing the high false-positive splice-junction detection rate seen with short-read tools; dRNA-seq additionally retains base modifications and enables poly(A) tail-length estimation.
Over 90% of human genes are alternatively spliced into two or more expressed isoforms — the central motivation for isoform-aware library prep and analysis covered throughout the review.
The review surveys the four computational phases of DGE (alignment/assembly, quantification, filtering/normalization, statistical modelling) and the major tool families at each, several of which are also the standard substrate for differential-splicing work.

Notable claims / data

Illumina short reads account for >95% of public RNA-seq data; an Illumina run yields 10^9-10^10 reads vs 10^6-10^7 per run for PacBio/ONT — a throughput gap of roughly two orders of magnitude that currently keeps long reads from supplanting short reads for large DGE experiments.
50% of human transcripts are >2,500 bp (range 186 bp to 109 kb); PacBio Iso-Seq generated full-length reads for transcripts up to ~15 kb, synthetic long reads reconstruct contigs up to ~4 kb, and dRNA-seq read lengths are ~1,000 bp (max >10 kb).
Long-read platforms have error rates 1-2 orders of magnitude higher than mature Illumina; PacBio circular consensus sequencing (CCS) can drive errors down to short-read levels or lower at the cost of throughput.
Replication matters more than read depth/length for confidence: a 48-replicate yeast study found DGE tools detected only 20-40% of differentially expressed genes with just 3 replicates, suggesting a minimum of ~6 biological replicates (a later study argues 4 may suffice if biological variance is measured in a pilot).
Read depth: 10-30M reads/sample is the accepted range for bulk eukaryotic DGE, but as few as 1M reads can give comparable abundance estimates for the most highly expressed half of the transcriptome; 50-bp single-end is adequate for DGE but compromises isoform/splice-junction detection vs 100-bp paired-end.
The quantification step has the largest impact on final results (greater than choice of aligner); read-count tools (HTSeq, featureCounts) discard multi-mapped/ambiguous reads, while RSEM (expectation-maximization) and alignment-free tools (Kallisto) handle them differently.
Caveat: RNA degradation produces a controllable 3’ bias in short reads but cripples long-read full-length analysis; spike-in controls (ERCCs, SIRVs, Sequins) normalize better at gene level than transcript/isoform level.

Connections

Provides the sequencing-technology and pipeline context underlying splicing-focused methods in this wiki: short-read spliced aligners and junction-counting are the substrate for tools like rmats (see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); isoform/exon quantification underpins percent-spliced-in and splicing-methods.
Complements the splicing-tooling surveys sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the by situating splicing analysis within the broader RNA-seq method landscape (long-read isoform discovery, dRNA-seq, single-cell, spatial).
Relevant to mechanism/regulation reviews (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative) as the measurement layer through which alternative-splicing biology is observed.
The long-read/dRNA-seq emphasis foreshadows the move toward direct isoform-resolved splicing measurement that short-read splicing-methods only approximate.

Open questions

When and in which niches will long-read and dRNA-seq throughput improve enough (≥2 orders of magnitude) to make differential isoform/splicing analysis routine and replace short-read inference?
How much does the choice of quantification and normalization method bias downstream alternative-splicing conclusions, given that quantification dominates final-result variance?
Can direct RNA-seq’s retention of base modifications and poly(A) tail length open splicing-coupled epitranscriptomic regulation (e.g., the observation that intron-retaining transcripts carry marginally longer poly(A) tails) to systematic study?

wiki/sources/stiles-2010-the-basics-of-b.title: stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain
wiki/sources/stiles-2010-the-basics-of-b.content:
The Basics of Brain Development
One-line: A foundational review of mammalian (largely human) brain development spanning macroanatomic, cellular, and molecular levels, framing development as dynamic, adaptive, gene-environment-interactive processes within genetically constrained but constantly changing contexts — a primer that anchors the brain-development-neurogenomics topic.
Key takeaways

Human brain development is protracted: it begins in gestational week 3 (GW3) with differentiation of neural progenitor cells and extends at least through late adolescence, arguably across the lifespan. Both gene expression and environmental input are essential, but neither is “prescriptive or determinative” — the central thesis is interactionist, not deterministic.
Genes are inert templates whose only direct product is protein; proteins (not genes) are the active agents in signaling cascades. Genes do not “directly create” traits like eye color, intelligence, or behavior — there is only an indirect, multi-step relationship between gene sequence and developmental outcome.
The mature brain has >100 billion neurons making >60 trillion connections (each neuron connecting to >1,000 others). Gray matter = neuronal cell bodies (neocortex, subcortical nuclei); white matter = myelinated axon tracts. Cortical folding (gyri/sulci) is an evolutionary adaptation packing a large cortex into a small cranial vault constrained by the birth process.
Embryonic period (conception–GW8): gastrulation transforms a 2-layer into a 3-layer embryo; neuroectodermal stem cells (the neural progenitor cells) arise via molecular signaling from the primitive node. The neural tube forms E20–27 (anterior neuropore closes E25, posterior E27), then balloons into 3 primary then 5 secondary brain vesicles establishing the rostral-caudal CNS plan.
Neural patterning is “continuous specification and refinement.” Early neocortical area identity is set by opposing transcription-factor gradients — Emx2 (high posterior/medial) vs Pax6 (high anterior/lateral), later joined by Coup-TF1 and SP8 — whose combinatorial levels assign motor (M1), somatosensory (S1), and visual (V1) cortex; mouse knockouts shift area sizes accordingly.
Fetal/cellular processes: neuron production begins E42 (shift from symmetric to asymmetric progenitor division) and is largely complete by midgestation (cortical neurogenesis done ~E108). Neurons migrate radially (somal translocation, then radial-glial guides — the glia being the progenitors themselves) and tangentially (interneurons from the ganglionic eminences). Cortex builds “inside-out” in six layers; Cajal-Retzius cells secrete Reelin to signal migration stop. Transient subplate neurons scaffold thalamocortical/corticothalamic pathway formation (complete by GW26).
Regressive events are normal and essential: apoptosis eliminates ≥50% (up to ~70% in some cortical regions) of neurons prenatally, regulated by competition for neurotrophic factors (neurotrophic hypothesis). Postnatally, synaptic exuberance peaks at ~2× adult synapse density then is pruned across childhood/adolescence; transient connections (corpus callosum, thalamocortical, corticospinal) are eliminated.
Postnatal/glial and imaging: neurogenesis continues only in the subventricular zone (→ olfactory bulb) and hippocampal dentate gyrus; glial progenitors (esp. OPCs) proliferate and migrate for a protracted period, myelinating into adulthood. MRI/DTI reveal childhood-adolescent gray-matter thinning, white-matter growth, and rising fractional anisotropy (FA) as fiber tracts mature.
Experience is constitutive: Greenough’s “experience-expectant” (normal early input needed for normal organization) vs “experience-dependent” (lifelong learning) framework; enrichment/deprivation, ocular-dominance-column shifts (Hubel & Wiesel), and Sur’s rewired auditory cortex acting as a visual area all demonstrate developmental plasticity.

Notable claims / data



100 billion neurons; >60 trillion synaptic connections; each neuron connects to >1,000 others (Pakkenberg & Gundersen 1997).


Brain reaches ~90% of adult volume by age 6 and increases ~4-fold during the preschool period (Reiss et al. 1996; others).
Neuron production starts E42; cortical neurogenesis complete ~E108 (Clancy et al. 2001). Symmetric progenitor division (E25–E42) expands the pool; asymmetric division (from E42) yields one progenitor + one neuron.
Neural tube closure: anterior neuropore E25, posterior neuropore E27; embryo grows from 3–5 mm (end of neurulation) to 27–31 mm by GW8 (~tenfold).
Apoptosis affects ≥50% of neurons in many regions, reaching ~70% in some cortical regions (Rabinowicz et al. 1996); rates in the ventricular zone rise across corticogenesis.
Synapse counts plateau at nearly twice adult levels, then decline through childhood/adolescence (Bourgeois & Rakic 1993; Huttenlocher & Dabholkar 1997).
Primary sulci form GW14–26; longitudinal fissure begins ~GW8 and completes GW22; secondary sulci GW30–35; tertiary sulci from GW36 into postnatal life.
