Thalamocortical/corticothalamic pathways form in the late second trimester, complete by GW26; TC axons wait ~4 weeks on subplate neurons before connecting to cortical layer 4.
DTI: FA reaches asymptote earliest in projection, then commissural, then association fibers (association continuing into adulthood); Lebel et al. show robust FA increases ages 5–12 with wide individual variability.
Caveat (author-stated): most human postnatal timelines are extrapolated from rodents and limited human postmortem tissue, so timing and relative ordering of postnatal proliferation/migration/differentiation/regression in humans remain uncertain.

Connections

Anchors the new brain-development-neurogenomics topic hub: provides the canonical biological scaffold (neurogenesis, migration, cortical lamination, patterning, pruning, myelination) that the molecular/genomic developing-brain sources build on.
Developmental-regulation sibling: pairs with kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated alternative splicing) — this review supplies the cellular/temporal context (fetal→postnatal transitions) for those splicing programs.
Disease and regional-patterning context for parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (autism cortex): the Emx2/Pax6 arealization and experience-dependent neocortical refinement here underlie that study’s “attenuated cortical patterning” framing.
Developing-brain genomic/resource siblings: walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform, humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia/OPC-relevant), and the developing-brain proteome wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of — all profiling the molecular layer of the developmental stages this review describes anatomically.
Cell-type framing: the inhibitory interneuron origin (ganglionic eminences) and OPC/oligodendrocyte lineages described here motivate single-cell developing-brain atlases such as psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource.

Open questions

Which postnatal proliferation/migration/regression timelines, currently extrapolated from rodents, actually hold in humans? (Author-flagged uncertainty.)
What signaling pathways drive progenitor fate restriction over corticogenesis (cell-intrinsic vs host-environment cues)? Mechanisms remain “poorly defined.”
How much MRI cortical “thinning” is genuine regressive loss of neuronal processes vs intracortical myelination reclassifying gray as white? The relationship between cortical change and fiber-tract myelination is still unresolved.
What is the full functional scope of neuron-glia (OPC/oligodendrocyte/astrocyte) interactions, given evidence some OPCs form synapses and signal directly?
How do molecular/genomic developing-brain datasets map onto the specific cellular milestones (E42 neurogenesis onset, GW26 thalamocortical completion, postnatal pruning) catalogued here?

wiki/sources/su-2018-alternative-splicin.title: su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and
wiki/sources/su-2018-alternative-splicin.content:
Alternative Splicing in Neurogenesis and Brain Development
One-line: A 2018 review surveying how alternative splicing, driven by neuron-specific splicing regulators, controls each step of nervous-system development — stem-cell self-renewal, neuronal fate specification, migration, and synaptogenesis — and how splicing defects can cause brain disorders.
Key takeaways

Alternative splicing occurs at exceptionally high frequency in brain tissue and contributes to cell-fate decisions, neuronal migration, axon guidance, and synaptogenesis. ~95% of human multi-exon genes are alternatively spliced.
Splicing outcomes depend on the interplay of trans-acting splicing regulators (splicing-factors) and cis-elements, modulated by transcription rate, histone modifications, and chromatin structure (splicing-and-chromatin).
Timely, cell-type-specific expression (and post-translational modification) of neuron-specific splicing regulators is the central organizing theme: developmental “switches” of regulators reprogram the splicing landscape during fetal-to-adult transitions.
A sequential ptbp1 → PTBP2 (nPTB) switch during the progenitor-to-neuron transition is a master driver; many regulators (RBM4, Nova/Nova2, the Rbfox family, nSR100/SRRM4, srsf1, Sam68, Quaking) act on specific neuronal targets.
Alternative splicing-coupled nonsense-mediated decay (AS-NMD), including poison-exon inclusion, is used to post-transcriptionally tune levels of key neuronal mRNAs.
Splicing also reprograms epigenetic enzymes (G9a, LSD1), metabolism (PKM2→PKM1 switch), centriolar dynamics (Ninein), and synaptic adhesion (neurexins/neuroligins) during neurogenesis.

Notable claims / data

~95% of human multi-exon genes undergo alternative splicing (citing Pan et al. 2008 pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing; Wang et al. 2008 wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human).
PTBP1→PTBP2 switch: PTBP1 suppresses PTBP2 exon 10 inclusion, producing an NMD-targeted isoform, restricting PTBP2 in non-neuronal cells/NPCs; PTBP1 is itself downregulated by neuron-specific miR-124. nSR100/SRRM4 promotes PTBP2 exon 10 inclusion to maintain PTBP2 in neurons.
RBM4 acts like PTBP1 (CU-rich preference) to suppress PTBP1/PTBP2 exon 11/10 in myoblasts; during neuronal differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells, RBM4 induces skipping of mammalian-specific PTBP1 exon 9, yielding a PTBP1 isoform with compromised splicing activity (Su et al. 2017). PTBP2 lacks an exon-9 equivalent, so is resistant to this RBM4 control.
Rbfox family members autoregulate to produce a truncated-RRM dominant-negative isoform; RBFOX3 switches from truncated to full-length in a development-dependent manner, required for late neuronal differentiation.
Table 1 catalogs regulator→target effects on neuronal phenotypes: nSR100/SRRM4→Protrudin (neurite outgrowth); Ptbp1&2→PSD-95/Dlg4 exon 18 (glutamatergic neuron development); Ptbp1→Flna poison exon (brain malformation); Ptbp2→Dnm1 exons 9a/9b (seizures, behavioral deficits); Nova2→Dab1 exon 7bc/9bc (radial + Purkinje migration); Rbfox3→Numb exon 12 (differentiation); Rbfox1→Snap25 exons 5a/5b (seizure); SRSF1→ApoER2 exon 19 (synapse formation); hnRNP H1/H2→TRF2 exon 7 (differentiation); RBM4→Numb (differentiation, outgrowth).
Metabolic switch: embryonic PKM2 → adult PKM1 (mutually exclusive exons) during brain development; RBM4 antagonizes PTBP1 to promote the PKM2→PKM1 switch, raising oxygen consumption and facilitating neuronal differentiation. Quaking (Qki5) maintains neural stem cells by preventing the PKM2→PKM1 switch.
Migration: Nova2 suppresses Dab1 exon 9b/c; Nova2 knockout causes cortical and cerebellar migration defects via aberrant exon-9bc Dab1. SRSF1 inhibits ApoER2 exon 19 inclusion (reduced in Alzheimer’s brain); antisense oligonucleotides blocking SRSF1 sites show therapeutic potential.
Disease links cited but not emphasized: familial dysautonomia (IKBKAP 5′ splice-site mutation → NMD), RBFOX1 in autism spectrum disorder, Flna mis-splicing in periventricular nodular heterotopia.
Caveat: this is a narrative review (mostly mouse/cell-line mechanistic studies plus some human organoid/fetal neocortex data), not a primary study; no new dataset or quantitative meta-analysis.

Connections

Companion developmental-splicing reviews: Baralle and Giudice 2017 (baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator, cited directly) and Kalsotra & Cooper-style developmentally regulated splicing (kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated).
Mechanistic backbone overlaps the proteome-diversity framing of nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome and the human-tissue isoform landscape of wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human / pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing.
Disease/mis-splicing sibling: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (familial dysautonomia, AS-NMD themes echo here).
Brain alternative-splicing landscape sibling: park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative.
Core regulator ptbp1; also touches srsf1 (ApoER2) and Drosophila dscam-style cell-specific wiring (here exemplified by Dscam2).

Open questions

How exactly do the four Numb isoforms (exons 3/9) differentially modulate Notch to set NPC fate — the mechanism remains incompletely known.
Are splice isoforms of synthetic/metabolic enzymes (e.g., PKMs) coordinately switched in response to dynamic neuroenergetic demands (glutamatergic stimulation, hypoxia)?
Can single-cell and in situ / spatial sequencing resolve alternative splicing at the single-neuron level across complex brain networks, and reveal pathological splicing programs cell-by-cell?
Whether RBM4 promotion of Nova-1 exon 4 inclusion (shown in brown adipocytes) also operates in neurons is unresolved.

wiki/sources/szabo-2016-detecting-circul.title: szabo-2016-detecting-circular-rnas-bioinformatic
wiki/sources/szabo-2016-detecting-circul.content:
Detecting circular RNAs: bioinformatic and experimental challenges
One-line: A review of why genome-wide detection of circular RNAs (circRNAs) from RNA-seq is hard, cataloguing the experimental and bioinformatic biases that produce false positives and the statistical approaches needed to control them.
Key takeaways

circRNAs arise from back-splicing, a covalent linkage between a downstream 5’ splice site and an upstream 3’ splice site (the reverse arrangement of canonical linear splicing), and lack poly(A) tails and 5’ caps. Their pervasive expression was only discovered in 2012, having been masked by poly(A) selection in standard rna-seq library prep and by bioinformatic filters in mainstream splice-detection algorithms.
circRNAs are an ancient, broadly conserved feature: expressed across the eukaryotic tree (humans to flies/worms, plus yeast, plants, amoeba, Plasmodium). Most are non-coding; only CDR1as and SRY have a known function (microRNA sponges).
Accurate detection of even linear spliced alignments is an unsolved problem (algorithms disagree on recall/FDR even with a near-identical reference genome), and circRNA detection inherits all of these difficulties plus its own.
Many circRNA-specific tools exist (MapSplice, find_circ, segemehl, CIRI, circExplorer, circRNA_finder, KNIFE, DCC, etc.), but they show little overlap in predictions — up to 40% of circRNAs are reported by only a single algorithm — and there is no gold standard for assessing genome-wide sensitivity/specificity.
The authors argue that read count is a poor predictor of whether a junction (linear or circular) is truly expressed, and push for statistical scoring (FDR estimation) over hard read-count thresholds.

Notable claims / data

circRNA abundance is roughly 2–4% of total mRNA in cells (~1–3% of mRNA level in common cell lines), higher in some cell types such as platelets; sizes range from <100 nt to >4 kb, most commonly a few hundred nt, usually 2–3 exons in humans.
Experimental biases: poly(A) selection depletes circRNAs; rRNA depletion retains them; RNase R (a 3’→5’ exonuclease) digests linear RNA and enriches circRNAs; oligo(dT) priming biases against circRNAs while random priming does not.
Artefacts mimicking back-splices: reverse-transcriptase template switching can account for 34–55% of detected isoforms for some genes (worse for genes with shared homologous exons); ligation can generate chimeric cDNAs; RT strand displacement / rolling-circle amplification inflates abundance estimates for small circles. Filtering out low-read circRNAs does not eliminate these.
Benchmarks found 31–76% of circRNAs detected in an rRNA– library were not re-detected in enrichment libraries, and 12–28% were depleted by RNase R (i.e. false positives). Algorithms with highest specificity had lowest sensitivity and vice versa.
RNase R validation is itself unreliable: validated circRNAs (CDR1as, NCX1, a CAMSAP1 intron-retention isoform, MAN1A2, Drosophila Pangolin/Ank2) are RNase R–sensitive, and <50% of circRNAs resistant in one replicate are resistant in a second from the same lab.
Statistical point: the 95% CI for the RNase R+/RNase R– read ratio is [0.75–1.33] at 100 reads but [0.23–4.34] at 5 reads — so naive fold-change comparisons without confidence intervals and depth normalization give discrepant conclusions. The authors recommend the circular-to-linear splicing ratio (normalization-free) and consider depletion in poly(A)+ libraries a better false-positive proxy than failure to enrich under RNase R.
A statistical approach relaxing the canonical U2 requirement uncovered the first circRNAs spliced by the minor U12 spliceosome (e.g. RANBP17, which produces three distinct circular isoforms from one locus). Lariats (5’–2’ linkage) are a confounding RNase R–resistant species but constitute <0.17% of reported circRNAs.
Conserved, algorithm-robust observations: ubiquity of circRNA expression, no correlation between linear and circular levels from the same gene, enrichment of longer flanking introns, and no global enrichment of microRNA-binding sites.

Connections

A methods/bias review squarely in the splicing-methods and data-quality-and-bias space; complements rna-seq and contrasts with the linear-isoform reconstruction problem also relevant to long-read-rna-seq and heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform.
Frames circRNAs as a class of alternative-splicing output that escaped detection, extending the alternative-splicing landscape surveyed in park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Mechanistically depends on splice-sites (canonical U2 GU–AG vs minor U12 signals) and the spliceosome; the U12-spliced circRNA finding ties circRNA biogenesis to minor-spliceosome usage.

Open questions

Can a genome-wide gold standard (e.g. in-vitro-transcribed circRNA ground-truth libraries, plus simulators that mix annotated/unannotated circular and linear isoforms) be built to benchmark sensitivity vs specificity?
What statistical normalization is appropriate for matched RNase R+/– and poly(A)+/– libraries so enrichment/depletion inferences are valid?
Do circRNAs have genuine biological function, and is genome-wide conservation real or an artefact of confounders such as Simpson’s paradox?
Are there yet other overlooked classes of linear/circular RNA isoforms — motivating reference-free approaches to quantify the full RNA repertoire of a cell?

wiki/sources/tau-2010-normal-development.title: tau-2010-normal-development-of-brain-circuits
wiki/sources/tau-2010-normal-development.content:
Normal Development of Brain Circuits
One-line: A wide-ranging developmental-neuroscience review that traces how human neural circuits are built, refined, and pruned from the first synapse through adolescence, integrating histology, cellular/molecular biology, and neuroimaging to provide a “normal” baseline against which the developmental origins of neuropsychiatric illness can be read.
Key takeaways

Frames brain function as residing in neural circuits — from local microcircuits (clusters of interconnected neurons) to distributed networks of brain regions — that are neither present at birth nor fixed through life, but are sculpted by genetic programs interacting with environment and experience along a largely predictable timeline. Relevant to the brain-development-neurogenomics hub as a circuit-level scaffold for cell-type and disease-genetics work.
Lays out the earliest events: neural-tube formation at gestational age (GA) weeks 2–3; three then five vesicles forming forebrain/midbrain/hindbrain by week 4; rapid neuroblast proliferation in the ventricular zone (germinal matrix) by GA weeks 5–6; differentiation into neurons vs macroglia by GA week 8.
Describes radial (inside–out) migration along radial glia (deeper layers first), versus tangential migration of GABAergic interneurons from the ganglionic eminences; radial glia can themselves give rise to neurons. Migration peaks GA weeks 12–20, largely complete by 26–29.
Tracks transient embryonic scaffolds — preplate (first synapses ~GA week 5, “pioneer neurons”), subplate (up to 5× thicker than cortical plate at GA weeks 18–22, a placeholder synaptic hub for thalamocortical afferents that later dissolves), marginal zone, and the cortical plate (generates laminae II–VI; marginal zone forms layer I).
Details the canonical six-layer cortical microcircuit (best defined in visual cortex): layer IV receives thalamic input; layers II/III make cortico-cortical projections; layers V/VI project subcortically (V to brainstem/midbrain/basal ganglia, VI to thalamus); inhibitory interneurons gate the excitatory flow. Cells organize into radial ontogenetic columns/minicolumns; clonally related pyramidal neurons preferentially wire to one another.
Emphasizes the progressive vs regressive balance: exuberant synaptogenesis (peaking ~GA week 34 at ~40,000 new synapses/second) counterbalanced by two apoptotic waves (proliferative cells ~GA week 7; postmitotic cortical-plate neurons GA weeks 19–23) and later activity-dependent synaptic/axonal pruning. Up to ~half of neurons born during neurogenesis die by the end of adolescence.
Covers postnatal trajectories via neuroimaging: brain reaches ~70% adult size by age 1 and ~80% by age 2; region-specific synaptic-density peaks (visual cortex 140–150% of adult levels at 4–12 months; prefrontal cortex peaks later, ~8 months onward); myelination proceeds posterior-to-anterior and subcortical-before-cortical; cortical gray matter later thins “back-to-front,” interpreted as combined pruning + myelination and treated as a marker of maturation.
Connects circuit maturation to executive function development (working memory in dorsolateral PFC / frontoparietal network; self-regulation/cognitive control in frontostriatal circuits) and to pediatric psychiatric disorders, illustrating the “normal baseline” frame with autism, Fragile X, childhood-onset schizophrenia, ADHD, OCD, and Tourette’s.

Notable claims / data

Synaptic density within the cortical plate grows ~4% per week until GA weeks 26–28; peak synaptogenesis at GA week 34 produces ~40,000 new synapses per second, continuing into early postnatal life.
Neuron number peaks at GA week 28, yet as many as half of neurons produced during neurogenesis are eliminated by apoptosis by the end of adolescence; two discrete apoptotic waves (GA week 7 onward; peak GA weeks 19–23).
Brain volume: ~36% of adult size at 2–4 weeks postnatal; ~70% by year 1, ~80% by year 2; cortical volume rises ~88% in year 1, ~15% in year 2. Total brain volume increases ~3-fold between GA weeks 29 and 41; gray matter goes from 35% to 50% of total in that window; myelinated white matter rises from 1% to 5% of brain volume between GA weeks 36–40.
Visual-cortex synaptic density peaks at 140–150% of adult levels between 4 and 12 months; PFC synaptogenesis peaks ~8 months, later than sensory cortex.
Brain glucose metabolism (PET) reaches ~2× adult levels by 4–5 years, stays high to 9–10 years, then declines to adult levels by 16–18 years; ~40% of synapses are eliminated during adolescence via activity-dependent removal of excitatory connections, while GABAergic interneuron levels stay fairly stable.
Critical-period claims (caveat: largely from sensory systems / animal models): uncorrected congenital cataracts by 6 months or strabismus by 7 years cause irreversible visual deficits; binocular-vision column formation depends on LTP/LTD/pruning driven by retinal activity (Hebbian).
Disease vignettes tie genes/exposures to circuit defects: lissencephaly (reelin-pathway, cytoskeletal, glycosyltransferase mutations); L1CAM mutation → CRASH syndrome and alcohol-sensitive cell-adhesion (fetal alcohol syndrome); Fmr1 silencing → LTP/LTD imbalance in Fragile X; childhood-onset schizophrenia shows accelerated/excessive cortical thinning correlating with symptom severity; ADHD shows delayed cortical maturation.
Major stated caveat: human knowledge is “highly imperfect,” extrapolated largely from cross-sectional rodent data; most pediatric imaging makes developmental inferences from cross-sectional samples; histological correlates of many imaging measures remain undefined. Calls for multimodal longitudinal imaging plus postmortem molecular study of the same tissue, and nonhuman-primate longitudinal work.

Connections

Provides the circuit-/imaging-level developmental backbone for the brain-development-neurogenomics hub; complements the molecular and cell-type-resolved developing-brain siblings such as parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (autism cortex transcriptome, neuronal exon exclusion, excitation/inhibition imbalance — the molecular counterpart to this review’s pruning/interneuron themes) and the psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource developmental atlas.
The review’s emphasis on developmentally timed, region-specific synaptic and splicing-factor activity links naturally to splicing-and-neurodevelopment (e.g. neuron-specific exon programs, Rbfox/PTBP1-type switches referenced in sibling sources).
A purely review-level, human-and-animal synthesis with no new dataset/tool — best read as orienting context alongside the data-generating sources, not as a primary resource.

Open questions

What are the precise cellular/molecular determinants of macroscopic imaging signals (cortical thinning, DTI/MRS changes), which the review repeatedly flags as “speculative”?
How do critical/sensitive periods for behavioral traits and psychopathology map onto the well-characterized sensory/motor critical periods, which remain comparatively undefined?
Can multimodal longitudinal imaging combined with postmortem molecular profiling of the same tissue (and longitudinal nonhuman-primate studies) replace hazardous cross-sectional rodent extrapolation for human circuit development?
What drives the large excess production of neurons/glia followed by apoptosis, and how does this regressive program interface with the cell-type-specific genetic programs studied elsewhere in this topic?

wiki/sources/tokolyi-2025-the-contributi.title: tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants
wiki/sources/tokolyi-2025-the-contributi.content:
The contribution of genetic determinants of blood gene expression and splicing to molecular phenotypes and health outcomes
One-line: A population-scale (n = 4,732) blood RNA-seq study from the INTERVAL cohort that maps cis/trans eQTLs and splicing QTLs (sQTLs), then integrates them with plasma protein, metabolite and lipid QTLs and disease GWAS via colocalization and mediation — repeatedly showing that splicing (especially excision of transmembrane-domain exons) is an eQTL-independent mechanism linking genetic variation to circulating protein levels and disease risk; delivered as the open-access intervalrna-portal.
Key takeaways

Mapped both expression and splicing QTLs in the same 4,732 blood donors: cis-eQTLs for 17,233 genes (89.9% of tested) and cis-sQTLs for 29,514 splicing events in 6,853 genes (cis-sGenes), establishing a large multi-omic splicing-qtl resource (see rna-seq).
Splicing events were quantified de novo / annotation-free from intron-usage ratios using the leafcutter pipeline (on Regtools-extracted junctions), not from a fixed transcript annotation — “splicing events” = differential intron usage ratios. This recovered 89.0% of GTEx whole-blood sGenes plus 4,470 new sGenes, attributed to the larger sample size and de novo excision quantification.
sQTLs carry information distinct from eQTLs: 82% of eQTL-colocalizing splicing events with multiple independent sQTL signals had additional sQTL loci that did NOT colocalize with the gene’s eQTL — i.e. independent regulatory layers (alternative splicing as a mechanism separate from total transcript abundance).
Transmembrane-domain excision is a recurring mechanism: ~98 splicing events colocalizing with pQTLs excised exons encoding transmembrane domains, generating soluble protein isoforms; many of these pQTLs did NOT colocalize with an eQTL, so the sQTL is the pivotal mechanistic clue (IL-6R, IL-7R, IL-17RA, FAS, SERPINA1, APOL1, IFNAR2, CD33).
Disease relevance demonstrated by multitrait colocalization + mediation across FinnGen (20 traits) and COVID-19 HGI (4 traits): WARS1 sQTL ↔ hypertension, IL7R sQTL ↔ dermatitis/eczema, OAS1 and IFNAR2 sQTLs ↔ COVID-19 severity (see splicing-and-disease).
The whole compendium of molecular QTLs + colocalization + mediation is released as the open-access intervalrna-portal (IntervalRNA.org.uk).

Notable claims / data

Discovery: 19,173 expressed autosomal genes and 111,937 de novo splicing phenotypes quantified in 11,016 genes; cis-eQTL window ±1 Mb of TSS, cis-sQTL window ±500 kb of the spliced-region center. 7,877,942 imputed variants (MAF > 0.005). FDR < 0.05.
Conditional/stepwise analysis: 56,959 independent cis-eQTL signals (median 3/gene, range 1–23) and 47,050 independent cis-sQTL signals (median 1 per cis-sQTL, range 1–20). Nonprimary signals existed for 81% of cis-eGenes and 49% of cis-sGenes.
sQTL architecture: cis-sQTL primary signals enriched in the gene body and toward the transcription end site (median 17.36 kb downstream of TSS) vs cis-eQTLs (median 5.51 kb), P = 8.42 × 10⁻²⁵⁹. Splicing events had median length 1,549 bp and excised protein-coding sequence in 32.4% of cases (rest intronic/UTR). Lead variants formed a bimodal distribution around the start/end of the sGene; median 187 bp upstream of the splicing-event center.
eQTL–sQTL colocalization: 3,979 of 6,252 genes had colocalized eQTL/sQTL signals; 49.0% (13,490) of tested splicing events colocalized with an eQTL for the same gene. Non-colocalizing sQTLs lay further downstream of the TSS (median 20.33 kb) than colocalizing ones (12.61 kb), P = 9.8 × 10⁻⁷⁰.
Trans analysis (using 53,457 conditionally independent lead cis-eSNPs): 2,058 trans-eGenes (P < 5 × 10⁻¹¹) and 644 trans-regulated splicing events / 209 trans-sGenes (P < 8.36 × 10⁻¹²). 240 splicing events (91 genes) were regulated only in trans. The RNA-binding splice factor QKI cis-eQTL trans-regulated 18 sGenes (most of any eGene); replicated GTEx trans-sQTLs for FYB1 and ABHD3. Cis-eGenes with trans-associations enriched for sequence-specific transcription factors (357/2,498, P = 1.83 × 10⁻³⁸) and C2H2 zinc-finger / KRAB domains.
Cross-omics colocalization (INTERVAL pQTLs/mQTLs): a cis-eQTL or cis-sQTL colocalized with cis-QTLs for 120 Olink proteins (65.9%), 404 SomaScan proteins (63.7%), 224 Nightingale metabolites (99.1%) and 495 Metabolon metabolites (81.5%); 3,430 molecular traits total. Extending to external deCODE (n = 35,559) and UKB-PPP (n = 54,219) pQTLs raised colocalizing Olink proteins from 120→1,203 and SomaScan from 404→984; replicated 95.1%/79.3% of eQTL/sQTL colocalizations in UKB-PPP and 87.0%/80.3% in deCODE.
Mediation: 143 cis-eGenes mediated 413 cis-eSNPs onto 202 molecular phenotypes (525 eQTL mediation triplets); 106 splicing events in 47 sGenes mediated 152 cis-sSNPs onto 50 molecular phenotypes (241 sQTL models). Net 222 molecular phenotypes significantly mediated by expression or splicing.
Worked disease examples (direction stated): IL17RA transmembrane-domain sQTL mediated 90.41% of the sSNP effect on plasma protein (P = 1.14 × 10⁻⁴³), no missense tag SNP; IL6R sQTL mediated only 4.67% (lead in r² > 0.99 with the rs2228145 ADAM-cleavage missense variant) — soluble isoform via both splicing and proteolysis. rs6897932(T): less IL7R exon-6 excision → lower plasma IL-7R → reduced dermatitis/eczema risk (coloc H4 100%/100%/94%). rs724391(C): less WARS1 exon-10 excision → higher plasma WARS1 → increased hypertension risk (H4 100%/100%/95%). OAS1: eQTL + seven sQTLs colocalize with COVID-19 (adjusting for OAS1 expression did not ablate sQTL, P < 1 × 10⁻¹⁶). IFNAR2: eQTL + 10 sQTLs (incl. exon 8/9 transmembrane excision) colocalize with COVID-19; eQTLs also trans-sQTLs for 5 IFI27 splicing events.
mQTLs colocalized with eQTL/sQTL very often (96%) but were mediated less than pQTLs (6.8% vs 32.6%), attributed to metabolite correlation structure.
Methods stack: STAR 2-pass alignment (GRCh38, Ensembl v99), featureCounts, Regtools junction extraction, LeafCutter clustering, tensorQTL for QTL calling, 35 PEER factors (eQTL) / 10 splicing PCs (sQTL), genotypes imputed with 1000G Phase 3 + UK10K.
Caveats stated by authors: single annotation-free splicing definition (other splicing aspects missed); limited trans power (prioritized cis-eSNPs); plasma (not intracellular) proteins, so effects conflate quantity and solubility; metabolite correlation structure; cohort is European ancestry only.

Connections

A multi-omic, disease-oriented sibling to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain-region sQTL atlas) and park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative — extends the splicing-qtl line from tissue/region cataloguing toward variant-to-protein-to-disease mechanism via colocalization + mediation.
Splicing quantification rests on leafcutter (li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna), the same annotation-free intron-ratio approach; contrast with PSI/event-based tools like rmats used in zhang-2020.
Mechanistic core (transmembrane-domain excision → soluble isoforms; QKI as a trans splice-factor) ties to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins; the disease links sit in splicing-and-disease.
Complements protein/binding resources such as van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional by supplying the population-genetics layer (which variants change splicing in vivo and what they do to circulating proteins and disease).
Primary deliverable is the intervalrna-portal resource.

Open questions

Would single-cell / cell-type-resolved population QTL mapping (which the authors flag as the next step) reassign bulk-blood sQTL signals to specific immune cell types and sharpen mediation estimates?
How transferable are these European-ancestry sQTL→protein→disease links to other ancestries?
For loci where sQTLs but not eQTLs colocalize with pQTLs (e.g. IL-6R, IL-7R), how generally does transmembrane-domain splicing govern the soluble vs membrane-bound protein balance across the secretome?
Can large meta-analyzed trans-QTL resources (e.g. eQTLGen phase II) confirm the QKI-class trans-splicing hubs that bulk RNA-seq power could only partially resolve?

wiki/sources/trapnell-2010-transcript-as.title: trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification
wiki/sources/trapnell-2010-transcript-as.content:
Transcript assembly and quantification by RNA-Seq reveals unannotated transcripts and isoform switching during cell differentiation
One-line: Introduces cufflinks, an annotation-free algorithm that assembles full transcripts from paired-end RNA-Seq and probabilistically quantifies isoform abundances, enabling discovery of novel transcripts and detection of isoform/TSS switching during differentiation.
Key takeaways

RNA-Seq promises simultaneous transcript discovery and abundance estimation, but the analysis is hard because reads cannot be assigned unambiguously to isoforms. Cufflinks solves both jointly without relying on prior gene annotations, accounting for alternative transcription and splicing.
Assembly: reduces the transcript-assembly problem to finding a minimum path cover of an “overlap graph” of compatible fragments, via a constructive proof of Dilworth’s Theorem (the number of mutually incompatible reads equals the minimum number of transcripts needed to explain all fragments). The assembler does not require an open reading frame, allowing recovery of noncoding isoforms (e.g., NMD-targeted forms).
Quantification: a generative statistical model where the probability of observing each fragment is a linear function of transcript abundances; it incorporates the fragment-length distribution to disambiguate which isoform a paired-end fragment came from. Reports abundances in FPKM (fragments per kilobase per million mapped), extending the unpaired Jiang–Wong model to the paired-end case. Confidence intervals via Bayesian importance sampling; reports the MAP estimate.
Demonstrated on a C2C12 mouse myoblast differentiation time series (>430 million paired 75-bp reads), detecting both known and novel transcripts and revealing widespread isoform/promoter switching during myogenesis.
Coupling transcript discovery WITH abundance estimation matters: including novel isoforms strongly changed abundance estimates of known isoforms in many genes.

Notable claims / data

Sequenced >430 million paired 75-bp reads. Of 215 million fragments, 171 million (79%) mapped to the mouse genome; 46 million spanned ≥1 putative splice junction; 70% of spanned junctions were in known annotations (UCSC/Ensembl/VEGA).
Recovered 13,692 known isoforms and 12,712 new isoforms of known genes; estimated 77% of reads came from previously known transcripts. Of new isoforms, 7,395 (58%) contain novel splice junctions; 11,712 (92%) have an ORF (8,752 ending at an annotated stop codon).
3,724 novel isoforms present in multiple time points (the conservative high-confidence novel set); 581 at all time points. 62% of multiple-time-point novel isoforms supported by independent EST/RefSeq evidence or homologous genes in other species (expected FDR <5%).
High-confidence quantification set: 11,079 genes, 17,416 isoforms (13,692 = 79% known; 3,724 = 21% novel).
Accuracy: spike-in controls R²=0.99; simulated data (FluxSimulator) R²=0.96; excluding novel isoforms degraded known-isoform estimates (R²=0.90), showing transcript discovery and quantification must be coupled.
Dynamics: 330 genes showed complete switches in dominant TSS or splice isoform; subtler shifts in 1,304 others. Information-theoretic test (square root of Jensen–Shannon divergence, points on a logarithmic spiral in Hilbert space) found significant (FDR<5%) differential transcription in 882/3,486 (25%) and differential splicing in 273/843 (32%) candidate genes; 70 genes both. 10% of multi-promoter genes switched major primary transcript; 7% of alternatively spliced primary transcripts switched major isoform.
Robustness: ~75% of moderately expressed transcripts (≥15 FPKM) recovered with <40 million 75-bp paired-end reads; at 40M reads, moderately expressed transcripts estimated within 15% of final FPKM.
Case studies: Myc (transcriptional → post-transcriptional control shift between 60–90 h); FHL3 (novel proliferation-dominant TSS validated by TAF1 and RNA Pol II ChIP-Seq); tropomyosin I/II isoform switching. 153/185 putative novel TSSs validated against ChIP-Seq.
Caveat: novel single-time-point isoforms conservatively excluded (hard to distinguish full-length transcripts from partial assemblies); trajectory classification too coarse for per-gene prioritization or significance testing, motivating the JS-divergence metric. Genes with novel isoforms excluded from coding-sequence analysis, likely underestimating the impact on coding potential.

Connections

Pipeline companion to TopHat (Trapnell, Pachter, Salzberg 2009) for spliced read mapping; ships cufflinks plus Cuffcompare and Cuffdiff utilities. New tool entity: cufflinks.
A foundational splicing-methods paper for isoform-level RNA-Seq quantification; conceptual ancestor of later differential-splicing tools such as rmats (see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).
The assembler explicitly weights its reachability graph using the percent-spliced-in metric (see percent-spliced-in), borrowed from prior tissue-transcriptome work, to phase distant exons.
Relevant to broader tooling surveys such as sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Open questions

How well does the parsimony (minimum-path-cover) assumption hold for genes with many lowly expressed or rare isoforms, where the minimal explanatory set may omit real transcripts?
Cufflinks here is annotation-free assembly on a single model system; how do error rates change across organisms, deeper alternative-splicing complexity, or short-read-only (unpaired) data?
The JS-divergence/Hilbert-space metric formalizes differential splicing vs. promoter use — how does it compare in power to later event-based PSI methods?

wiki/sources/van-nostrand-2020-a-large-s.title: van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional
wiki/sources/van-nostrand-2020-a-large-s.content:
A large-scale binding and functional map of human RNA-binding proteins
One-line: The ENCODE Phase III RBP resource — an integrative atlas of binding, in vitro specificity, function, chromatin association, and subcellular localization for 356 human RNA-binding proteins in K562 and HepG2 cells, producing 1,223 replicated datasets.
Key takeaways

Systematically characterizes 356 human rna-binding-proteins using five assays (eCLIP, RNA Bind-N-Seq, knockdown RNA-seq, immunofluorescence, ChIP–seq), producing 1,223 replicated datasets — the largest such effort to date at the time.
98 RBPs (28%) function in alternative-splicing regulation; 162 (46%) have more than one reported RNA function; 83 (23%) have no verified mechanistic RNA function in humans; 57% contain well-characterized RNA-binding domains (RRM, KH, zinc finger, helicase, etc.).
eCLIP (enhanced CLIP) of 150 RBPs (223 datasets) identified 844,854 enriched peaks covering 18.5% of the mRNA transcriptome and 2.6% of the pre-mRNA transcriptome, using stringent IP-versus-input enrichment and IDR reproducibility.
In vitro RNA Bind-N-Seq specificities (78 RBPs) are highly correlated with in vivo binding; eCLIP peaks that contain the high-affinity in vitro motif (RBNS+) confer stronger splicing regulation than peaks lacking it.
Per-RBP “RNA splicing maps” confirm classical antagonism: SR proteins generally repress and hnRNP proteins generally promote cassette-exon inclusion; relevant to ptbp1 and RBFOX2 maps.
Introduces the named encode-rbp-map resource (data at the ENCODE DCC and the RBP Image Database).

Notable claims / data

488 eCLIP experiments performed → 223 high-quality datasets for 150 RBPs (120 in K562, 103 in HepG2, 73 in both). Peaks required fold enrichment ≥8 and P ≤ 0.001, reproducible by IDR, with 57 blacklist regions removed.
Knockdown RNA-seq: 472 KD–RNA-seq profiles for 263 RBPs (235 in K562, 237 in HepG2). 375,873 differential-expression instances (20,542 genes affected by ≥1 RBP KD); 221,612 differential-splicing cases (38,555 alternatively spliced events). AQR knockdown alone drove >13,000 splicing changes (threefold more than any other RBP).
Only 5,214 genes had eCLIP peaks AND were knockdown-responsive for the same RBP → much of expression response is indirect.
RBNS (78 RBPs): single clusterable 5mer motif for 37/78; two motifs for 32/78; three or more for 9. For repressed cassette exons, RBNS+ exon-proximal peaks gave ~25% larger change in exon inclusion than RBNS– peaks.
Cross-cell-type: RBFOX2 HepG2 peaks averaged 6.2-fold enrichment in K562 when target expression was within fivefold; peak discrepancies mostly reflect cell-type-specific RNA expression, not differential binding.
Splicing-map finding: the upstream 5′ splice site of cassette exons shows greater RBP enrichment than the directly flanking introns — “an underappreciated region for splicing regulation.” QKI co-regulates RBFOX2-excluded exons (R² = 0.19, P = 1.2×10⁻⁵).
ChIP–seq of 37–58 nuclear RBPs: 792,007 peaks covering 3.8% of genome; RBPs occupy ~30% of open-chromatin regions and ~70% of gene promoters; ChIP–eCLIP target overlap is low (avg 6% of eCLIP, 2.4% of ChIP peaks) except specific pairs (e.g., HNRNPK–PCBP1/2, RBFOX2).
Immunofluorescence of 274 RBPs (HepG2) / 268 (HeLa): 217,412 images; speckle-localized RBPs affect more splicing events; mitochondrial-localized RBPs (e.g., DHX30, GRSF1, FASTKD2) enriched on mtRNAs; DHX30 binds an unannotated H-strand stem–loop possibly marking a transcription termination signal.
Caveat: eCLIP did not include visualization of protein-associated RNA, so orthogonal validation (in vitro motifs, KD response) is essential; GC-content biases in KD–RNA-seq required Salmon + CQN normalization.

Connections

This is the foundational ENCODE RBP resource underlying many splicing-factor analyses; complements mechanistic reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Its splicing-map methodology and SR/hnRNP antagonism connect to fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing and to factor pages ptbp1, srsf1, hnrnpa1, u2af1.
The RBNS in-vitro-specificity component derives from Dominguez et al. 2018 (Mol. Cell); the eCLIP method from Van Nostrand et al. 2016 (Nat. Methods).
Provides nucleotide-resolution candidate binding sites useful for predicting variants that alter RNA processing — links conceptually to splice-variant prediction tools like spliceai.

Open questions

Why do RBNS-motif (longer-dwell) peaks confer stronger repression than activation of splicing?
What is the mechanism and topology behind HNRNPK/PCBP overlap of ChIP and eCLIP within gene bodies?
Does the DHX30-bound H-strand stem–loop genuinely mark the unknown mitochondrial H-strand transcription termination signal?
Extending coverage beyond ~10% of predicted human RBPs and beyond two cell lines, and large-scale functional validation of these RNA elements via CRISPR.

wiki/sources/vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-spl.title: vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous
wiki/sources/vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-spl.content:
RNA splicing analysis using heterogeneous and large RNA-seq datasets
One-line: Introduces the majiq-v2 software suite, a set of algorithms and visualization tools for detecting, quantifying, and visualizing local splicing variations from large, heterogeneous RNA-seq datasets (thousands of non-replicate samples), benchmarked against existing methods and applied to GTEx v8 brain subregions.
Key takeaways

Existing local-event splicing tools (original MAJIQ, rmats, LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet) are ill-suited to large, heterogeneous datasets: the assumption of a shared per-group PSI is violated even with few non-replicate samples, methods don’t scale or support incremental/multi-group analysis, and de novo splice complexity is hard to represent.
MAJIQ v2 contributes: (1) MAJIQ HET, a non-parametric per-sample differential-splicing test (TNOM, InfoScore, or Mann–Whitney U) that quantifies PSI per sample rather than assuming a shared group value; (2) an incremental splicegraph builder that reads BAM coverage once into reusable SJ files; (3) a faster, more accurate intron-retention quantification algorithm; (4) a “simplifier” to prune lowly-used junctions/introns; (5) the VOILA Modulizer, which parses overlapping LSVs into single-entry/single-exit alternative splicing modules (ASMs) classified into 14 basic AS event types; (6) VOILA v2, a cross-platform server-capable visualization app.
Builds on the local splicing variation (LSV) formulation from the original 2016 MAJIQ, capturing complex (>2 junction) and de novo events plus intron retention; quantification is in percent-spliced-in (Ψ) and dPSI (ΔΨ) via a Bayesian posterior model.
Applied to 2,335 GTEx v8 RNA-seq samples (374 donors, 13 brain subregions); cerebellar tissues show distinct splicing patterns, and the regulatory analysis recovers known neuronal splicing regulators including ptbp1, RBFOX family, SRRM4/nSR100, SRSF11, and QKI.
MAJIQ is the only benchmarked method offering de novo intron-retention detection and quantification.

Notable claims / data

On a large synthetic dataset (~300+ samples mimicking GTEx cerebellum and skeletal muscle, quantified by RSEM): MAJIQ and MAJIQ HET maintained low FDR (~0.3%) with low FNR; rMATS, Whippet, SUPPA2 had FDR ~15–30% and high FNR (60%, 32%, 49% respectively). SUPPA2 reported >6000 differentially spliced genes (thousands more than others); Whippet reported ~727 (far fewer).
MAJIQ and LeafCutter both reach MCC 0.97–0.99, but MAJIQ reports 34% more changing genes (2337 vs 1739) and 6% more non-changing (7110 vs 6713); at the event level the gap widens (4267 vs 2169 changing events) due to finer LSV-based event resolution.
Upset/unique-gene analysis (10 vs 10): SUPPA2’s 1713 unique genes have >25% FPR; rMATS and Whippet unique sets have 47% and 16% FPR; both MAJIQ unique sets have ~0 FPR; LeafCutter unique set 9.5% FPR.
On real GTEx data, MAJIQ HET improves reproducibility-ratio (RR) detection power over MAJIQ dPSI at equal reproducibility; intra-to-inter ratio (IIR, a real-data FDR proxy) was 2–7% for MAJIQ/HET/LeafCutter vs 13% (rMATS), 26% (SUPPA2), 23.7% (Whippet) on small groups.
RT-PCR PSI correlation (gold standard, >100 mouse experiments): MAJIQ, Whippet, rMATS ~0.97–0.98; LeafCutter 0.915, SUPPA 0.869.
Builder step is ~6.6x slower (wall-time) than quantification, motivating the one-time builder + reusable coverage design; all methods run in 0.5–4 GB memory.
GTEx brain analysis with E[Ψ] ≥ 5% simplification threshold defined 32,435 AS modules; 70.6% contain ≥1 unannotated junction/intron; 59.2% are binary events, ~40% contain multiple AS events; non-classical event types make up 13.1% of modules / 11.6% of events. Intron retention was the most common event type, consistent with high neuronal IR.
Cerebellar vs other-brain comparison: 3995 unique changing AS modules, >7500 changing AS events; intron retention most prevalent, then cassette exons.
QKI mechanism: cerebellar cassette-exon events 5.6x more likely to show QKI regulation than non-changing events (binomial p < 2.8e-31; ENCODE HepG2 QKI shRNA knockdown); decreased cerebellar QKI expression proposed to drive both inclusion (loss of upstream repression) and exclusion (loss of downstream enhancement).
Caveat: the cerebellar regulatory analysis is from bulk GTEx tissue and may be confounded by cell-type composition (cerebellum has higher neuronal fraction); validation tools aim only at a “bronze” reproducibility level.

Connections

A splicing-methods / percent-spliced-in tool paper; direct successor/competitor framing against rmats (rMATS turbo), LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet, and the original MAJIQ.
Mechanistic findings link to splicing-factor biology: ptbp1 regulation during neurogenesis, plus RBFOX, SRRM4, SRSF11, and QKI as cerebellar regulators — sibling to RBP-mechanism splicing sources in the wiki.
Heavy use of intron retention quantification complements other splicing-quantification entities; the Modulizer’s AS event typology parallels event definitions in MISO and rMATS.
Companion resources released: the largest synthetic RNA-seq splicing benchmark to date (RSEM-based) and the validationstools comparison package.

Open questions

How much does bulk-tissue cell-type heterogeneity confound the cerebellar regulatory signatures, and would single-cell or deconvolution change the QKI/RBFOX/SRRM4 conclusions?
Can long-read integration extend MAJIQ’s local-event quantification toward full isoform-level deconvolution?
How well does MAJIQ HET translate to clinical RNA diagnostics and to improved sQTL analysis, both flagged as future directions?
Are the novel non-classical AS module subtypes (putative alternative first/last exons, putative alt 3’/5’ from simplification) biologically validated beyond pattern-matching heuristics?

wiki/sources/velmeshev-2023-single-cell-.title: velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal
wiki/sources/velmeshev-2023-single-cell-.content:
Single-cell analysis of prenatal and postnatal human cortical development
One-line: A single-nucleus RNA + chromatin atlas of >700,000 nuclei spanning second-trimester gestation through adulthood that charts lineage-specific developmental gene programs of human cortical cell types and pinpoints the cell types, developmental stages, and (notably female-biased) gene programs most enriched for autism genetic risk. Data are browsable via the human-cortical-development-cell-browser.
Key takeaways

Builds a comprehensive transcriptomic atlas of human cortical development across prenatal and postnatal life, deliberately covering the third trimester, birth, and early postnatal stages that prior single-cell work (largely confined to the second trimester) under-sampled — a functional-single-cell-genomics resource.
Single-cell trajectory analysis reconstructs developmental programs for subtypes of excitatory neurons, interneurons, glia (astrocytes, OPCs, oligodendrocytes, microglia), and brain vasculature, with pseudotime tracking real developmental age.
Integrating snRNA-seq with single-nucleus open-chromatin (snATAC-seq) data defines enhancer gene regulatory networks (eGRNs) and candidate transcription factors driving lineage commitment.
Intersecting lineage- and sex-specific programs with disease risk-gene sets localizes genetic vulnerability: ASD/SCZ/BPD risk genes are enriched in second-trimester programs and in deep-layer excitatory neurons; AD risk genes are enriched in microglia.
A central finding: lineage-specific genes up-regulated in female cells are especially enriched for autism risk genes, supporting an ASD “female protective effect” and offering a mechanistic account of the male bias in autism.
Methodologically and biologically a close sibling of the Kriegstein-lab proteomic study wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (developing human cortex, single-cell resolution, autism-vulnerability focus); the two are complementary RNA/chromatin vs protein views of the same developmental system.

Notable claims / data

Generated snRNA-seq from 413,682 nuclei / 108 tissue samples / 60 neurotypical individuals; after debris removal 358,663 nuclei retained; after integrating published datasets the final atlas is 709,372 nuclei, 169 tissue samples, 106 donors (45 female, 61 male). Median 1,106 genes / 1,609 transcripts per nucleus.
Sampled prefrontal, cingulate, temporal, insular, and motor cortices plus the ganglionic eminences (interneuron source). 10x Genomics 3′ v2 capture; reads aligned to unspliced transcriptome to capture pre-mRNA; integration via Harmony, clustering/UMAP via Seurat, trajectories via Monocle 3 (cross-checked with Slingshot for microglia).
Identified 1,062 lineage-specific and 405 branch-specific genes. Neuronal lineages split into two maturation tempos: those maturing by end of second trimester (L5, L5-6-IT, all interneurons) vs protracted maturation through third trimester and after birth (L2-3, L4, L6) — evidence of a protracted human neuronal maturation timeline.
Expression dynamics dominated by transient vs burst patterns: broad-branch genes tend transient, mature-cell-type genes tend burst — suggesting gradual commitment via transient+burst transcriptional events. Putative regulators include MN1 (L2-3/L5-6-IT/L4), HS3ST4 (L5), noncoding CYP1B1-AS1 / LINC00507 (L2-3).
snATAC-seq: combined 284,907 profiles / 57 samples / 42 individuals from four datasets; SCENIC+ identified 51 TFs regulating 1,373 lineage-specific genes via 4,846 predicted regulatory regions. Recovered known regulators (SOX5, LHX6, OLIG2, SOX9) and newly implicated ones (BACH2 in L5; NFIX, ZNF184 → CUX2 in L2-3; MAFB in PV interneurons; FOXN2, RFX4 in glia).
Sex programs: 740 female-enriched vs 312 male-enriched dynamically expressed genes. Female genes → CNS development, cell adhesion, synaptic transmission; male genes → RNA metabolism / translation. >90% of sex-enriched genes peak in the second trimester (early, transient sex modulation). Sex enrichment not driven by sex-chromosome transcription (despite XIST, PCDH11Y among top hits).
Disease enrichment: ASD risk (SFARI score 2/3, and high-confidence ASD-HC by TADA) enriched in L5-6-IT and L5 neurons; female-specific developmental genes strongly enriched in both SFARI and ASD-HC lists, with subplate and L6 neurons prominent (e.g. NR4A2, MEF2C, NRXN2, PCDH15 up in female cells). Authors invoke this to explain the ~4:1 male:female ASD ratio via greater male susceptibility to haploinsufficiency.
Synaptic-pruning complement genes up-regulated in microglia, and glutamate-transport maturation in protoplasmic astrocytes, both peaking between birth and ~1 year — flagged as a critical postnatal synaptic-plasticity window.
Caveats stated: difficulty integrating snRNA-seq with snATAC-seq; microglia/vascular lineages omitted from chromatin analysis due to low ATAC profile counts; first-trimester and some intermediate stages not included; 3D/epigenetic imprinting questions left open.

Connections

A developing-human-brain single-cell resource that pairs directly with wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (same lab/system, proteomic layer) — both converge on autism vulnerability in the prenatal cortex from different molecular modalities.
The integrative snRNA + snATAC + eGRN design is a functional-single-cell-genomics multiomic approach conceptually adjacent to other single-cell regulatory-mapping efforts in the wiki such as chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.
Disease-risk enrichment via SFARI/GWAS gene sets parallels developing-brain functional-genomics efforts like li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.
Interactive atlas exposed through the human-cortical-development-cell-browser.

Open questions

Can earlier (first-trimester) and missing intermediate stages be integrated to complete the developmental trajectory, given scRNA-seq/snRNA-seq integration hurdles?
Are upper-layer (L2-3) neuron disturbances specific to idiopathic ASD, and driven by common vs rare variants?
Does genomic imprinting contribute to the observed sex enrichment of developmentally expressed genes (single-cell epigenetic profiling needed)?
Cell-type- and stage-specific vulnerability for BPD and SCZ remains preliminary and needs deeper genetic dissection.
Mechanistic test of whether higher baseline female expression of ASD risk genes confers resistance to haploinsufficiency.

wiki/sources/venkatasubramanian-2025-spl.title: venkatasubramanian-2025-splicing-regulatory-dynamics-for-precision
wiki/sources/venkatasubramanian-2025-spl.content:
Splicing regulatory dynamics for precision analysis and treatment of heterogeneous leukemias
One-line: Introduces oncosplice, an unsupervised computational workflow that defines cancer molecular subtypes from alternative-splicing profiles alone, and uses it to discover a prognostic “U2AF1-like” missplicing program in adult and pediatric AML that lacks splicing-factor mutations yet is druggable via PRMT5 inhibition acting through IRAK4.
Key takeaways

Splicing measurements alone classify AML genetic subtypes better than gene expression, motivating splicing-first, unsupervised subtype discovery (an application of splicing-and-disease).
oncosplice couples unsupervised splicing-subtype detection (splice-ICGS), supervised genetics/subtype identification (Bridger), and RBP regulatory prediction (RBP-Finder) on top of percent-spliced-in values from MultiPath-PSI / AltAnalyze.
In Leucegene adult AML it recovered 25 subtypes (15 known, 10 previously undefined) and defined >12 recurrent novel subtypes across TCGA, BEAT-AML, and pediatric TARGET cohorts.
Two opposing broad signatures — U2AF1-like and SRSF2-like — describe ~79-80% of all adult and pediatric AML patients and partially phenocopy u2af1-S34 and SRSF2-P95 mutant splicing, respectively, despite pediatric AML lacking splicing-factor mutations.
U2AF1-like is tumor-intrinsic (varies by patient not cell type in long-read scRNA-seq), stable through relapse, co-opts a healthy circadian splicing program, induces a leukemic stem cell program, and predicts poor survival.
The PRMT5 inhibitor PRT543 reverses U2AF1-like missplicing, suppresses the long IRAK4-L isoform, and promotes myeloid differentiation; genetic IRAK4 knockout blocks leukemia development in xenografts — pointing to a mutation-independent splicing-therapeutics axis (PRMT5 → IRAK4).

Notable claims / data

Splicing vs expression classification: splicing improved accuracy for splicing-factor-mutant subtypes (n=200, threefold CV, SVM one-vs-rest).
U2AF1-like and SRSF2-like together associate with ~88,000 events (66% of all detected splice events); not attributable to batch, depth, sex, or age. U2AF1-like incidence higher in bone marrow (17%) vs peripheral blood (LSC-suggestive).
U2AF1-like shifts isoforms toward full-length/domain-preserving products; SRSF2-like shifts toward truncation / nonsense-mediated decay (AltAnalyze protein-domain prediction).
Survival: U2AF1-like associated with poor overall survival in both TCGA (coxph P=0.01) and pediatric TARGET (P=0.04); SRSF2-like improved survival in TCGA (P=0.02) but not TARGET.
Mechanism is “promiscuous overactivation” of WT U2AF1, not altered specificity: U2AF1-like induced more atypical 3’ UAG splice sites than U2AF1-S34 (5193 vs 1920 impacted events) but bound predominantly conventional CAG sites like WT (HOMER motif analysis).
Of 287 poor-survival U2AF1-like genes shared in pediatric+adult AML, 42 were required for leukemic growth in >6 of 13 CRISPR AML cell lines (Wilcoxon P<0.005).
Long-read single-cell: PacBio Kinnex/MAS-seq on CD34+ BM from 3 AML patients; U2AF1-like vs SRSF2-like monocytic progenitors showed highly significant isoform-length increase (P<1e-100), confirming bulk predictions.
Stability: U2AF1-like/SRSF2-like retained diagnosis→relapse (Pearson rho=0.86, n=19; rho=0.69 across 41 BEAT-AML serial patients).
Circadian link: CYCLOPS ordering placed U2AF1-/SRSF2-like in distinct circadian phases; circadian regulators (BMAL/ARNTL, PER1, PER2, CRY2, NCOR1, PRMT5) implicated.
PRMT5i (PRT543) in MDSL/THP1 cells: dose-dependent reduction of IRAK4-L, decreased NF-κB activity, decreased viability, increased myeloid differentiation; IRAK4-KO blocks engraftment/leukemia in NSGS xenografts.
Caveat: SRSF2-like (unlike U2AF1-like) could not be modeled in vitro — all cultured cells/lines shift to U2AF1-like, implying extrinsic (stromal/cytokine) drivers of the splicing choice.

Connections

A splicing-subtype discovery resource for splicing-and-disease cancer genetics; the AML/MDS focus and u2af1 mutant phenocopy directly extend mutant-splicing-factor biology.
Therapeutic angle (PRMT5 → IRAK4 exon-4 inclusion) is a concrete splicing-therapeutics case where a non-mutational splicing program is the actionable target.
Methodologically it sits in the percent-spliced-in / splicing-methods family (MultiPath-PSI, AltAnalyze), but is an unsupervised subtype-discovery layer rather than a differential-splicing caller like rmats or leafcutter.
Long-read single-cell validation complements short-read rna-seq subtype calls.

Open questions

What extrinsic stromal/cytokine signals fix the U2AF1-like vs SRSF2-like splicing choice that collapses in vitro?
Is the circadian splicing program causal for the U2AF1-like state or a correlated phase marker?
Does PRMT5 inhibition translate to durable clinical benefit in U2AF1-like (mutation-negative) AML/MDS, and how to stratify patients prospectively?
How transferable is OncoSplice subtype discovery beyond AML to other heterogeneous cancers?

wiki/sources/von-bartheld-2016-search-fo.title: von-bartheld-2016-search-for-true-numbers
wiki/sources/von-bartheld-2016-search-fo.content:
The Search for True Numbers of Neurons and Glial Cells in the Human Brain: A Review of 150 Years of Cell Counting
One-line: A historical and methodological review showing that the long-taught “100 billion neurons / one trillion glia / 10:1 glia-neuron ratio” picture of the human brain was an unsupported myth, and that ~86 billion neurons with a glia-neuron ratio under 1:1 — first quantified rigorously by the isotropic fractionator — was actually consistent with primary data all along.
Key takeaways

For ~half a century the human brain was believed to hold ~100 billion neurons and ~1 trillion glial cells, a glia-neuron ratio (GNR) of 10:1. The newly validated isotropic fractionator method instead demonstrates a GNR of less than 1:1 and fewer than 100 billion glial cells.
The 10:1 (or 50:1) GNR was never backed by primary counts; histological data always supported a roughly 1:1 whole-brain ratio and 40-130 billion glia. The error persisted because three independently reported quantities — neuron counts, glia counts, and the assumed GNR — were mutually contradictory (since G/N = GNR), but no one noticed the arithmetic conflict, and authoritative textbooks presented the 10:1 figure as settled fact, bypassing peer scrutiny.
Cells in the human brain can be quantified three ways — total neurons, total glia, and the glia-neuron ratio — and these followed surprisingly separate historical trajectories that only superficially appeared to agree.
A key conceptual driver of the myth: the cerebral cortex (80-85% of brain volume) was treated as representative of the whole brain, but the cerebellum holds ~80% of all neurons (very small, densely packed granule cells), so cortex-based extrapolation badly inflated the apparent glia fraction.
The review reinforces that distinguishing small neurons (e.g. cerebellar granule cells) from glia is a long-standing, still-unresolved technical problem that underlies much of the historical confusion — relevant to anyone classifying neuronal-cell-types. It sits within the broader project of brain-development-neurogenomics.

Notable claims / data

Whole-brain totals (isotropic fractionator, Azevedo et al. 2009): ~86 billion neurons, ~85 billion non-neuronal cells → GNR < 1:1. Because those 85 billion non-neuronal cells include 20-25 billion endothelial cells, true glia are well below 85 billion.
Three counting-method families: (1) histology/stereology (model-based profile counting vs design-based stereology) — best for well-bordered regions, weak for large heterogeneous tissue; (2) DNA extraction — elegant but error-prone (incomplete recovery, mitochondrial DNA, up to 20% of adult human neurons are hyperploid); (3) homogenization / “direct enumeration” of nuclei in suspension (“brain soup”), whose refined modern form is the isotropic fractionator.
Isotropic fractionator (IF): introduced 2005 (Herculano-Houzel & Lent); fixes tissue, mechanically dissociates into an isotropic nuclear suspension, stains nuclei with DAPI, and distinguishes neuronal from non-neuronal nuclei via the anti-NeuN antibody; counts are independent of tissue volume/density and robust to heterogeneity. Limitations: doesn’t subtype non-neuronal cells, a few neuron populations are NeuN-negative, and only macroscopically dissectable regions can be analyzed. Validated side-by-side against stereology (Bahney & von Bartheld 2014; Miller et al. 2014).
Cerebral cortex (gray matter): majority of studies converge on 10-20 billion neurons; GNR ~1.5 (known since Mühlmann 1936, never seriously disputed); including white matter raises cortical GNR to ~3-4. Glia subtypes (mostly cortex): oligodendrocytes most frequent (45-75%), then astrocytes (19-40%), microglia ≤10% — refuting claims that microglia alone roughly equal neuron numbers.
Historical totals for whole brain: neurons estimated at 30 billion (Szentágothai 1983), 70-80 billion (Haug 1986), 85 billion (Williams & Herrup 1988); Haug (1986) already implied <50 billion glia and GNR <1. Three sequential challenges to the trillion-glia claim (Blinkov & Glezer 1968 → Haug 1986 → Azevedo et al. 2009) were ignored until the third finally triggered acceptance.

Connections

A foundational neurocounting reference for brain-development-neurogenomics; it grounds quantitative claims about composition of the human brain.
Directly informs how to think about neuronal-cell-types census work: the neuron-vs-glia discrimination problem and NeuN dependence are caveats for any cell-type cataloguing or atlas effort.
Methodologically a counterpoint within the wiki’s broader theme of measurement bias and data-quality pitfalls — here, an entire field’s “consensus” was an unverified citation cascade rather than a measurement error.

Open questions

Can the IF be extended to resolve glial subtypes (astrocyte / oligodendrocyte / microglia) and overcome NeuN-negative neuron populations?
How reliably can neurons be distinguished from glia in disease tissue, where the distinction directly affects neuropathological cell-loss claims (e.g. Alzheimer’s, aging)?
Given >2-fold biological variation in neocortical neuron number between individuals, what is the functional/cognitive significance of these counts?

wiki/sources/wagner-2025-aberrant-splici.title: wagner-2025-aberrant-splicing-prediction-during-human
wiki/sources/wagner-2025-aberrant-splici.content:
Aberrant splicing prediction during human organ development
One-line: Introduces absplice2, an updated tissue-specific aberrant-splicing-variant predictor that adds developmental SpliceMaps (embryogenesis→adult, 7 tissues) so that splice-disruptive variant effects can be scored across developmental stages — revealing a class of >18,000 brain variants whose splicing impact is confined to early development and clinically relevant to neurodevelopmental disorders (splicing-and-disease).
Key takeaways

Extends the AbSplice framework (contextualizing sequence-based predictions with empirical tissue-specific splice-site usage, “SpliceMaps”) rather than retraining a de novo splicing model. Three core improvements yield AbSplice2: (i) refined ground truth from the aberrant-splicing caller FRASER2 (intron-Jaccard metric) instead of FRASER; (ii) replacing spliceai’s single max delta score with the directional gain/loss scores of the Pangolin sequence model; (iii) a continuous (sigmoid) representation of splice-site usage replacing a binary threshold.
New capability: developmental SpliceMaps built from a published human organ-development RNA-seq series (Mazin et al.) covering 7 tissues (brain, cerebellum, heart, liver, testis, ovary, kidney) × 15 timepoints (4 weeks post conception → adult), enabling variant scoring across life stages. Variants near developmentally regulated alternative splice sites (devAS) get time-varying effect predictions while the underlying deep-learning scores stay constant.
Genome-wide scoring of all SNVs around 47,769 devAS sites finds a class of “purely developmental” variants — high impact early, no effect in adult. The brain has the most (N=18,206; 10% of high-impact brain variants around devAS sites). These are enriched in LoF-intolerant genes and neurodevelopmental disorder (NDD) genes.
Clinical validation in rare-disease cohorts: in Solve-RD, high-impact brain/cerebellum predictions are enriched in NDD-affected individuals within NDD genes; predicted developmental timing of disruption correlates with clinical age of onset. In Genomics England (NGRL), 26 unique variants in disease-linked genes had stronger predicted developmental than adult effects, including a candidate new diagnosis in FGFR1.
Deliverables: genome-wide precomputed AbSplice2 and Pangolin scores for all protein-coding SNVs (Zenodo), plus a web interface scoring SNVs/indels across 49 GTEx tissues and 15 developmental timepoints in 7 tissues.

Notable claims / data

Ground truth: switching FRASER→FRASER2 outliers gave a ~2-fold recall increase for all models (FRASER2 reduces false-positive outliers ~10×), at some precision cost among top predictions. The improvement was independent of retraining — feature contributions were nearly identical — evidence the framework is robust to imperfect training labels.
Directional SpliceAI scores (acceptor/donor gain/loss instead of max delta) improved average precision ~12% (P=3.3×10⁻⁶); replacing SpliceAI with Pangolin’s gain/loss scores added a further ~9% (P=1.3×10⁻⁸).
Continuous splice-site usage (vs binary threshold) improved average precision ~41% (P=1.8×10⁻¹⁵), mostly by re-ranking false positives near splice sites of intermediate usage. Overall AbSplice2 ≈ 2-fold precision improvement over AbSplice1.
AbSplice2-RNA (incorporating RNA-seq from clinically accessible tissues) improved average precision ~21% over the best DNA-only model. Replacing AbSplice1 with AbSplice2 as input to the underexpression predictor AbExp improved AP ~7% (P=9.3×10⁻⁴).
Independent benchmark: 230 BrainGVEx brain frontal cortex samples (paired WGS+RNA-seq) reproduced the relative model ranking (AbSplice2 best), though max recall was lower than GTEx, possibly due to sequencing quality.
Cutoffs: AbSplice2 high=0.2, low=0.05. “Purely developmental” = >0.2 at an early stage AND <0.05 in adult GTEx; “purely adult” = the reverse.
LoF intolerance: genes in the most LoF-intolerant decile are 6.8× more likely to harbor a development-specific variant than the most LoF-tolerant decile (Fisher P=6.7×10⁻⁶⁵).
Cohorts: GTEx (946 individuals, n=16,213 RNA-seq); Solve-RD (24,560 individuals: 15,028 affected / 9,532 unaffected; 4,780 NDD); NGRL Genomics England (64,736 rare-disease participants: 39,915 100k + 24,821 GMS).
Lead candidate: FGFR1 c.449-6G>A (chr8:38428099 C>T) creates an AG in the AG-exclusion zone, activating a weak acceptor only in early forebrain development (wpc 4–6) and kidney, not after birth — consistent with the Kallmann-syndrome / hypogonadotropic-hypogonadism mechanism. Confirmed positive control: ARFGEF1 c.4960-1G>A in a solved intellectual-disability case.
Worked example SSBP3 exon 6: brain-included at 4 wpc, removed by birth; nearby SNVs scored high early, low in adult — Pangolin/MMSplice scores are stage-invariant while AbSplice2 tracks the SpliceMap reference-PSI change.

Connections

Builds directly on the deep-learning splice-site model spliceai (Pangolin shares SpliceAI’s architecture/context window but adds quantitative RNA-seq usage across 4 tissues / 4 mammals) — this paper swaps SpliceAI for Pangolin inside the AbSplice integration.
Squarely in splicing-and-disease: a variant-interpretation tool for rare-disease diagnostics, with a strong brain/neurodevelopment emphasis.
Sibling to brain/disease sources here: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (which likewise leans on SpliceAI/Pangolin delta scores for variant interpretation), zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain tissue-specificity of splicing regulation), and scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (mis-splicing as a disease mechanism).
Relates to developmentally regulated alternative splicing as a theme — cf. kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated — here operationalized for variant scoring via developmental SpliceMaps.

Open questions

Larger developmental cohorts (e.g. upcoming developmental GTEx) would yield more robust SpliceMaps and capture weakly spliced junctions present in few individuals; the modular framework needs only new SpliceMaps, no retraining.
The framework is bounded by the underlying sequence model: tissue-specific splice sites underrepresented in annotation-trained models (e.g. SpliceAI) cannot be recovered; foundation models + broader training data may help.
Many NGRL candidates are heterozygous in biallelic-mechanism genes — predicted splicing disruptions may be real but not the primary phenotype driver; experimental validation of developmental-stage-specific splicing effects is still needed.
Using the max score across tissues is explicitly NOT the intended use (lower precision); interpretation depends on having a SpliceMap for the relevant tissue/stage.

wiki/sources/waldron-2016-splicing-linki.title: waldron-2016-splicing-linking-genetic-variation
wiki/sources/waldron-2016-splicing-linki.content:
Splicing: linking genetic variation and disease
One-line: A short Research Highlight summarizing a Science study (Li et al.) showing that RNA splicing is a primary link between common genetic variation and complex disease.
Key takeaways

Noncoding variants contribute to complex traits, but their mechanisms are unclear; the highlighted study maps how variants act across the full chain of splicing and gene regulation, from chromatin to protein.
In human lymphoblastoid cell lines (LCLs), the authors quantified eight molecular phenotypes (chromatin modifications, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, mRNA levels, transcription rate, RNA decay rate, translation levels, protein levels) and mapped QTLs for each.
Splicing QTLs (splicing-qtl) emerge as a major, partly chromatin-independent route by which variants drive complex traits and splicing-and-disease.

Notable claims / data

65% of eQTLs exert their primary effect on expression at the chromatin level; the remaining 35% are enriched in transcribed regions, acting on transcriptional/post-transcriptional processes in a chromatin-independent manner.
The novel tool LeafCutter identified 2,893 sQTLs; most had no effect on overall expression, but 89% directly affected predicted protein sequences.
sQTLs were modestly enriched for chromatin-level phenotypes, giving direct evidence that splicing can be altered at the chromatin level.
In GWAS data (rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer disease, schizophrenia, height, BMI), sQTLs were enriched comparably to eQTLs — and even more strongly for multiple sclerosis.

Connections

Concentrates the disease-genetics angle of splicing-and-disease via the lens of splicing-qtl.
Complements the sQTL/genetic-control work in walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and.
The original Science article introduces the LeafCutter tool (see entity leafcutter).

Open questions

How generalizable are LCL-derived sQTLs to disease-relevant tissues such as brain or immune cells?
What mechanisms link chromatin-level changes to altered splicing outcomes?

wiki/sources/walker-2019-genetic-control.title: walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and
wiki/sources/walker-2019-genetic-control.content:
Genetic Control of Expression and Splicing in Developing Human Brain Informs Disease Mechanisms
One-line: The first genome-wide map of splice QTLs (and the largest eQTL map) in mid-gestational human cortex (201 brains), showing that prenatal-specific regulatory variation — splicing as much as expression — carries substantial neuropsychiatric disease risk, and integrating it with GWAS via TWAS to nominate dozens of novel candidate genes.
Key takeaways

A well-powered splicing-qtl/eQTL study of 201 mid-gestational human brains identifies 7,962 eQTLs (6,546 eGenes) and 4,635 sQTLs (2,132 sGenes), including several thousand prenatal-specific regulatory regions absent from adult brain datasets.
sQTLs and eQTLs are mostly independent regulatory layers: half of sGenes are also eGenes and 67% of sQTLs also affect total expression, but only 22% of eQTLs affect splice-junction usage, and the eSNP/sSNP for a shared gene are typically far apart — consistent with splicing being a distinct axis of genetic control (allele-specific-splicing).
Variation in splicing carries as much, if not more, disease risk than expression: sQTL regions are enriched for SCZ, educational-attainment, and (trending) ASD/ADHD GWAS signal, with ASD GWAS only trending in sQTLs (not eQTLs) — extending the link between splicing-and-disease into human brain development.
Genetic control of expression and splicing is strongly developmental-stage-dependent: prenatal eQTLs carry significant SCZ SNP-heritability while adult-brain eQTLs (GTEx, PsychENCODE) do not reach significance, and prenatal+adult annotations explain disease risk additively — arguing these epochs capture distinct, complementary risk.
TWAS integrating prenatal QTLs with GWAS nominates dozens of new candidate risk genes for schizophrenia (62 genes + 91 introns) and intracranial volume, several invisible to adult-brain TWAS (e.g., INO80E, TMEM219, NT5DC2, GLN3/PBRM1 splicing at SCZ loci).
The data are released as a browsable atlas via the depict-viewer web portal (gene/SNP summary-level eQTL and sQTL), making this a reusable developmental-brain QTL resource.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 233 prenatal cortical RNA-seq samples QC’d to 201 individuals; 15,925 genes and 6.6M SNPs. Ancestry: 40% Mexican, 25% African-American, 14% European-Mexican, 8% ≥3-ancestry admixed, 6% Chinese, 5% African-American-Mexican, 2% European.
eQTLs: cis-eQTLs via FastQTL; 6,546 eGenes (FDR < 0.05) + 1,416 secondary eQTLs = 7,962. Reproducible: 94% of eGenes also found by EMMAX, 84% in subpopulation meta-analysis. >20% of eQTLs within 10 kb of TSS; 70% within 100 kb; enriched in prenatal open chromatin (OR = 4.42) and distal eQTLs in Hi-C 3D contacts (OR = 2.96). eSNPs enriched in binding sites for 39 TFs/DBPs (ELK4, NRF1, SMARCC1/2, CHD8). eGenes more loss-of-function tolerant.
sQTLs: annotation-free intron-excision clustering (LeafCutter approach; see leafcutter) found 92,449 excision clusters; 4,635 sQTLs (5% FDR) in 2,132 genes — 71% annotated introns, 27% novel cryptic introns. 42% of sSNPs within 10 kb of the splice junction; 64% lie within the gene body. sSNPs enriched in binding sites for 36 RBPs including HNRNPH, ATXN2, SRRM4. Proof-of-principle: TRMT1 (intellectual disability) sSNP falls in an SRRM4 CLIP-seq peak and SRRM4 overexpression alters TRMT1 intron splicing.
Stage/tissue specificity: prenatal-specific eQTLs have larger effect sizes (p < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶) and lower LoF tolerance (pLI) than tissue-shared eQTLs. Only ~1/3 of eGenes overlap GTEx adult cortex; >1,000 eGenes prenatal-specific vs PsychENCODE (which is ~10× larger). Only 24% of prenatal sGenes are adult sGenes.
SCZ SNP-heritability (LDSR): prenatal eQTLs 3.7% (p = 9.1×10⁻⁴) and prenatal sQTLs 2.1% (p = 6.6×10⁻³) — both significant, both higher than adult eQTLs (non-significant). Adult sQTLs do contribute (2.3%); prenatal+adult sQTLs additive to 3.5%. Inflammatory bowel disease used as negative control (no enrichment).
WGCNA: 19 co-expression modules; SCZ GWAS enriched in the “blue” (neurogenesis: DLX1, FGF2, LHX6) module and ASD trending in “yellow” (gene regulation in developing neurons; upper-layer cortical projection neurons: MEF2, HNRNPH3, FOXP4). Common and rare variation converge within modules per disorder; ASD implicates superficial cortical neurons. SCZ and ASD affect distinct developmental modules.
SCZ TWAS: 62 genes + 91 introns (P_Bonferroni < 0.05); 8 genes + 6 introns also pass SMR+HEIDI. 23 new SCZ risk loci not GWAS-significant (5 prenatal-unique). Highlighted: INO80E and TMEM219 at 16p11.2; NT5DC2 (expression), GLN3 and PBRM1 (splicing) at a chr3 SCZ locus; AP3B2, MPHOSPH9 splicing shared with adult. FOCUS fine-mapping: credible set of 155 genes (~3/locus).
ICV TWAS: 7 genes + 8 introns; LRRC37A prioritized at the 17q21 inversion locus; NT5C2/USMG5 splicing at chr10.
Caveats stated: cross-stage sQTL comparison is imperfect because splicing-detection methods differ between studies; prenatal-specific QTL counts are likely underestimates given the ~10× smaller sample vs adult datasets; bulk tissue; TWAS is sensitive to tissue source and fine-mapping shows many loci need experimental dissection (e.g., 16p11.2 CNV contains 29 genes).

Connections

A developmental-stage counterpart to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (GTEx brain-region sQTLs): both establish splicing-qtl as a major disease-relevant layer, but Walker maps the prenatal epoch and ties sQTLs to SCZ/ASD/ICV via TWAS, whereas Zhang dissects regional ubiquity vs specificity in adult brain.
The annotation-free intron-excision quantification is the leafcutter strategy (Li et al. 2018), quantifying splicing as intron usage rather than percent-spliced-in of predefined exons.
sSNP/RBP enrichment (HNRNPH, SRRM4, ATXN2) connects to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins; the SRRM4/microexon thread relates broadly to neuronal splicing programs and aligns with disease-splicing reviews like scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Mechanistically situates squarely in splicing-and-disease: prenatal sQTLs carry SCZ/ADHD heritability and ASD GWAS trends, arguing mis-regulated splicing in early corticogenesis contributes to neuropsychiatric risk.
